name: Unknown Threat Actors Conducted Ransomware Attack On Wriezen Town Administration In Germany On 17 July 2026
description: Unknown threat actors targeted the town administration of Wriezen, Germany, on 17 July 2026. The attack compromised the administration's ability to work. Mayor Karsten Ilm confirmed the incident. The affected IT systems were taken offline as a precautionary measure to protect the systems and limit further damage.

name: Unattributed Hacker Defaced Kenyan President's Website On 18 July 2026
description: Unattributed hacker defaced the official website of the Kenyan presidency, replacing the homepage with a message targeting President William Ruto and demanding a ransom of five bitcoins on 18 July 2026. The attackers threatened to disclose unspecified information if the ransom was not paid. Kenyan Minister of Information, Communication and Digital Economy, William Kabogo, confirmed the cyberattack, stating that government cybersecurity teams were mobilised to respond to the incident. The website's functionality was fully restored on 19 July 2026
inclusion_criteria: Attack on (inter alia) political target(s), politicized

name: Unknown Threat Actors Conducted Ransomware Attack On Petroleum Company Ecopetrol In Colombia On 17 July 2026
description: Unknown threat actors stole and threatened to publish data stolen from Colombian Ecopetrol’s information systems on 17 July 2026. The attack affected cloud file storage environments of approximately 15 companies in the Ecopetrol Business Group, with around 3,300 user accounts affected. The attacker's attempt to hijack the company's IT systems was blocked by cybersecurity controls. The external actor made extortion demands, threatening to publicly disclose the illegally extracted information. In response, Ecopetrol activated its incident response and management protocols, revoked unauthorised access, blocked mechanisms associated with mass downloads of information, filed a complaint with the Attorney General’s Office, and deployed cooperation activities with national specialised entities.

After detecting the incident, Fairlife temporarily suspended production operations in the United States.

name: Unknown Threat Actor Stole £18 Million From Defi Exchange Ostium on 15 July 2026
description: An unknown threat actor stole £18 million from DeFi exchange Ostium via an exploit, by using a registered PriceUpKeepforwarder and predated authorised Oracle reports to generate artificial trading profits on 15 July 2026. The incident was first reported by the blockchain security firm Blockaid on X. Some of the stolen USDC coins were exchanged for Ethereum via the Kyber Network. The attack occurred shortly after Ostium's partnership with Nasdaq was announced.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Threat Actors Infiltrated Paraguayan Government Networks in 2026
description: Chinese state-sponsored threat actors infiltrated Paraguayan government networks in 2026. The Paraguayan Ministry of Technology and Information (MITIC) announced on 10 July 2026 that in a joint cybersecurity review with the United States multiple threat actors were identified in Paraguayan government networks, which are linked to the Chinese government. A MITIC spokesman explained that while they continue to make the diagnosis of the impact, it can confirm that an exfiltration of data occurred.

name: Ransomware Group World Leaks Breached Largest Nuclear Power Plant in India on 29 May 2026
description: The ransomware group World Leaks breached the systems of Reliance Infrastructure, a contractor for the Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant, by exploiting a server hosted by third-party data centre provider Yotta on 29 May 2026, and leaked around 19,000 files, totalling nearly 14.3 GB, onto the dark web, including engineering drawings, vendor details, inspection records, and other project-related files, which were reportedly accessible online since 11 June 2026. The breach was reported by independent cybersecurity researcher Rakesh Krishnan, and the Indian Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT-In) is investigating the incident alongside the Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL) and Reliance Infrastructure. Yotta said in a statement it had noted suspicious activity on May 29 on a server it hosts that belongs to Reliance Infrastructure. World Leaks claimed responsibility for the breach, and the leaked information is believed to have originated from Reliance Infrastructure's systems. The Nuclear Power Corporation, which commissions and operates the country's nuclear power plants, said in a statement to Reuters that the ‌information said to ⁠be available in the public domain pertains only to common service facilities and does not relate to any nuclear safety or nuclear security-related systems.

name: Unattributed Hackers Disrupted Loyalist College in Canada on 9 July 2026
description: Unattributed hackers disrupted the Loyalist College in Ontario, Canada, on 9 July 2026. The attack impacted various systems and services, including email, on-campus internet access, myLoyalist, Canvas, and college systems that support student registration and tuition and fee payments. The college confirmed the incident with a post on their website and notified provincial authorities and alerted its community through a website announcement. Online classes were suspended until further notice, but in-person classes continued, and the campus remained open.

name: Unidentified Perpetrators Breached Network Operators Netze BW and Stuttgart Netze in Germany in July 2026
description: An unidentified perpetrator breached two network operators, Netze BW and Stuttgart Netze in Germany in July 2026, affecting thousands of households. The attackers gained access to customer data records through a vulnerability in the systems of a third-party provider, which had taken over the installation of modern electricity meters in Baden-Württemberg households on behalf of the network operators. The compromised data included full names, addresses, email addresses, phone numbers and serial numbers of the installed meters, as well as recordings of the metering units. The attack was reported by the German regional public broadcaster SWR.

name: Unknown Threat Actor Disrupted Japanese Frozen Food Supplier Nichirei on 15 July 2026
description: An unknown threat actor disrupted the operations of subsidiaries of Nichirei, Japan's top frozen food supplier, on 15 July 2026, causing system failures through unauthorised access. The disruption affected deliveries to clients including Kentucky Fried Chicken Japan and Kura Sushi Inc, a popular rotary sushi chain, prompting both businesses to warn of possible shortages and store closures. Supermarkets, pastry chefs, and elderly care centres were also affected by resulting shortages, according to local media. Nichirei disclosed the incident in a statement on 15 July 2026 and announced it was investigating and taking measures to restore its systems, with plans to gradually resume disrupted operations from 19 July 2026.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Stole Data of 40,000 Customers from Energy Company IWB in Switzerland on 15 July 2026
description: Unknown threat actors breached an external service provider of Industrielle Werke Basel (IWB), the electricity and drinking water utility for the canton of Basel City, Switzerland, stealing data belonging to around 40,000 customers on or around 15 July 2026. The stolen data included names, addresses, and technical information related to energy meters but did not include telephone numbers, email addresses or bank details. The third-party provider promptly informed IWB, which restricted data exchange and initiated security checks, whilst the company's internal systems remained unaffected. IWB Board Chairman Claus Schmidt confirmed the incident and announced additional controls.

description: Members of Unit 61240 of the 16th Centre of the Russian Federal Security Service (FSB) (aka TURLA/Venomous Bear/Uroburos) exfiltrated a significant amount of data from a research institute specialised in sensitive technologies and working for the French defence industry in February 2025. Using custom malware tracked as TURLA, the FSB gained unauthorised access to the institute's network and exfiltrated the data. The incident was disclosed in a joint attribution statement by the EU and France on 13 July 2026.

The attackers stole personal data, including name, date of birth, address, contact details, insurance information, and sensitive medical records like pathology and diagnostic results, consultation notes, and referral letters.

name: Unidentified Hackers Hit German Public Transport Company Nah SH With DDoS Attack on 13 July 2026
description: Unidentified hackers carried out a DDoS attack on the website of German public transport company Nah SH, disrupting access from around 10am on 13 July 2026. The attack made www.nah.sh intermittently unavailable, disabling the online information service, whilst the Nah SH app and shop remained unaffected. Spokesperson Dennis Fiedel confirmed appropriate measures had been taken, though the source could not be identified and the disruption was ongoing at the time of reporting.

name: Unattributed Hackers Breached AssuranceAmerica In United States On 16 March 2026
description: Unattributed hackers breached US auto insurer AssuranceAmerica, stealing the personal information of up to 6.9 million people, after a targeted phishing attack compromised a single employee's credentials on 16 March 2026. The threat actor accessed parts of the insurer's IT systems and copied files containing customers' names, contact information, driver's license numbers, auto insurance policies and accounts, driver and vehicle details, and accident claims information, with some customers' Social Security numbers and tax identification numbers also compromised. AssuranceAmerica discovered the breach on 17 March 2026 and disclosed the incident publicly, sending notification letters to affected customers on 10 July 2026 following completion of its file review on 15 June 2026.

name: Unidentified Perpetrators Carried Out DDoS Attacks On Internet Pages Of Leipzig Group In Germany In July 2026
description: Unidentified perpetrators carried out DDoS attacks against several internet pages of the Leipzig Group in Germany on 12 July 2026, overloading servers with a high volume of requests. The attack affected the websites of the Leipzig Group, Leipziger Stadtwerke, Leipziger Verkehrsbetriebe (LVB) and Leipziger Wasserwerke. The "Leipzig Move" timetable app continued to function without restriction. Technicians restored the websites on the same day. The Leipzig Group stated that no data was accessed during the incident.

The threat actor stole pharmacist names, pharmacy business registration numbers, pharmacy phone numbers and email addresses, whilst the company's POS and payment services continued operating normally. name: UNC1151 aka Ghostwriter Conducted Phishing Campaign Targeting Gmail Accounts of Polish Citizens Since March 2026
description: UNC1151 aka Ghostwriter conducted a sustained phishing campaign targeting Gmail accounts of Polish citizens, with CERT Polska observing high-intensity activity since March 2026. The threat actor sent fraudulent emails in Polish imitating official Gmail administrator communications, directing recipients to fake login pages capable of harvesting both passwords and two-factor authentication codes (SMS-based and application-generated). Targets included individuals in political and public life, researchers, journalists, and public administration and law enforcement employees, alongside their family and social contacts. The group used dedicated phishing domains (registered under TLDs including .icu, .digital, and .top) as well as subdomains hosted on the Netlify platform and in some cases compromised Polish organisations' websites to host fake login panels. UNC1151/Ghostwriter has been linked to Belarus by Mandiant and other cybersecurity researchers in previous reporting

name: Iran-Linked Threat Actor Homeland Justice Claimed Breach of Albanian Post and Leaked Internal Staff Emails in March 2026
description: Homeland Justice, an Iran-linked threat actor, claimed to have breached Albanian Post's systems and published leaked internal staff emails on Telegram in March 2026. The attackers framed the action as retaliation against the postal service's alleged support for the Mujahedin-e-Khalq's presence in Albania. The leaked emails contained data on postal deliveries to individuals along with other internal information. Albania's Minister for Local Governance, Ervin Demo, stated that postal services continued to operate normally and that no evidence had emerged of systems or data having been compromised or encrypted, pending a detailed analysis by the National Agency for Information Society. The incident followed a claimed intrusion by Homeland Justice against the Albanian parliament's digital infrastructure earlier the same month.

name: Hacker "Kurdish sr" Defaced US Army Subdomains on 6 July 2026
description: Hacker "Kurdish sr" defaced multiple US Army subdomains on 6 July 2026 in a 404 hijacking campaign. The affected subdomains oil.army.mil (Army's Open Innovation Lab) and ai2c.army.mil (Artificial Intelligence Integration Center) displayed pro-Kurdish sentiments and insults directed at President Donald Trump and US Ambassador to Türkiye Tom Barrack. The defacement messages called to "FREE KURDISTAN" and included "Kurdish sr was here." The attackers exploited the websites' error-handling system to insert unauthorised content on error pages while the rest of the sites appeared untouched. The affected sites run on WordPress and Microsoft cloud infrastructure. Independent cybersecurity researcher Ronald Lovelace discovered the defacements and notified US Army officials. The Army took immediate action to secure the affected pages and investigate the incident. CyberScoop reported the incident on 6 July 2026.

The attackers exploited the protocol's Lazy Summer contracts, specifically the LazyVaultLowerRiskUSDC pool, by using a flash loan to distort the internal liquidity and then withdrawing user funds. The attackers' address, exploit smart contract, and affected contracts were published by security company Blockaid. The incident was reported by Kamina Bashir on Yahoo finance on 06 July 2026.

name: Unattributed Hackers Deployed Ransomware Against University of Alicante In Spain On 02 July 2026
description: Unattributed hackers deployed ransomware against the University of Alicante in Spain on 2 July 2026 in what the university described as the largest cyberattack the institution has suffered to date. The university's monitoring systems detected suspicious activity and alerted the Information Technology Security Division, which immediately activated preventive measures and deactivated certain services to stop the spread. The attack was limited to a few secondary servers. As a precaution, the university shut down UACloud (the intranet), remote access for workers and electronic administration, resulting in the suspension of legal deadlines for procedures. Essential services were restored within 24 hours. The university confirmed there was no data breach and that personal, economic and academic data remained safe. Diario de Alicante reported the incident on 6 July 2026.

name: Unknown Russian Threat Actor Launched DDoS Attack on Ukrainian Television Channel Website in 2025
description: An unknown Russian threat actor launched a DDoS attack on the website of an unidentified Ukrainian television channel in 2025. The attack lasted three hours, with the attacker using a botnet of compromised devices in Asia, Europe and the United States to launch up to 200,000 requests per minute. name: Unknown Threat Actor Targeted Government and Diplomatic Entities Worldwide with SharkLoader Malware in 2026
description: An unknown threat actor targeted several organisations worldwide with undocumented malware in an ongoing campaign discovered by Kaspersky in 2026. Targets included diplomatic entities in Indonesia, government agencies in Taiwan, software development companies, and businesses in Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal and Serbia. The attackers used a previously undocumented malware loader called SharkLoader to infiltrate targeted systems, exploiting known vulnerabilities in internet-exposed applications including Microsoft Exchange, Microsoft SharePoint and Openfire servers. They also distributed malicious droppers masquerading as legitimate software such as Google Update and Cisco AnyConnect installers, and used PDF documents in phishing campaigns. The malware employed advanced evasion techniques including DLL sideloading to load encrypted malicious modules, with the final stage injecting and executing Cobalt Strike Beacon for command and control. Kaspersky does not attribute the activity to any known APT group and published its findings on 24 June 2026.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Brazil's National Emergency Alert System on 19-20 June 2026
description: Unknown threat actors compromised Brazil's national Civil Defence alert system between 19 June and 20 June 2026, sending false emergency messages to approximately 30 million people across eight states and the Federal District. The attack targeted the Civil Defence Alert system operated by the Ministry of Integration and Regional Development. The National Secretary for Civil Protection and Defence Wolnei Wolff confirmed that ten different notifications were issued during the intrusion: nine via Cell Broadcast and one via SMS. The messages contained terms such as "misanthropy" and "alien invasion." The Federal Police and the National Telecommunications Agency (Anatel) launched investigations. Anatel reported that the alerts did not go through the official channels of the system's technical platform operated by ABR Telecom. The system was temporarily suspended following the incident.

name: Iran-Linked Handala Breached And Leaked Data Of Water Utility Provider California Water Service In United States On 11 June 2026
description: Iran-linked hacking group Handala (aka Handala Hack Team/ Hamsa) breached and leaked data of water utility provider California Water Service in the United States on 11 June 2026. The attacker published a 5GB proof-of-concept data dump to prove its access to the company's customer billing database and its RTKBase system. The customer billing database contained names, service addresses, phone numbers, account numbers, and payment histories across multiple Cal Water districts, and the internal RTKBase deployment is an open-source GNSS base station platform used by field crews to receive GPS data when mapping and maintaining water infrastructure. Dataminr researchers stated that the RTKBase platform was the entry point and the group merely used it to access the customer information database. Handala’s public release shows access to at least seven operational districts of California Water Service, including Bakersfield, Chico, Salinas, Stockton, Visalia, San Mateo and a regional engineering segment. The group stated that its attack was a response to US attacks on Iran. The breach was reported by Dataminr on 11 June 2026.

name: Hacktivist Group Nullsec Philippines Defaced Philippine Senate Website on 10 June 2026
description: The Filipino hacktivist group Nullsec Philippines defaced the website of the Philippine Senate on 10 June 2026. The hacktivist group identifying as “Nullsec Philippines” claimed responsibility for the defacing attack in a social media post, accusing the government of not doing enough against corruption. On 11 June, the Senate confirmed that its official website was recently subjected to an “unauthorized modification” or website defacement but said that initial assessment showed “no indication that confidential or sensitive information had been compromised.” The Senate President Pro Tempore and Acting Senate President Sherwin Gatchalian condemned the act and called for a unified government response to identify and prosecute those responsible. An investigation was launched by the National Bureau of Investigation (NBI), the Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT), and the Cybercrime Investigation and Coordinating Center (CICC).

name: Cyber Threat Actor Siribclone Stole Military Information From Russian Military Personnel In Ukraine and Russia Between Mid 2025 and May 2026
description:
A previously unknown cyber espionage group, dubbed SiribClone by Russian cybersecurity firm F6, stole information from Russian military personnel between Mid 2025 and May 2026. The group primarily targeted members of the Russian armed forces stationed in border regions and combat zones, with the campaign appearing to be aimed at gathering battlefield intelligence by stealing files, monitoring communications and collecting sensitive military information from Russian troops deployed near the front line. The hackers impersonated women seeking romantic relationships or volunteers offering humanitarian assistance to initiate conversations with servicemen on Telegram and other messaging platforms before persuading them to download malicious applications or enter their Telegram credentials on fake websites. Victims were tricked into clicking malicious links under various pretexts, including being asked to test a new application or exchange intimate photographs through a secure photosharing application, which installed previously undocumented Android spyware named SafeLoveStealer. The malware can steal photographs, videos, documents, location data and other information from infected devices while also allowing attackers to remotely activate the targets microphone and record conversations. 
In addition to mobile spyware, the group deployed previously undocumented malware for desktop computers, dubbed SiribGrabber, whose primary purpose is to steal files from infected systems. Researchers from F6 informed the public about the attack in a technical report on 4 June 2026.

name: Unknown Threat Actor Exploited Vulnerability in the Bridge of the Syscoin Blockchain in early June 2026
description: An unknown threat actor exploited a validation vulnerability in the bridge process of Syscoin (SYS) blockchain and generated approximately 5 billion unauthorized SYS on the networks UTXO blockchain in early June 2026. The attacker created the unauthorized tokens by exploiting the bridge relay path, which verifies transactions between two blockchains, and the system treated the fraudulent transaction as valid. The funds were initially sent to a single address and later distributed to various wallets. In an update posted on X Syscoin said that it paused its bridge and is working with exchanges to prevent the affected credits from entering open markets.

name: Russian State-Sponsored Group Gamaredon Launched Long-Term Espionage Campaign on Ukrainian Targets in January 2026
description: Russian state-sponsored group Gamaredon launched a long-term espionage campaign on Ukrainian targets by exploiting a WinRAR vulnerability to drop nearly fileless malware. The group used a weaponized XHTML file, likely delivered as a spearfishing attachment, to trigger a tracking request and deliver a RAR archive that exploited the CVE-2025-8088 vulnerability. The malware, which included components such as GammaPhish, GammaLoad, GammaWorm, GammaSteel, and GammaWipe, was designed to be highly evasive and persistent, using techniques such as HTML smuggling, path traversal, and NTFS Alternate Data Streams to hide its presence. The campaign was discovered by Sekoia’s Threat Detection Research team based on forensic artifacts recovered from compromised hosts, proactive threat hunting, and live payload acquisition, reconstructing a significant portion of an infection chain deployed in January 2026. The report was published on 1 June 2026.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Hijacked Multiple High-Profile Instagram Accounts Including US Space Force Lieutenant John Bentivegna and Former US President Barack Obama in United States in late May 2026
description:
Unknown threat actors hijacked multiple high-profile Instagram accounts in late May 2026, including the personal account of US Space Force Lieutenant John Bentivegna and former US President Barack Obama's inactive Instagram account from his time in the White House. US Space Force Lieutenant John Bentivegna was misused to share images of Vietnamese radio host 'Hanoi Hannah' and spread anti-American content and Iranian propaganda produced by artificial intelligence. From Obama's account shared images with a comment 'The White House is under the control of Shiites'. Journalists from 404 Media reported that hackers used Meta’s AI support chatbot to break into a host of high-profile Instagram profiles by asking the support bot to change the email address associated with the target account. The accounts were later recovered by American authorities.
On 5 June 2026, Meta revealed in a breach letter filed with Maine's Office of the Attorney General that 30 Instagram users had their accounts hijacked. The attacker exploited a flaw in the company's High Touch Support (HTS) tool, an AI-assisted support system that helps users regain access after being locked out of their Instagram accounts. In total, the accounts of 20,225 Instagram users are affected.

Kasiunas claimed that the leak bore the hallmarks of a Russian intelligence operation and that the residential address data of intelligence and military personnel could be used for surveillance, phishing campaigns, identity theft, or coercion attempts. The Lithuanian Prosecutor General's office did not confirm or deny the accusations of Russian involvement and did not reveal the foreign state allegedly linked to the attack.

name: Unknown Threat Actor Stole Tens of Thousands of Sensitive Patient Data from Service Provider in Germany in Mid-April 2026
description:
An unknown threat actor compromised the service provider Unimed based in Saarland that processes invoices for private medical services resulted in the theft of sensitive patient data in mid-April 2026, including names, dates of birth, addresses, and billing-relevant data, from approximately 61,000 patients at Ulm University Hospital and other clinics in Baden-Württemberg in May 2026, including university hospitals in Freiburg, Heidelberg, and Tübingen. The hospital management was informed of the data theft a few days prior to the publication date. The Ulm University Hospital made the cyberattack public in a press statement on 21 May 2026. The hospital has stopped transmitting data to the service provider and is demanding clarification on the criminal offense with the hospital management. In late May 2026, the State Commissioner for Data Protection of Baden-Württemberg confirmed that, until now, 17 hospitals in Baden-Württemberg reported a data breach. The Federal Office for Information Security was contacted and notified by the hospital.
The breach also affected hospitals in Northern Germany, including the University Hospitals in Schleswig-Holstein (UKSH) and Hamburg-Eppendorf (UKE), resulted in the theft of data from around 9,000 patients at UKSH and over 5,000 patients at UKE. Moreover, the public broadcaster NDR reported that at least 6 clinics in Lower Saxony are affected by the breach of Unimed. In late May, the SPD faction in the Schleswig-Holstein state parliament calls for transparency and a complete clarification after the cyberattack. On 28 May, the president of the UKSH said in front of the Schleswig-Holstein state parliament that Unimed reached an agreement the hackers making it very unlikely that sensitive data will be published.

name: China-Linked Mustang Panda aka Twill Typhoon Targeted Organizations in Asia since September 2025
description: Mustang Panda aka Twill Typhoon, a China-linked group, was observed targeting organisations across Japan and the Asia-Pacific region (APJ), starting in late September 2025. The group used fake Apple and Yahoo sites for espionage, and was discovered when several computers connected to fake content delivery networks (CDNs). The hackers used DLL sideloading, where they took a real, safe piece of software and tricked it into running a hidden, malicious file at the same time. They also used real Windows tools, such as dfsvc.exe and vshost.exe, to stay hidden. They used a framework called FDMTP to send new commands and updates to the infected computer, and set up scheduled tasks or hid code in the computer's registry to ensure they didn't lose access if the computer restarted. The attack was first discovered by security experts at Darktrace, who disclosed their findings in a report published on 14 May 2026.

name: Iranian-Nexus Threat Actor Targeted Government Institutions In Oman On 8 April And 10 April 2026
description: An Iranian-nexus threat actor carried out a targeted attack against Oman's government, specifically against the Ministry of Justice and Legal Affairs, on 8 April and 10 April 2026. In an effort to comprimise judicial records and citizen identity data, multiple government entities were infiltrated. The attackers exploited a custom webshell deployed on mersaltest.mjla.gov.om, which provided persistent access to the ministries' network. The group used various tools, including C2 code, session logs, and exfiltrated data, to compromise the network and extract over 26,000 Ministry of Justice user records, judicial case data, committee decisions, and both SAM and SYSTEM registry hives. The attack was discovered by AttackCapture scans on 8 April 2026, and a second directory was captured two days later on 10 April. The attackers used a dedicated gov.om folder, which included 12 exploit scripts, to target multiple Omani government entities. The IT-security company Hunt.io disclosed the incident to the public on 5 May 2026.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Daemon Tools Installers In Supply-Chain Attack On Users In Over 100 Countries On 8 April 2026
description: Unknown threat actors compromised the installers of Daemon Tools, a widely-used disk imaging software, in a supply-chain attack that affected users in more than 100 countries on 8 April 2026. Among the affected countries are Russia, Brazil, Turkey, Spain, Germany, and China. The attackers tampered with the installers and distributed them through the software's official website, embedding backdoors into core software components that executed automatically at system startup. The malicious versions, which were first observed in early April 2026, affected multiple releases of the software installed on thousands of machines. Most victims received only a basic information collector designed to gather system data, while a second, more advanced payload was deployed to a handful of targets, including organizations in government, science, manufacturing, and retail sectors in Russia, Belarus, and Thailand. The attackers likely used the initial data collection to profile infected systems before selectively deploying more sophisticated malware, including a lightweight backdoor used to deliver a more complex implant dubbed Quic RAT. Kaspersky researchers informed the public about the attack on 5 May 2026, and the company Disc Soft, which produces Daemon Tools, disclosed that it was aware of the findings and was investigating. Disc Soft stated that the issue had been successfully addressed within less than 12 hours of identifying it and recommended that all users update their Daemon Tools Lite software to the latest version.

name: Threat Actor PhantomCore Used Malicious Trueconf Client To Target Organizations In Russia Since September 2025
description: PhantomCore exploited TrueConf video conferencing software vulnerabilities to breach the networks of Russian organizations since September 2025. The group leveraged an exploit chain comprising three vulnerabilities (BDU-2025-10114, BDU-2025-10115, and BDU-2025-10116) to execute commands remotely on susceptible servers. The attackers used various tools, including PhantomPxPigeon, PhantomSscp, MacTunnelRat, and PhantomProxyLite, to establish a foothold in the breached environment, conduct reconnaissance, and harvest credentials. The attackers, primarily aimed at the internal processes of a compromised organization and stay in the victim’s infrastructure for a long time, gradually expanding their presence on the network. In some cases, the attackers carried out encryption of both virtual machines and physical servers, as well as workstations of ordinary users. The group targeted public and private organizations of a wide range of industries in Russia, and their attack chains also used phishing lures for initial access. The activities were made public by researchers from the Russian security company Positive Technologies on 20 April 2026.

name: China-Linked Group Mustang Panda Breached Indian Financial Sector And South Korean Political Targets In March 2026

In India, the malware was delivered through spear phishing, sending a file named Request for Support.chm to trick workers in the Indian banking sector by impersonating HDFC, the country’s largest private bank. This file opened a fake popup window, which re-directed users to a server which downloaded and executed a malicious JS File. In South Korea, the threat actor impersonated Victor Cha, a former Director for Asian Affairs on the U.S. National Security Council and current Senior Vice President at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS). The actor sent out Google Drive links to folders named March 30 with fake invitation letters crafted to infect target systems, using DLL sideloading to stay undetected. The Acronis Threat Research Unit informed the public about the campaign in a technical report on 21 April 2026.

name: Russia-Linked APT28 aka Fancy Bear Deployed PRISMEX Malware Against Defence and Critical Infrastructure Targets in Central and Eastern Europe Between September 2025 and January 2026
description: Russia-linked group APT28 (aka Fancy Bear / Pawn Storm) deployed the PRISMEX malware suite against government institutions, military units and critical infrastructure in Ukraine and allied countries including Poland, Romania and Slovakia since September 2025. The threat actors exploited CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513 as zero-days via spearphishing emails themed around military training, weather alerts and weapon smuggling. The attack chain triggered exploitation of CVE-2026-21509, forcing systems to connect to attacker-controlled WebDAV servers and execute malicious LNK files. The PRISMEX malware suite included PrismexSheet, PrismexDrop, PrismexLoader and PrismexStager, employing steganography, COM hijacking and abuse of cloud service Filen.io for command and control. Decoy documents included Ukrainian drone inventories, supplier price lists and military logistics forms. Trend Micro attributed the activity to APT28 with high confidence, noting the campaign reflects a shift toward tactical disruption of Ukraine's supply chain and NATO-linked logistics rather than pure espionage, publishing its findings on 26 March 2026.

name: Unnamed Threat Actors and ShinyHunters Claimed Breach of European Commission Cloud Provider on 24 March 2026
description:
Unnamed threat actors and the cybercrime group ShinyHunters both claimed responsibility for a breach of at least one Amazon Web Services account hosting europa.eu sites of the European Commission on 24 March 2026. The unnamed threat actors contacted BleepingComputer claiming the theft of over 350 GB of data, including multiple databases, and provided screenshots showing access to European Commission employee data. ShinyHunters separately claimed responsibility, adding the European Commission to its dark web leak site and publishing over 90 GB of files allegedly stolen from the Commission's cloud environment, including mail servers, databases, confidential documents and contracts.
The European Commission confirmed the breach, stating that early findings suggest data was taken from the affected websites and that it is notifying Union entities that may have been affected. The Commission stated that no Europa.eu websites were disrupted and that internal systems were not affected. Amazon did not confirm a security incident on its side.
On 2 April 2026, the CERT-EU confirmed in a statement published on their website that about 91.7 GB compressed data was exfiltrated and it is assessed with high confidence that that initial access was obtained through the Trivy supply-chain compromise, which was publicly attributed to a threat actor known as TeamPCP.

name: Russian Intelligence Services Targeted Commercial Messaging Application Users Globally in March 2026
description:
Cyber actors associated with Russian intelligence services conducted global phishing campaigns targeting commercial messaging application (CMA) accounts, including those of current and former US government officials, military personnel, political figures and journalists. The FBI and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) disclosed the campaign in a Public Service Announcement on 20 March 2026.
The campaigns aimed to bypass encryption by compromising individual user accounts rather than the encryption of the applications themselves. The attackers gained unauthorised access to thousands of individual CMA accounts, enabling them to view victims' messages and contact lists, send messages and conduct additional phishing against other CMA accounts.
On 26 June 2026, CISA published an update on their previous announcement stating that the campaign is ongoing and the attackers use new variants of phishing messages.

name: WorldLeaks and Ababil of Minab Claimed Attack on City of Los Angeles and Metro System in the United States on 19 March 2026
description:
The ransomware group WorldLeaks claimed responsibility for an attack on the City of Los Angeles and its Metro system on 19 March 2026. The group added the city to its data leak site on 20 March 2026 and claimed to have stolen 159.9 GB of data. The attack forced the transit agency to limit access to internal administrative computer systems which disrupted station arrival displays. Riders faced issues adding funds to TAP cards online or via support. Rail and bus services continued to run normally. Metro stated that no customer or employee data was affected and continued security checks to restore full access. 
On 9 April 2026, the Iranian threat actor Ababil of Minab also claimed to have hacked the LA Metro (LACMTA). The group allegedly gained administrative access to virtualization infrastructure, web servers, and an operational rail yard management system. Ababil of Minab claimed the data destruction of 500 TB of data and the exfiltration of 100 TB of sensitive data. On 26 May 2026, the Israeli IT security company Gambit published a technical report in which they link the threat actor to Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS).

name: Suspected Russian Espionage Group UNC6353 Compromised Ukrainian Government Website and Spread Full iOS Exploit Chain in late 2025
description: In late 2025, researchers from Lookout Threat Labs discovered DarkSword, a full iOS exploit chain for recent iOS versions that was distributed from a compromised Ukrainian government website. DarkSword leverages several vulnerabilities including multiple zero-day vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-31277, CVE-2026-20700, CVE-2025-43529, CVE-2026-20700, CVE-2025-14174, CVE-2025-43510, CVE-2025-43520). Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) attributed the activities to the suspected Russian espionage group UNC6353. DarkSword aims to extract an extensive set of personal information including credentials from the device and specifically targets a plethora of crypto wallet apps. Since at least November 2025, GTIG has observed multiple commercial surveillance vendors and suspected state-sponsored actors utilizing DarkSword in distinct campaigns. The attack was carried out from a domain domains tied to the probable Russian threat actor UNC6353, as it was discoverd by Google earlier in 2025.

name: Suspected Russian Espionage Group UNC6353 Compromised Ukrainian Government Websites and Spread a Sophisticated iOS Exploit Kit in 2025
description: In 2025, Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) discoverd a suspected Russian espionage group UNC6353 having compromised Ukrainian websites, ranging from industrial equipment and retail tools to local services and ecommerce websites, and leveraging them to deliver a sophisticated iOS exploit toolkit to selected iPhone users from a specific geolocation. In a campaign in July 2025, Coruna was spread by infected Ukrainian websites (watering hole attack). CERT-UA was notified to clean up all compromised websites.

name: China-Nexus Threat Actors Including Camaro Group Compromised Qatari Energy Industry And Military On 1 March 2026
description: On 1 March 2026, China-linked threat actors, including Camaro Dragon group,  targeted unspecified entities in Qatar with malware disguised as Middle East conflict news. The attackers used tools like PlugX and Cobalt Strike to target the Gulf's energy industry and military targets amid rising regional tensions. They sent out files disguised as photos and news articles, which, when opened, contacted a hacked server to pull down more data and used DLL hijacking to secretly run the PlugX backdoor. The malware allowed hackers to steal files, record keystrokes, and take screenshots. The group also targeted Qatar's vital oil and gas industries using a password-protected file named 'Strike at Gulf oil and gas facilities.zip', which delivered a new loader program written in Rust, hiding its malicious code inside a component of NVDA, a legitimate open-source screen reader for the blind. Check Point Research detected and reported the incident, revealing that these hackers began their operations on 1 March 2026.

name: Chinese Threat Actors Suspected of Breaching Federal Bureau Of Investigation Networks In The United States On 17 February 2026
description:
On 17 February 2026, unknown Chinese threat actors breached the Federal Bureau of Investigation's (FBI) networks. The affected system contain sensitive data related to court-authorized wiretaps, pen registers, and FISA warrants, along with personal information on active FBI targets. The hackers exploited the infrastructure of a commercial internet service provider used by the FBI and utilised sophisticated techniques to penetrate the federal network. The incident was detected by the FBI and reported to Congress on 8 March 2026. The National Security Agency (NSA) and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) were involved in the investigation. The Wall Street Journal reported that unnamed U.S. investigators believe that the intruders are affiliated with the Chinese government.

name: Russian State Hackers Infiltrated Messenger Accounts of Dutch Government Employees an a Spearphishing Campaign on 9 March 2026
description:
In early 2026, Russian state hackers infiltrated WhatsApp and Signal groups containing Dutch officials and military personnel by posing as the Signal Support chatbot and using the 'linked devices' function to gain access to sensitive information. The attackers likely targeted journalists and other individuals of interest to the Russian government, in addition to Dutch public servants and military personnel. The attack was conducted solely through social engineering. The Netherlands General Intelligence and Security Service (AIVD) and the Military Intelligence and Security Service (MIVD) confirmed the attack in a joint statement on 9 March 2026 and said that the Russian hackers likely gained access to sensitive information through this campaign.
On 12 March 2026, the German media The Spiegel reported that the former BND VP Arndt Freytag von Loringhoven was among the victims.
On 20 March, the French Cyber Crisis Coordination Center (C4) published a warning note stating they have observed a resurgence in attack campaigns targeting the instant messaging accounts of political figures, high-ranking officials, and government executives. Sectors with a strong government presence are specifically targeted. In their note, C4 directly referenced the publication by Dutch intelligence services as a credible source and stressed the connection to Russian state-sponsored hackers.

description: In late 2025, APT36, a Pakistani threat group also known as Transparent Tribe, unleashed an artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted malware propagation campaign, targeting Indian government entities, diplomatic missions, and defence-related organisations in South Asia. The group used AI-powered development tools to generate large volumes of mediocre-quality malware variants, written in various programming languages. The threat actors leveraged trusted cloud services, including Slack, Discord, Supabase, and Google Sheets, for command and control to make detection more difficult. The campaign was made public by Bitdefender researchers on 5 March 2026. Malware samples were identified between late December 2025 and late January 2026. Bitdefender assess with medium confidence that the observed malicious activity is linked to APT36, also known as Transparent Tribe.

name: Iran-Linked Group MuddyWater Targeted US and International Organisations with New Backdoors in February 2026
description:
Iran-linked threat actor MuddyWater (also known as Seedworm) compromised the networks of multiple organisations in the United States and internationally in February 2026. The US government has previously linked MuddyWater to Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS).
The threat actors targeted an aerospace and defence contractor, a bank, a non-governmental organisation operating in the US and Canada, a software company with a presence in Israel, a US airport and a non-profit organisation. The attackers deployed a new backdoor dubbed Dindoor on the networks of the software supplier's Israeli branch, the US bank and the Canadian NGO, and attempted to exfiltrate data from the software company's Israeli branch. They also deployed a Python backdoor called Fakeset on the networks of a US airport and a non-profit organisation. The activity was disrupted, though other organisations may remain vulnerable.

name: Iran-Nexus Group Dust Specter Targeted Iraqi Government Officials with Phishing Campaign in January 2026
description:
Iran-nexus threat actor Dust Specter targeted Iraqi government officials with a phishing campaign in January 2026, impersonating Iraq's Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Zscaler ThreatLabz published the findings on 6 March 2026, attributing the activity to Dust Specter with medium-to-high confidence based on overlapping tools, techniques and procedures.
The threat actors delivered previously undocumented malware families through multiple infection chains. Attack Chain 1 used password-protected archives containing SPLITDROP, a dropper disguised as a WinRAR application that deployed TWINTASK (a worker component executing PowerShell commands) and TWINTALK (a C2 orchestrator). The malware established persistence through registry keys and used DLL sideloading with legitimate software such as VLC and WingetUI. Attack Chain 2 used GHOSTFORM, a single binary executing commands directly in memory while displaying a fake Google Form posing as a survey from Iraq's Ministry of Foreign Affairs. The campaign also employed a ClickFix lure disguised as a Cisco Webex meeting page to trick victims into running malicious PowerShell commands. Zscaler identified indicators that generative AI may have been used in malware development.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Stole Sensitive Customer Data From GVV Insurance Companies In Germany On Beginning Of February 2026
description: On the beginning of February 2026, a successful hacker attack was carried out on GVV insurance companies, specifically GVV Direct Insurance AG and GVV Communal Insurance VVaG, resulting in the theft of sensitive customer data from approximately 2,600 customers. The attackers gained unauthorised access to parts of the IT systems and downloaded personal data from a temporary database behind the website. The stolen data included contact details and bank connections of the affected customers. The incident was made public in March 2026. The company took immediate action, initiated crisis measures, and informed the affected customers in accordance with the GDPR. The Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (Bafin) and the responsible data protection authority in Rhineland-Palatinate, the State Office for Data and Information (LDI), were also informed about the incident. External IT forensics experts were consulted, and a criminal complaint was filed. The attack had an impact on ongoing business operations, leading to the temporary shutdown of online services, which were later restarted with enhanced protection mechanisms.

name: DumpSec Stole Patient Data In France's Health Care Sector In Late 2025
description: Cybercrime group DumpSec targeted the systems of 1500 medical practices using software of Cegedim Santé, exfiltrating data of 15 million patients in late 2025. The attack compromised between 3 and 15 years of history, depending on the date of installation of the software in doctors' offices. The leaked data included full names, phone numbers, and postal addresses of patients, as well as sensitive medical data of around 169,000 individuals, including that of some high-ranking politicians. On 26 February, Cegedim publicly admitted the incident after France 2 reported the incident after obtaining the publicly available dataset. In the report, the hacker France 2 identified as responsible for the attack disclosed that Cegedim had not responded to his groups communication attempts, and that only parts of the data had been released. The company claimed to have taken all necessary measures, including informing the French Data Protection Authority (CNIL). French Minister of Health, Stéphanie Rist asked Cegedim Santé to account for the causes of the incident,  "corrective measures" to be implemented, and for "guarantees" to prevent any further data leaks.

description: An individual threat actor breached the systems of health company MediMap using login credentials of a legitimate user in New Zealand. The online health portal is used widely across New Zealand in the aged care, disability, hospice, and community health sectors. name: Russian Cybercriminals Diesel Vortex Compromised Logistics Platforms In The United States and Germany Countries Between September 2025 And 24 February 2026
description: From September 2025, Russian cybercriminals, known as Diesel Vortex, carried out a phishing operation targeting Western cargo companies in the U.S. and Europe. Among the affected companies are DAT Truckstop, Penske Logistics, Electronic Fund Source (EFS) and TIMOCOH GmbH. The group exploited the exposed .git directory to gain access to the logistics platforms and compromised over 1,600 login credentials from the platforms over five months, allowing them to intercept and divert freight shipments and commit check fraud. The cybercriminals built phishing infrastructure targeting users of load boards, fleet management portals, and fuel card systems, impersonating carriers and brokers to access freight systems. Researchers with Have I Been Squatted discovered the exposed .git directory, which revealed the operation's details, including messages between the cybercriminals. The researchers informed the public about the attack on 24 February 2026, after uncovering and taking down the infrastructure of the phishing operation.

name: Chinese-Speaking Criminal Group REF4033 Deploying BADIIS Malware On Windows Web Servers In Multiple Countries in Ongoing Campaign Since November 2025
description:
On 11 February 2026, Elastic Security Labs published a report outlining how it had observed an ongoing campaign since at least November 2025 by a Chinese-speaking cybercrime group known as UAT8099 or REF4033; the campaign had impacted victims in Australia, Bangladesh, Brazil, China, India, Japan, Korea, Lithuania, Nepal, and Vietnam. 
The group was observed deploying BADIIS malware onto Windows web servers and coordinating a large-scale SEO poisoning operation from countries across the globe, impacting over 1800 victims across government agencies, educational institutions, healthcare providers, e-commerce platforms, media outlets, and financial services; Elastic links this to a large-scale, opportunistic effort rather than specifically targeting these entitites.
The campaign operates through a two-phase process, first serving keyword-stuffed HTML to search engine crawlers to poison search results, and next, redirecting victims to a sprawling “vice economy” of illicit gambling platforms, pornography, and sophisticated cryptocurrency phishing sites. By deploying the BADIIS malware, a malicious IIS module that integrates directly into a web server's request processing pipeline, the group hijacks the web servers for legitimate government, educational, and corporate domains. This high-reputation infrastructure is used to manipulate search engine rankings, thereby allowing attackers to intercept web traffic and facilitate widespread financial fraud. The malware uses the CopyFileA function to move the contents from the masqueraded files into the .NET directory. Next, the malware parses the DefaultAppPool.config file, examining each node to update the and nodes. The module will inject configuration content from the previously masqueraded file, updating the IIS configuration via a series of append operations. The BADIIS module registers within the request processing pipeline, positioning itself as both the first and the last handler. For each request, the module verifies specific properties and selects an injection or redirection strategy based on the results.

The timely response prevented escalation and limited the perceived impact on users.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Exploited Zero-Day Flaws In Ivanti EPMM In The United States And Europe Around 29 January 2026
description: On or before 29 January 2026, Ivanti Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) was breached by hackers who exploited two critical zero-day code injection flaws, tracked as CVE-2026-1281 and CVE-2026-1340, which allowed remote code execution. The vulnerabilities, classified as CWE-94, were given a severity score of 9.8 out of 10. WatchTowr researchers discovered that the flaws were being actively exploited by attackers to break into systems and set up digital backdoors by sending specifically crafted requests to trick the system's Bash scripts into running malicious code. Ivanti released an emergency advisory and provided a temporary RPM patch fix on 29 January 2026. The attack was disclosed by watchTowr through their blog post and shared with Hackread.com on 2 February 2026. Several affected organizations reported being breached, including the Dutch Data Protection Agency and Council of Judiciary, and the Finnish national agency Valtori. The European Commission also issued a statement disclosing an intrusion in its “central infrastructure managing mobile devices” without explicitly linking the breach to Ivanti. However, several media reports suggest a connection between the incident affecting the European Commission and the Ivanti breach.

Chinese state-linked group Amaranth-Dragon conducted cyberespionage campaigns targeting government and law enforcement agencies across Southeast Asia from March 2025 to October 2025. Check Point Research published findings on 4 February 2026, assessing that the group's tools and techniques overlap with APT-41, suggesting a connection or shared resources. The campaigns targeted entities in Cambodia, Thailand, Laos, Indonesia, Singapore and the Philippines, with attack themes coinciding with significant local geopolitical events. Beginning in August 2025, the group exploited a newly disclosed path traversal vulnerability in WinRAR (CVE-2025-8088) to achieve code execution and persistence on victim systems. The vulnerability was disclosed on 8 August 2025; Amaranth-Dragon weaponised it by 18 August 2025.
The threat actors distributed malicious RAR archives via spearphishing emails, often hosted on legitimate services such as Dropbox. The archives contained a custom loader dubbed Amaranth Loader, which retrieved AES-encrypted payloads and deployed the Havoc command-and-control framework.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Group Mustang Panda Targeted Government Officials and Diplomats with Phishing Campaign from December 2025 to January 2026
description: Chinese state-sponsored group Mustang Panda (also known as HoneyMyte) targeted government officials and international diplomats across Asia and Eastern Europe with a cyberespionage campaign between late December 2025 and mid-January 2026. Security researchers at Dream identified the campaign and shared their findings with Hackread.com on 4 February 2026.The threat actors distributed spearphishing emails masquerading as official US diplomatic correspondence, with subject lines referencing policy updates or internal briefings. The emails contained malicious PDF documents designed to appear as authoritative summaries typically shared by the United States after high-level meetings. Opening the file was sufficient to trigger the compromise. The attackers deployed DOPLUGS, a downloader variant of the PlugX surveillance tool, using DLL search-order hijacking and custom encryption routines to evade detection. The malware used PowerShell to deploy additional tools for data collection.Dream Research Labs first detected the threat in mid-January 2026 after an AI-based hunting agent flagged a suspicious archive. Researchers assessed that the campaign targeted individuals involved in elections and international coordination.

description: On 29 January 2026, HarfangLab published a report describing a new Iranian state-sponsored campaign dubbed "RedKitten" in which attackers were observed conducting a cyber espionage campaign targeting regime-critical individuals living outside Iran via WhatsApp, Instagram, and Telegram since December 2025, coinciding with anti-regime protests in Iran. The attackers used phishing tactics, including sending fake WhatsApp verification codes or links, and a fake web page that mimics the WhatsApp Web login screen with a QR code that belongs to the attackers' own WhatsApp Web page. name: Unknown Threat Actors Hacked Jamaat-E-Islami Party Chief's X Account In Bangladesh On 31 January 2026
description:
On 31 January 2026, the X account of the chief of the Bangladeshi political party Jamaat-e-Islami, Shafiqur Rahman, was allegedly hacked by unknown attackers using malware to briefly gain control of the account and disseminate false and unauthorised statements. 

The attack occurred between 4:37pm and 5:09pm, during which time the hackers logged in from another location without changing the account password. Jamaat-e-Islami's cyber team later regained control using existing security protocols. The party alleged that the attackers used a government email address to hack the account. Following the incident, Sirajul Islam, a member of Jamaat-e-Islami's National Election Steering Committee, filed a general diary (GD) at Hatirjheel Police Station in Dhaka on 01 February 2026. The party claimed that the name of the individual and the email address used in the incident are available on a government website. The attack was reported by Jamaat-e-Islami's central publicity department in a statement issued shortly after midnight on 01 February 2026.

name: US Law Enforcement Seized RAMP Cybercrime Forum In United States On 28 January 2026
description: On 28 January 2026, the US Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), in coordination with the US Attorney's Office for the Southern District of Florida and the Department of Justice's Computer Crime and Intellectual Property Section (CCIPS), seized the clearnet and dark web domains of RAMP (Ramp4u.io), a Russian-language cybercrime forum used by ransomware affiliates, malware developers, and initial access brokers. The seizure was confirmed by seizure banners visible on both domains, which redirected to notices with FBI and DOJ seals, and the nameservers were updated to reflect the seizure. name: Pakistan-Linked Threat Actors Targeted Indian Government Entities with Phishing Campaigns in September 2025
description:
Pakistan-linked threat actors conducted two cyberespionage campaigns, codenamed Gopher Strike and Sheet Attack, targeting Indian government entities in September 2025. Zscaler ThreatLabz identified the campaigns and assessed with medium confidence that the activity may originate from a new subgroup or a Pakistan-linked group operating in parallel to APT36.
In the Gopher Strike campaign, the threat actors distributed spearphishing emails containing PDF documents with blurred images, prompting recipients to download a fake Adobe Acrobat update. The payload delivery infrastructure was configured to respond only to requests from IP addresses in India. The malicious ISO file contained GOGITTER, a Golang-based downloader that deployed GITSHELLPAD, a backdoor leveraging private GitHub repositories for command-and-control communication. Zscaler observed the threat actors issuing more than 200 post-compromise commands and deploying Cobalt Strike Beacon via GOSHELL, a Golang-based shellcode loader. Zscaler ThreatLabz published the findings on 26 January 2026.

The mayor, Telmo Martín, informed the public about the attack, stating that it would take longer than initially thought to resolve the issue.

name: Russia's FSB 16th Centre aka Turla Carried Out Sabotage Operations Against Polish Energy Infrastructure on 29 and 30 December 2025
description: Russia's Federal Security Service (FSB) 16th Centre (aka Turla / Secret Blizzard) carried out disruptive sabotage operations against Polish critical infrastructure, including combined heating and power plants, on 29 and 30 December 2025. The attackers used destructive wiper malware including DynoWiper and LazyWiper, targeting both IT and OT systems. Polish Minister of Digitalisation Krzysztof Gawkowski and Minister of Energy Milosz Motyka disclosed the attack on 13 January 2026, describing it as the most severe attack on energy infrastructure in recent history. Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk stated on 15 January 2026 that "everything indicates that these attacks were prepared by groups directly linked to the Russian services." ESET attributed the attack to Sandworm with medium confidence on 23 January 2026, while Dragos attributed it to ELECTRUM with high confidence. CERT Polska noted that while the malware contained similarities to Sandworm tools, the level of similarity was too low for definitive attribution. On 13 July 2026, the European Union, the United Kingdom and France officially attributed the attack to the FSB's 16th Centre in coordinated statements. The EU imposed restrictive measures on nine individuals and four entities. Germany summoned Russia's ambassadors over the attacks.

name: North Korean State-Sponsored Hackers Targeting South Korean Systems By Abusing Microsoft Visual Studio Code Tunnels In Ongoing Spearphishing Campaign as of January 2026
description:
On 21 January 2026, researchers with DarkTrace published a report on an ongoing North Korean state-sponsored spearphishing campaign targeting victims in South Korea by abusing Microsoft Visual Studio Code tunnels for remote access. 

While exact victimology and timeline is not clear, the report stated that the DPRK actors used government-themed lures to target victims: through using legitimate documents obtained from government websites, they were able to impersonate the Ministry of Personnel Management, a South Korean government agency responsible for managing the civil service.

Through victims downloading the legitimate-appearing but malicious documents, the DPRK actors were granted terminal and file access and proceeded to use living-off-the-land (LOTL) techniques to establish persistence. DarkTrace warned that the use of VS Code tunnels enabled the DPRK actors to communicate through trusted Microsoft infrastructure instead of dedicated C2 servers, making detection more difficult.

name: Individual Hacktivist 'Vindex' Leaked Personal Data of Spanish Politicians Periodically Since 19 January 2026
description:
An individual hacker known under the alias 'Vindex' leaked personal data of 47 politicians from 15 autonomous communities in Spain on or around 19 January 2026. The affected individuals included regional presidents Isabel Díaz Ayuso (Community of Madrid), Juanma Moreno (Andalusia), María Guardiola (Extremadura), Alfonso Fernández Mañueco (Castile and León), Imanol Pradales (Basque Country), Francisco Pérez Llorca (Valencian Community), María Chivite (Navarre) and Jorge Azcón (Aragon), as well as regional councillors and advisors. 
The exposed data included personal email addresses, mobile phone numbers, ID numbers, home addresses, bank account numbers and car registration numbers- 
'Vindex' claimed responsibility for the leak and announced intentions to target additional autonomous communities in a "version 2", potentially including family members of politicians. This incident appears to be related to the Adamuz train accident.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Targeted Government Employees In Afghanistan Using Malicious ISO File And FALSECUB Malware On 23 December 2025
description: On 23 December 2025, an unknown threat group targeted Afghan government employees with phishing emails disguised as official correspondence from senior officials, including the office of the country's prime minister. The infection chain started with a malicious ISO file named Afghanistan Islami Emirates.iso, which contained a decoy document, a malicious LNK file, and the final payload, a C executable named FALSECUB. The FALSECUB malware had anti-analysis features and connected to a command-and-control server to receive commands and exfiltrate data. The malware enumerated the target's username, machine name, and Windows version, and exfiltrated files from the Desktop and Documents directories. Seqrite researchers discovered that the attackers relied on GitHub as a temporary hosting service for the malicious payload and identified multiple legal and administrative documents uploaded by the threat actor to the Scribd library. The alleged threat actor used an alias 'Afghan Khan' shared on other platforms, including Pinterest and Dailymotion, with at least one account linked to Pakistan. Seqrite informed the public about the attack in a report released on 19 January 2026 and designated the malicious campaign Nomad Leopard.

name: Russian State-Sponsored Hacking Group Void Blizzard Targeted Representatives of Ukrainian Military Using Fake Donation Lures from October-December 2025
description:
From October to December 2025, the Russian state-sponsored hacking group Void Blizzard (aka Laundry Bear) allegedly carried out a cyber campaign targeting representatives of the Armed Forces of Ukraine using PLUGGYAPE malware, as reported by CERT-UA on 12 January 2026. 

The attackers exploited instant messaging apps to target their victims, luring targets into visiting fake webpages of charitable donation funds. The fake websites, which looked legitimate, prompted visitors to click documents on the webpage which included misleading file extensions, thus appearing harmless. Upon clicking the document, the victim would then unknowingly download malicious executable files, which installed the PLUGGYAPE backdoor, giving Void Blizzard remote access to infected systems. Void Blizzard was also observed utilising instant messaging apps to directly send fraudulent documents to victims, skipping the webpage step entirely.

The attack was attributed to Void Blizzard by CERT-UA with medium confidence.

name: Unknown Hackers Stole Digital Currencies From Kontigo In Venezuela On 5 and 8 January 2026
description: On 5 January 2026, Kontigo, a popular digital wallet in Venezuela, detected an intrusion resulting in the loss of approximately 290.000€ from over 1,000 users. The attack exploited a vulnerability in a legacy Supabase Apple OIDC authentication flow, allowing attackers to mint valid authentication tokens and access user accounts, compounded by insufficient row-level security on certain backend tables. Kontigo reported the incident on 5 January 2026, and assured users that they are committed to reinstating the entirety of the stolen funds. On 8 January 2026, a follow-up attack reused previously captured wallet session tokens from Kontigo’s embedded wallet provider, Thirdweb, resulting in an additional 49.000€ loss from 258 users. Kontigo invalidated all active session tokens, strengthened authentication controls, reduced token lifetimes, and issued 100% refunds within 24 hours, warning users of potential scam attempts and reaffirming its commitment to fund restitution and platform security.

name: Russian State-Sponsored APT28 Stole Credentials From Organisations in Energy and Government In Europe and Asia Between February And September 2025
description: Between February and September 2025, the Russian state-sponsored group APT28 (aka UAC0001, Fancy Bear, Pawn Storm, Sofacy Group, Sednit, BlueDelta, and STRONTIUM) carried out credential-harvesting campaigns against Turkish, European, and Central Asian organisations. Targets of the campaigns included Turkish energy and nuclear staff, European think tank personnel, a military organisation in North Macedonia, and an unmentioned target in Uzbekistan. APT28 used fake login pages mimicking Outlook, Google, and Sophos VPN to steal credentials, redirecting victims to real sites. They relied on free hosting, tunneling services, and PDF lures, showing the GRU's ongoing, low-cost focus on credential harvesting for intelligence purposes. The Insikt Group reported the attack in a publication dated 12 January 2026, which included Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) and mitigations.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Spanish Energy Provider Endesa On 29 December 2025
description: On 29 December 2025, Endesa, a Spanish energy company, was breached by an unknown actor, who gained access to its commercial platform through its supplier, Energia XXI. The actors extracted personal data, including basic identification data, contact details, ID, contract data, and payment methods (International Bank Account Numbers/IBANs) of approximately 10 million customers, comprising about 1TB of data. The attack did not compromise access passwords. Endesa reported the incident to its customers and notified the National Institute of Cybersecurity (Incibe) and the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD). Through a preliminary investigation, Spanish police tracked dark web sites related to the stolen data and stated that the attack was believed, at least tentatively, to have originated in Spain. Security protocols were activated, and technical and organisational measures were taken to contain and mitigate the effects of the attack, including blocking access by compromised users and analysing accesses. Endesa warned potential victims to be cautious about communications received due to the possibility of phishing campaigns or spam.

name: Pakistani State-Sponsored Hacking Group APT36 Breached Indian Governmental And Academic Entities Since At Least Mid-December 2025
description: Transparent Tribe (APT36), a Pakistan-aligned cyber-espionage group, conducted a targeted phishing campaign against Indian governmental, academic and strategic entities using a multi-stage malware chain centred on weaponised Windows shortcut (LNK) files. The campaign was active from at least mid-December 2025 and was publicly documented at the end of December 2025. The attackers distributed spear-phishing emails containing ZIP archives with oversized LNK files disguised as legitimate PDF documents. When opened, the shortcut invoked the trusted Windows utility mshta.exe to retrieve and execute a remote HTML Application (HTA) script in a fileless manner. This loader performed layered decryption routines and reconstructed encrypted payloads entirely in memory, while simultaneously opening a decoy PDF to avoid arousing suspicion. The infection chain deployed a fully featured Remote Access Trojan (RAT) capable of persistent access, system reconnaissance, file theft, surveillance and remote command execution. The malware dynamically adapted its persistence and execution methods based on the victim’s installed antivirus software and used encrypted command-and-control communications to maintain long-term, covert access.

name: Chronus Hacking Group Breached Multiple Mexican Government Institutions in Coordinated Data Theft and Defacement Campaign In December 2025
description:
The hacking group Chronus breached the systems of more than 20 Mexican public institutions in December 2025, including the Tax Administration Service (SAT) and IMSS Bienestar. The attackers used tools designed to infiltrate government web portals, gaining unauthorised access with the aim of stealing user information. The campaign involved both data theft and website defacement. Chronus modified the appearance of several government websites, leaving messages mocking the security of the affected portals, and exfiltrated personal data that was later leaked through underground online forums. Investigations indicated that the group carried out at least 26 attacks in total, comprising 23 incidents of information theft and three defacements.
Mexican authorities, including the Secretariat for Anti-Corruption and Good Governance, announced an investigation into the suspected data leaks on the same day. The attacks were publicly reported by journalist Ignacio Gómez Villaseñor. Chronus effectively claimed responsibility through its defacement messages and data disclosures.

name: Kazu Breached New Zealand–Based Health Application ManageMyHealth In December 2025
description:
Kazu gained unauthorised access to the Manage My Health platform before 29 December 2025, a New Zealand–based application used by patients and general practitioners to communicate, manage prescriptions and share test results. According to court documents, the attackers were able to exploit an API endpoint.

The incident affected approximately 120,000 users, representing around 6-7 per cent of the platform’s 1.8 million registered users in New Zealand. Exfiltrated data included clinical discharge summaries, referrals, and related files relating to patients, historical clinical referral records, personal health information uploaded by patients, and clinical referral requests relating to patients. The courts found that this included highly sensitive data such as patient medical histories. 

Manage My Health stated that the unauthorised access had been contained and that the platform remained operational. On 29 December 2025, Kazu claimed responsibility for the breach on its darkforum site and attempted to extort the platform.

name: An Unknown Hacker Using the Alias 888 Stole Sensitive Data From European Space Agency On 18 December 2025
description:
A hacker using the alias “888” breached the European Space Agency on 18 December 2025 and claimed to have exfiltrated more than 200 GB of internal data, which was subsequently advertised for sale on DarkForums in exchange for Monero (XMR).
The allegedly stolen material included private Bitbucket repositories, internal documentation, infrastructure definitions and sensitive credentials. Screenshots shared by the actor appeared to show internal ESA environments, including build configuration files, proprietary technical documentation and internal development and project management tools. The actor claimed responsibility for the intrusion on DarkForums. The European Space Agency confirmed awareness of a cybersecurity incident affecting a limited number of external servers outside its corporate network and initiated a forensic investigation alongside containment measures. Preliminary findings suggest the impact was confined to a small set of unclassified servers supporting collaborative scientific engineering activities.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Cryptocurrency Wallet Provider Trust Wallet In The United States On 21 December 2025
description: On 21 December 2025, unknown hackers infiltrated Trust Wallet’s Chrome extension, resulting in approximately 6 million euros in losses for around one million users. Trust Wallet provides non-custodial crypto wallets enabling users to store and manage digital assets across multiple blockchains. The attackers exploited a malicious code inserted into the 2.68 update, which iterated through all stored wallets, prompting mnemonic phrase requests, and sent the decrypted mnemonics to an attacker-controlled server, api.metricstrustwallet.com. The domain was registered on 8 December 2025, with activity starting on 21 December 2025. The attack was discovered on 25 December 2025, and Trust Wallet urged users to update to version 2.69 to mitigate the issue. Changpeng Zhao, co-founder of Binance, announced that the lost funds would be reimbursed. Blockchain cybersecurity firm SlowMist and on-chain detective ZachXBT reported on the incident, with SlowMist's Yu Xian suggesting that the attacker was 'very familiar' with the Trust Wallet extension's source code, leading some to speculate about possible insider involvement. The attack was disclosed to the public by Trust Wallet on 25 December 2025.

description: On 21 December 2025, Romanian Waters, the country's water management authority, suffered a ransomware attack, which affected approximately 1,000 computer systems across the central organization and 10 of its 11 regional offices. The attackers used Windows BitLocker to encrypt systems and issued a ransom note demanding contact within seven days. The incident disrupted IT assets, including GIS servers, databases, email, and web services, Windows workstations, and domain name servers. Technical teams from the National Directorate of Cyber Security (DNSC), Romanian Waters, the SRI's National Cyberint Center, and other authorities investigated the incident and worked to contain its impact. The DNSC and Romanian Waters informed the public about the attack on 22 December 2025.

name: Hamas-Affiliated Threat Group Ashen Lepus Conducted Cyber Espionage On Middle Eastern Government And Military Entities In Middle East in 2025
description: Ashen Lepus, a threat group assessed to be affiliated with Hamas, conducted an espionage campaign targeting government and military entities across the Middle East. Unit42 observed that long-standing espionage efforts continued beyond the October 2025 ceasefire between Hamas and Israel, at least through 11 December 2025. The operation used Arabic-language phishing lures referencing regional political and military developments to compromise targeted endpoints. name: Anonymous Hackers Infiltrated Russian Tech Firm Involved In Building Unified Military Registration Database For Russia In 2025
description: In 2025, anonymous hackers infiltrate the servers of the Russian tech firm Mikord alleged to be involved in building the country’s unified military registration (UMR) database for multiple months and stole many documents. According to Grigory Sverdlov, head of the Russian anti-war human rights group Idite Lesom (“Get Lost”), the hackers contacted him and handed over a trove of internal Mikord documents, including source code, technical and financial records, and internal correspondence.
