name: China-Nexus WARP PANDA Breached Organizations In North America In Summer 2025
description: A China-nexus threat actor, tracked by CrowdStrike as WARP PANDA, conducted multiple intrusions against US and North American organizations. The attackers exploited internet-facing devices and pivoted into VMware vCenter environments and cloud platforms, such as Microsoft Azure, using valid credentials or known vulnerabilities. The group used SSH, SFTP, and the privileged vpxuser account to move laterally across networks. They employed techniques such as log wiping, file timestomping, and the creation of malicious virtual machines to maintain covert, persistent access. WARP PANDA also tunnelled traffic through BRICKSTORM implants on vCenter servers, ESXi hosts, and guest VMs, and extracted information from thin-provisioned VM snapshots using an ESXi-compatible version of 7Zip. The group accessed email accounts of employees working on issues aligned with PRC strategic interests, and infiltrated Microsoft Azure environments at multiple organizations, accessing email, OneDrive, and SharePoint. Additionally, WARP PANDA staged data for exfiltration, extracted information from thin-provisioned VM snapshots, and cloned domain controller virtual machines to access sensitive Active Directory data. The intrusion activity is believed to have started around summer 2025. CrowdStrike reported the campaign on 5 December 2025, noting that WARP PANDA's ability to maintain long-term persistence indicates both high skill and a singular focus on extracting valuable internal and national-security-relevant data. The attackers' motivations appeared to be aligned with the long-term intelligence priorities of the People's Republic of China.

name: Chinese State-Backed Threat Actors Earth Lamia And Jackpot Panda Exploited React2Shell Vulnerability On 3 December 2025
description: On 3 December 2025, the React2Shell vulnerability, also known as CVE-2025-55182, was exploited by Chinese state-backed hackers, including Earth Lamia and Jackpot Panda. The vulnerability is in the React Server Components tool, which was maintained by Meta and is embedded in approximately 50 million websites and products. The vulnerability was initially reported to Meta by researcher Lachlan Davidson on 29 November 2025 and publicly disclosed on 4 December 2025, when a fix was rolled out. On 3 December 2025, the attackers were observed by Amazon Web Services (AWS) to be exploiting the bug, using IP addresses and infrastructure linked to known China state-nexus threat actors. They attempted to exploit the vulnerability using both automated scanning tools and individual proof-of-concept (PoC) exploits. AWS reporting warned on 5 December 2025 that the vulnerability could be fully exploited in the coming days and weeks as PoCs spread widely. The US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency added the bug to its catalogue of exploited vulnerabilities on 6 December 2025.

name: Unidentified Perpetrators Carried Out Ransomware Attack Against Southold Town In United States On 24 November 2025
description:
Unidentified perpetrators hit Southold Town in Suffolk County, New York, US with a ransomware attack on 24 November 2025 that disrupted municipal operations and disabled access to town email and servers. The incident was discovered early in the morning when core IT systems went offline, which forced departments to revert to manual processes, including police officers writing reports by hand.
Southold worked with the Suffolk County Office of Emergency Management and state and federal partners, including DHS and the FBI, to contain the incident. Town Supervisor Al Krupski confirmed that a ransom note was found but not opened. Phone systems, including 911, remained operational, while the Laserfiche records system stayed offline.
The attack caused widespread disruption, with staff relying on text messages, fax machines or pen and paper to maintain services. Issuance of resident beach and waste disposal permits was postponed until 15 December. It remained unclear how the attackers accessed town systems or whether any resident data was compromised.

name: Unidentified Ransomware Group Breached Fintech Company Marquis And Exfiltrated Sensitive Data From 780,000 Customers In the United States On 14 August 2025
description:
On 14 August 2025, an unidentified ransomware group breached Marquis, a Texas-based fintech and software firm, by exploiting its SonicWall firewall, and stole personal and financial data. Based on data breach filings with authorities in Iowa, Maine, Massachusetts, New Hampshire, South Carolina, Texas, and Washington, personal information from around 780,000 individuals as well as data related to dozens of banks were affected. Compromised information including names, addresses, phone numbers, Social Security numbers, Taxpayer Identification Numbers, financial account information without security or access codes, and dates of birth. The company detected suspicious activity on its network and determined that it was the victim of a ransomware attack. After that, an investigation revealed that an unauthorized third party accessed Marquis’ network through its SonicWall, and federal authorities were notified. Marquis started sending written notification letters to affected individuals and filed data breach notices with the Attorney Generals Offices in several US states.
At the end of January 2026, Marquis confirmed the incident, with reports stating that over 700 banks and financial institutions were impacted by the incident.

name: Lazarus Suspected of Stealing Approximately 33.3 Million Euros From Crypto Exchange Upbit In South Korea On 27 November 2025
description: On 27 November 2025, South Korea's top crypto exchange, Upbit, suffered a major security breach, losing approximately 44.5 billion Korean won, about 33.3 million euros, in digital assets, primarily Solana-based tokens. The attack began just hours after its parent company, Dunamu Inc., unveiled a massive 9.3 billion euros takeover by tech giant Naver Corp. The attackers exploited a vulnerability in one of Upbit's Hot Wallets, used for fast, day-to-day transactions. The intrusion forced Upbit to halt all deposits and withdrawals on 27 November 2025. The stolen assets included over 20 tokens, prominently featuring Solana (SOL), USDC, Bonk (BONK), Jupiter (JUP), Render Token (RENDER), Orca (ORCA), and Peace Network (PYTH). Oh Kyoungsuk, CEO of Dunamu, informed the public about the attack on 27 November 2025, expressing his deep regret and reassuring customers that Upbit's assets will be fully covered to prevent any damage. Yonhao News Agency (YNA), the main national newswire of South Korea, reported that unnamed government officials suspect Lazarus, a North Korean state-linked threat actor, to be responsible for the attack.

name: North Korea-nexus Group Moonstone Sleet Suspected of Compromising South Korean Managed Service Provider and Deploying Qilin Ransomware Against Downstream Customers In September 2025
description: In September 2025, a Managed Service Provider (MSP) in South Korea was breached, affecting at least 20 South Korean asset management companies. The threat actors, believed to be associated with Qilin, a large ransomware-as-a-service platform, released over 2TB of data on the dark web, combining political and propaganda messages. The breach of the MSP was the initial access vector, and the attack began with the simultaneous release of 10 financial management companies' data, followed by ransomware-focused messages, and finally returning to traditional ransomware messages. On 24 November 2025, the IT security company Bitdefender reported on the campaign discussing the possible involvement of Moonstone Sleet, a North Korean state-affiliated group. Bitdefender based this assessment on Moonstone Sleet's use of Qilin ransomware, first documented in February 2025 by Microsoft.

name: Suspected GRU-Linked RomCom Threat Actor Delivered Mythic Agent via SocGholish to U.S. Civil Engineering Firm Around September 2025
description: RomCom threat actors used the SocGholish fake-update loader to deliver Mythic Agent malware to a United States civil engineering firm on or around September 2025. Arctic Wolf Labs, which reported the incident on 25 November 2025, noted that the payload executed about ten minutes after initial exploitation and assessed the attack with medium to high confidence as linked to Russia’s GRU Unit 29155. SocGholish, a fake browser-update framework spread through compromised websites, initiated the infection chain via obfuscated JavaScript and PowerShell reconnaissance before deploying RomCom’s loader disguised as msedge.dll, which executed only when the victim’s Active Directory domain matched a hardcoded value. Arctic Wolf identified seven related command-and-control domains tied to RomCom. The end-to-end timeline from FAKEUPDATE infection to attempted Mythic Agent delivery was under thirty minutes.

name: INC Ransom Breached Crisis24's Emergency Notification System "OnSolve" and Disrupted Emergency Services in Several Counties In United States On 10 November 2025
description:
Around 1 November 2025, INC Ransom targeted the third party emergency alert system of several hundred entities on both state and local government levels through Crisis24's OnSolve CodeRED system, which sends calls, texts, emails, and mobile alerts in the event of emergencies. INC Ransom initially breached OnSolve on 1 November and encrypted data on 10 November, disrupting CodeRED systems and ultimately leading to the temporary disruption of numerous localities' emergency alert services as several municipalities in the United States worked to migrate to new emergency alert systems after the disruption of OnSolve CodeRED. The incident also led to alerting authorities having their access to FEMA's Integrated Public Alert and Warning System revoked. 

According to OnSolve/Crisis24 and INC's sample of data published on their Tor leak site, information stolen includes phone numbers, addresses, emails, usernames, and passwords for CodeRED accounts.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Telangana Government And Media Groups In India By Sending Malicious APK Files in fake ID-update Messages On 23 November 2025
description: On 23 November 2025, unknown threat actors compromised several official WhatsApp groups linked to cabinet ministers, senior government officers, and journalists in the Indian state Telangana by sending a fake SBI Aadhaar update message embedded with a harmful APK file. The malicious APK file allegedly gained remote control of the users' devices, including WhatsApp access, once downloaded and opened. Multiple media groups, ministers' official communication channels, the CMO WhatsApp group, and the Deputy Chief Ministers' group were believed to have come under the hackers' control. The phones of public relations officers and several journalists were also reportedly breached. The Telangana Cyber Crime police initiated verification and containment measures, while cybersecurity experts urged WhatsApp users to avoid interacting with suspicious messages or files and issued precautions such as not installing or opening any APK file received via WhatsApp or SMS, ignoring unsolicited Aadhaar or bank-related update links, enabling two-factor authentication, and reporting unfamiliar or suspicious activity immediately. The breach was reported by various media outlets on 23 November 2025, with screenshots and warnings spreading rapidly online, and the Cyber Crime police were informed about the incident, which is still under investigation.

description:

name: Unidentifed Perpetrators Stole Data From Italy's Railway Company Ferrovie Dello Stato Around 20 November 2025
description:
Unknown threat actors breached Almaviva’s systems, which manage core IT functions for Italy's railway company Ferrovie dello Stato (FS) on 20 November 2025 and exfiltrated approximately 2.3 terabytes of internal data. The threat actors appeared to possess prior knowledge of the environment and accessed shared folders containing operational and strategic files. The Rome Prosecutor’s Office opened an investigation and assigned the case to Cnaipic, the Postal Police unit responsible for protecting national strategic infrastructures.

Everest demanded that a Petrobras representative contact them via the encrypted messaging platform Tox within four days.

name: ByteToBreach Infiltrated Telco Company Eurofiber France And Other Regional Brands On 13 November 2025
description: ByteToBreach exploited a vulnerability in Eurofiber's ticketing system and ATE customer portal to breach the European fiber operator on 13 November 2025, stealing data and attempting extortion. The attackers targeted Eurofiber France and its brands Avelia, Eurafibre, FullSave and Netiwan. The stolen data included SSH private keys, VPN configurations, API keys, cloud access tokens, SQL backups, source code, internal scripts, support tickets, attachments, internal messages, ID scans, screenshots, documentation, network inventories and architecture details. Eurofiber France informed the CNIL and ANSSI and filed a complaint, stating that the incident did not affect customers using services of other Eurofiber entities on platforms located in Belgium, Germany or the Netherlands.

name: Howling Scorpius Deployed Akira Ransomware On Unnamed Global Data Storage and Infrastructure Company Sometime Before 19 November 2025
description:
According to a publication on 18 November 2025 by Palo Alto Network's Unit42, Howling Scorpius was observed deploying Akira ransomware against a global data storage and infrastructure company.
Howling Scorpius compromised the data storage company through a compromised car dealership website, which hosted a ClickFix-type malware loader disguised as a security captcha. An employee of the data company engaged with the captcha, inadvertently downloading SectopRAT malware onto the data storage company's servers, giving the attackers a foothold. The attackers established a backdoor, conducted reconnaissance, and compromised multiple privileged accounts, eventually deploying Akira ransomware across servers in three separate networks over a period of 42 days.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Hacking Group Salt Typhoon Breached Government Entities In Latin America Between June and September 2025
description:
Between June and September 2025, FamousSparrow (also known as Salt Typhoon), a Chinese state-sponsored APT group, was observed by ESET researchers to be targeting several government entities in Latin America.
In July 2025, ESET observed FamousSparrow's SparrowDoors loader and samples on the systems of government entities in Argentina, Guatemala, and Honduras, with ESET's report stating that FamousSparrow likely exploited a ProxyLogon vulnerability. 
The following month, ESET detected a compromise of several systems belonging to a governmental entity in Panama, with "clear evidence" that FamousSparrow used atexec-pro, an open-source post-exploitation tool, to move laterally within the network. 
FamousSparrow's victims in the campaign documented for Latin America included multiple governmental entities in Argentina, a governmental entity in Ecuador, a governmental entity in Guatemala, multiple governmental entities in Honduras, and a governmental entity in Panama.

name: Russian-Aligned Hacker Group Noname057(16) Disrupted Danish Political Parties' Websites On 17 November 2025
description:
A pro-Russian group known as NoName057(16) claimed responsibility for carrying out DDoS attacks on several Danish political parties' websites on the eve of local elections, including those of the Conservatives, the Red-Green Alliance, the Moderates and the ruling Social Democrats on 17 November 2025.
The attacks temporarily prevented access to the websites on the eve of the aforementioned elections; the Danish Agency for Society Security confirmed that several websites had become inaccessible due to DDoS attacks and noted that this coincided with a forecast expressed in a security assessment published on 7 November, which stated that the threat of cyberattacks was high and that pro-Russian groups were likely to carry out such attacks. NoName057(16) claimed responsibility for the attack on their social media channels, stating that they had targeted the websites of these Danish political parties; the attacks also targeted other entities, such as the Copenhagen Post.  On 18 December 2025, the Danish Defence Intelligence Service (DDIS) officially attributed the attack to NoName057(16) and linked the group to the Russian state.

The CNIL and ANSSI were notified, and a criminal complaint was filed with the Public Prosecutor's Office on 17 November 2025.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Hacking Group GTG-1002 Conducted A Sophisticated AI-Orchestrated Cyber Espionage Campaign Against 30 Critical Entities In September 2025
description:
Chinese state-sponsored group of hackers, dubbed GTG-1002, manipulated Anthropic's Claude Code model to conduct an AI-orchestrated cyber espionage campaign against 30 entities including major technology corporations, financial institutions, chemical manufacturing companies, and government agencies across multiple countries in mid-September 2025. The attackers bypassed Claude Code's protections by inducing it to believe it was performing regular defensive security activities for a company, allowing the AI to operate without recognising the illicit nature of the tasks performed. The model identified vulnerabilities, generated exploit code, collected credentials, created backdoors, classified stolen data, and prepared intelligence packages ready for use. The human
operator tasked instances of Claude Code to operate in groups as autonomous penetration
testing orchestrators and agents, with the threat actor able to leverage AI to execute
80-90% of tactical operations independently at physically impossible request rates. In November 2025, Anthropic published a detailed report on the cyber espionage campaign and stated that new measures were already implemented to mitigate such activities in the future.

name: Unknown Perpetrators Breached French Weda Software Around 10 November 2025
description:
On or around 10 November 2025, unknown threat actors launched a cyberattack against Weda, a medical software platform used by around 85,000 healthcare professionals in France, including general practitioners, specialists, midwives and paramedical staff, to manage patient records. 
The incident reportedly affected at least 23,000 users. Weda detected suspicious activity suggesting unauthorised access to certain accounts and took the platform offline as a precaution to prevent data leakage. Initial analyses indicated that partial data extraction may have occurred, though the extent of any breach remained unconfirmed. The incident caused major disruption as doctors were forced to revert to manual record-keeping.

name: Pro-Russian Hacker Group Noname057(16) Conducted Distributed Denial Of Service Attack On Danish Government And Defence Websites In Denmark On 13 November 2025
description:
On 13 November 2025, the pro-Russian hacker group NoName057 claimed responsibility for a series of DDoS attacks targeting Danish government and defence websites, including the Ministry of Transport, the public sector portal Borger.dk, and the defence company Terma. The Danish Civil Protection Agency stated that it was aware of several companies and websites experiencing disruptions and operational failures due to DDoS attacks and was closely monitoring the situation in coordination with Danish military intelligence. Tobias Brun-Falkencrone, a spokesperson for Terma, confirmed that access to the company's website was disrupted. The attacks followed attacks claimed by NoName057(16) which took place the previous day and coincided with upcoming Danish local elections.
On 18 December 2025, the Danish Defence Intelligence Service (DDIS) officially attributed the attack to NoName057(16) and linked the group to the Russian state.

name: China-linked UAT-4356 Exploiting Cisco Secure Firewalls to Target Government Organisations in Ongoing ArcaneDoor Campaign discovered in November 2025
description:
On 5 November 2025, Cisco updated a publication from 25 September 2025 related to an ongoing campaign earlier attributed to Chinese state-sponsored threat actors, UAT-4356, in what was dubbed "ArcaneDoor." In its 5 November publication, Cisco stated that it was observing new waves of attacks against Cisco Secure Firewalls targeting unnamed government institutions, specifically through a new attack variant exploiting the previously reported vulnerabilities CVE-2025-20333 and CVE-2025-20362 in Cisco ASA and FTD devices. The attackers exploited these vulnerabilities to gain remote access and control over unpatched systems, potentially obtaining root privileges and installing malware such as RayInitiator and LINE VIPER. While in the US CISA alerted the public of the existence of the ArcaneDoor campaign in September 2025 and in an earlier update on the exploited vulnerabilities in April 2025, Cisco's updated warning about government targets did not specify the countries in which governmental organisations were being targeted.
While Cisco confirmed the compromise of several targets that were brought to the attention of Cisco through government incident response agencies assisting the affected organisations, Cisco also stated that the attacks put government and telecommunications infrastructure at broader risk of outages and takeovers, with potentially severe operational and data losses.

name: North Korean State-Sponsored KONNI Group Conducted Sophisticated Spear-Phishing Campaing Via KakaoTalk Messenging App And Remotely Wiped Android Devices In South Korea In November 2025
description: In September 2025, a threat actor, attributed to the North Korean state-sponsored KONNI Group, conducted a sophisticated spear-fishing campaign, infiltrating Android phones and personal computers using malicious software sent via the KakaoTalk messaging app. In some cases, Google Android–based smartphones and tablet PCs were remotely reset by abusing Google's "Find Hub" service, resulting in the unauthorized deletion of personal data stored on the devices. On 5 September 2025, the threat actor compromised and abused the KakaoTalk account of a South Korea–based counsellor who specializes in psychological support for North Korean defector youth, and sent a malicious file disguised as a “stress relief program” to an actual defector student. After gaining initial access, the threat actor conducted prolonged internal reconnaissance and remote monitoring, during which they collected and exfiltrated the victim’s information and key account credentials, ultimately gaining access to Google and Naver accounts. On 15 September, a separate victim’s KakaoTalk account was used to distribute malicious files en masse in a simultaneous wave. The Genians Security Centre (GSC) published a detailed report on 6 November 2025.

description:
On 4 November 2025, the Akira ransomware group compromised a US subsidiary of the Swiss defence company RUAG MRO Holding Ltd. in Virginia. The attack targeted the liaison office, which employs eight people and is responsible for exchanges with American institutions, suppliers, and partner companies. The Akira ransomware group claimed responsibility for the incident and threatened to publish 24 gigabytes of company data, including confidential military information, contracts, and agreements. The company stated that due to the self-sufficiency of the IT systems used there, the incident is isolated and has no impact on other systems within the RUAG Group. Moreover, no particularly sensitive data relating to employees in Switzerland has been affected.
On 8 June 2026, Ruag Board President Jürg Rötheli acknowledged to the public broadcaster SRF that a ransom was paid.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Garden Finance and Stole US$10.8 Million on 30 October 2025
description:
Unknown threat actors compromised Garden Finance, a Bitcoin bridge protocol, on 30 October 2025 by exploiting one of its solvers, resulting in the theft of approximately US$11 million in digital assets. Garden Finance stated that the incident was limited to the solver’s inventory and that user funds remained unaffected, though it temporarily shut down its application to investigate.
Blockchain investigator ZachXBT reported that affected assets straddled several blockchains and shared a message allegedly from Garden Finance, offering the attackers a 10% bounty for returning the stolen funds and explaining the exploit. The message suggested that the compromised solver may have been operated internally rather than autonomously. Garden’s co-founder, Jaz Gulati, confirmed that the company was working with external security experts to identify the root cause and strengthen protocol resilience.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Hacking Group Bronze Butler Exploited Zero-Day Vulnerability In Corporate Systems In Japan in April 2025
description:
On an unspecified date in April 2025, a Chinese state-sponsored APT known as Tick (aka Bronze Butler, Daserf, REDBALDKNIGHT, Stalker Panda, Stalker Taurus, and Swirl Typhoon) exploited a zero-day vulnerability in Motex Lanscope Endpoint Manager, tracked as CVE-2025-61932, to hijack the systems of corporate victims "in sectors aligned with their intelligence objectives," according to a report on the activity published by Sophos Threat Intelligence. 
Tick used the vulnerability to execute arbitrary commands with SYSTEM privileges on on-premise versions of the program and dropped a backdoor, known as Gokcpdoor, which established a proxy connection with a remote server and acted as a backdoor to execute malicious commands on the compromised host(s). The attack was characterised by the deployment of the Havoc post-exploitation framework, DLL sideloading, and the use of various tools such as goddi, Remote Desktop, 7-Zip, io, LimeWire, and Piping Server to facilitate lateral movement and data exfiltration. JPCERTCC and Sophos reported the attack, with Sophos detecting two different types of Gokcpdoor and observing the exploitation of CVE-2025-61932.

name: Unnamed Hacktivists Tampered Internet-accessible Industrial Control Systems In Canada In October 2025
description: In October 2025, unnamed hacktivists internet-accessible industrial control systems in Canada, including a water facility, where they tampered with water pressure values, resulting in degraded service for its community. They also targeted a Canadian oil and gas company, manipulating an Automated Tank Gauge (ATG) and triggering false alarms. Additionally, a grain drying silo on a Canadian farm was compromised, with temperature and humidity levels being manipulated, potentially creating unsafe conditions if not addressed promptly. The incidents were reported by Canadian Centre for Cyber Security without further details on 29 October 2025.

name: Unattributed Suspected Eastern European Threat Actor Group Breached City of Gloversville In The United States On 14 March 2025
description: The City of Gloversville, New York, was hit by a ransomware attack in March 2025. The breach was detected on 14 March 2025 through a digital ransom note by the attackers. According to city officials, the incident compromised the personal information of current and former city employees, including payroll records, direct-deposit account numbers, and other internal data. The city reported the breach to the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the New York State Police and the U.S. Department of Homeland Security Incident Response Team, and retained external cybersecurity and legal advisors to assist in negotiations and recovery. The threat actor reportedly demanded €279,000 (US$300,000), but the city council approved a payment of €138,500 (US$150,000) in exchange for the return of the stolen data and decryption of affected systems. The investigation remains ongoing, with law-enforcement tracing the transaction and pursuing the ransomware perpetrators, believed to operate from Eastern Europe. The City of Gloversville disclosed the incident to the public on 25 October 2025.

name: Everest Ransomware Gang Breached Swedish Power Grid Operator Svenska kraftnät On 25 October 2025
description: Sweden’s power grid operator, Svenska kraftnät, confirmed a data breach after a ransomware group, known as Everest, claimed responsibility for the attack on 26 October 2025 and threatened to leak hundreds of gigabytes of purportedly stolen internal data the day before. The incident affected a limited external file transfer solution and did not disrupt Sweden’s power supply. Chief Information Security Officer Cem Göcgören informed the public about the incident, stating that the company took immediate action and was working closely with the police and national cybersecurity authorities to determine the extent of the breach. The ransomware gang claimed to have exfiltrated about 280 gigabytes of data and said it would publish it unless the agency complied with its demands. In a press release from 6 November, Svenska kraftnät acknowledged that a small portion of exfiltrated data contained classified information related to electrical systems and contact details.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached the Iranian Cyber Training Center Ravin Academy Around 22 October 2025
description: An unidentified actor breached Ravin Academy, a cyber training centre linked to Iran’s Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), exposing personal data of over 1,000 individuals enrolled in its technical programmes on or around 22 October 2025. The academy, established by MOIS affiliates Seyed Mojtaba Mostafavi and Farzin Karimi and sanctioned by the US in 2022, confirmed the breach in a Telegram post, stating that usernames and phone numbers from a student database were compromised. Ravin Academy described the incident as an attempt to undermine Iran’s security ahead of its National Tech Olympiad, while UK-based activist Nariman Gharib reported that the leaked dataset had been made searchable online.

name: Unknown Hackers Disrupted Systems of Texas Kaufman County in the US on 20 October 2025
description:
Unknown threat actors compromised systems of Kaufman County, TX in the United States on 20 October 2025. The attackers disabled multiple county systems including computers at the county courthouse. The incident, affecting a jurisdiction of nearly 200,000 residents, forced county officials to notify state and federal agencies while maintaining operation of the Sheriff's Office and emergency services.
County Judge Jakie Allen confirmed the incident on 20 October. The FBI and state agencies engaged in incident investigation and recovery operations.

name: China-linked Groups Glowworm aka FamousSparrow and Emissary Panda aka UNC5221 Exploited Patched ToolShell Vulnerability to Breach Middle Eastern Telecom Since July 2025
description:
China-linked groups Glowworm (also tracked as FamousSparrow and Earth Estries) and Emissary Panda (aka UNC5221) exploited the ToolShell SharePoint vulnerability (CVE-2025-53770) shortly after its patch release in July 2025 to compromise a telecommunications company in the Middle East. The incident began on 21 July 2025 and involved webshell deployment, DLL sideloading, and the use of custom and publicly available tools.
The threat actors installed backdoors including Zingdoor and ShadowPad, as well as the Rust-based KrustyLoader, to execute commands, exfiltrate data, and extract credentials. Broadcom’s Symantec Threat Hunter Team attributed the intrusion to China-based espionage activity and further noted overlaps with other campaigns exploiting the same flaw.

name: Unidentified Perpetrators targeted Food Shipment Systems of Russia’s Food Safety Agency Rosselkhoznadzor with DDoS Attack on 22 October 2025
description:
The Russian Federal Service for Veterinary and Phytosanitary Supervision (Rosselkhoznadzor) experienced a DDoS incident on 22 October 2025 that disrupted key information systems, including VetIS and Saturn, which are used to monitor the movement of agricultural products and chemicals. The disruption affected the Mercury electronic veterinary certification platform.
According to Russian trade outlet Shopper’s, two major dairy producers and a baby food manufacturer were unable to dispatch goods for several hours due to the system’s unavailability. Industry representatives reported financial losses caused by the lack of a contingency procedure allowing shipments without digital certification. The agency reported that normal operations resumed later the same day.

name: Hacking Group Cavalry Werewolf Launched A Sophisticated Campaign Targeting Public Sector And Key Industries In Russia Between May And August 2025
description: The hacking group Cavalry Werewolf (also tracked as YoroTrooper, Silent Lynx, and others) launched a campaign targeting Russia's public sector and key industries, including energy, mining, and manufacturing between May and August 2025. The campaign was made public by the Turkish cybersecurity firm Picus Security in a report published on 20 October 2025. Cavalry Werewolf's primary method of initial access involves spear-phishing emails disguised as official correspondence from legitimate Kyrgyz government officials, but there is evidence that they may have also used compromised real official email accounts for their operations. The phishing emails carry a RAR archive containing the custom malware, including the FoalShell reverse shell and the StallionRAT remote access trojan.

name: North Korea-Aligned Lazarus Targeted European Defence Companies Working On UAV Technology Since March 2025
description: The North Korea-aligned Lazarus group (also known as HIDDEN COBRA) targeted several European companies active in the defence industry, which some of them heavily involved in the unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) sector in an advanced fishing campaign since March 2025. On 23 October 2025, ESET published a report, where they confirmed the successful intrusion for at least three companies based on telemetry data: a metal engineering company in Southeastern Europe, a manufacturer of aircraft components as well as a defence company in Central Europe. Lazarus relies on the social-engineering technique used for initial access, trojanizing open-source projects from GitHub, and the deployment of ScoringMathTea. ESET assessed these attacks to be part of the Operation DreamJob campaign. The suspected primary goal of the threat actors was likely the theft of proprietary information and manufacturing know-how.

name: RansomHouse Group Attacked Japanese E-Commerce And Logistics Platform Askul With Ransomware On 19 October 2025
description: On 19 October 2025, Japanese e-commerce and logistics platform Askul announced that it had suspended ordering and shipping operations due to a ransomware attack on its website. System failure occurred due to the ransomware infection, forcing the company to cancel orders and suspend shipping operations, as well as other services. On 30 October, the ransomware group RansomHouse claimed to have exfiltrated and encrypted 1.1 terabytes of data from Askul. On 31 October, the company confirmed data theft affecting customer and product supplier data. This included company names, the names of the persons in charge, email addresses, registered phone numbers and details of enquiries.

name: Convict Aurel Z. Breached Prison Administration Systems of Romanian Târgu Jiu Penitentiary and Modified Sensitive Data Starting in August and October 2025
description:
A convict at Romania's Târgu Jiu Penitentiary compromised the national prisoner management platform between August and October 2025, gaining unauthorised access to sensitive data including sentences, visitation records and inmate rights. The breach originated at the Dej Prison Hospital, where an inmate exploited vulnerabilities in tablets and kiosks provided for prisoners to access the National Penitentiary Administration (ANP) system. By manipulating the kiosk interface, the perpetrator escaped the ANP application's sandbox and accessed a networked printer's login page, obtaining administrator credentials belonging to a former director of the Dej complex.
The attacker, identified as inmate Aurel Z., transferred the method to Târgu Jiu Prison after relocation in July. Using the stolen credentials, he granted inmates access to adult content, altered financial balances and modified sentence reduction records. He inflated balances in at least fifteen accounts, including one exceeding €1.15 million, and spent approximately €2,300 monthly. The breach involved over 300 hours of undetected administrative access and affected facilities in Timișoara and Pelendava. CEC Bank then discovered the irregularities through transaction discrepancies.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Conducted Spearphishing Campaign Against Ukraine War Relief Groups In Ukraine in October 2025
description:
A coordinated spearphishing campaign, dubbed *PhantomCaptcha*, targeted Ukrainian war relief organisations, including the International Committee of the Red Cross, UNICEF and regional administrations in October 2025. The threat actors impersonated the Ukrainian President’s Office in emails containing weaponised PDF attachments that redirected victims to a fake Cloudflare CAPTCHA page, prompting the execution of a three-stage PowerShell payload.
The payload deployed a WebSocket-based remote access trojan (RAT) capable of executing arbitrary commands, exfiltrating data and delivering additional malware. The campaign used a multistage attack chain designed to exploit user trust and evade conventional security measures. SentinelOne researchers uncovered the operation on 8 October and publicly reported it on 22 October 2025. The campaign was later linked to a related operation distributing spyware through adult-themed Android APKs and fake cloud storage tools targeting users in Lviv, Ukraine.

name: Unidentified Threat Actors Exploited Reward Logic of DeFi Protocol Betterbank In United States Between 26-27 August 2025
description:
An unidentified actor exploited a flaw in BetterBank’s reward logic between 26 and 27 August 2025, using a counterfeit liquidity pool to generate unlimited value due to insufficient validation. BetterBank, a decentralised finance (DeFi) protocol operating on the PulseChain network, lost approximately $5 million in digital assets, of which around $2.7 million was later returned following on-chain negotiations, leaving a net loss of roughly $1.4 million.
The vulnerability had been identified in a prior Zokyo security audit just a month prior but was downgraded in severity and left unpatched, which enabled the compromise.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Hacking Group Salt Typhoon Infiltrated European Telecom In Europe In July 2025
description: Since July 2025, Chinese state-sponsored hacking group Salt Typhoon infiltrated a European telecommunications organization. Darktrace disclosed in a report published on 20 October 2025 that the activities involved advanced methods such as DLL sideloading to infiltrate systems while avoiding standard detection measures. Darktrace attributes the observed activities with medium confidence to Salt Typhoon. The intrusion likely began in July 2025 by exploiting a Citrix NetScaler Gateway vulnerability (CVE-2025-5777), before pivoting to Citrix Virtual Delivery Agent hosts. The hackers used living-off-the-land techniques, deploying native tools, and uploaded antivirus executable files to execute payloads via DLL sideloading. A backdoor was inserted into the telecom infrastructure, using virtual private servers for command and control over HTTP and an unidentified TCP-based control.

name: Unknown Nation-State Threat Actors Breached F5 In The United States On 9 August 2025
description: On 9 August 2025, F5 was breached by a nation-state threat actor who gained long-term access to its internal systems, stealing valuable technical data, including parts of its BIG-IP source code, internal vulnerability research, and configuration details for some customers. The UK's National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) confirmed the incident compromised parts of F5's internal network and involved advanced, persistent techniques consistent with nation-state operations. The breach was detected in August 2025, and F5 engaged external experts, including CrowdStrike, Mandiant, NCC Group, and IOActive, to assist with the investigation. The company found no evidence that its software build systems or update mechanisms were tampered with, and normal operations remained unaffected. Government agencies and enterprise customers using F5's BIG-IP and related products were urged to apply all recent patches and review access controls. The US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) warned that hackers were using F5 devices to target government networks, presenting an imminent threat to federal networks using F5 products. CISA's Executive Assistant Director for Cybersecurity Nick Andersen encouraged others to identify F5's devices on their network and apply urgent updates, noting that the risk of this vulnerability extends to every organization and sector that's using this product. British authorities also issued an alert urging F5 users to update their software. The incident was publicly disclosed by F5 on 15 October 2025, and reported by various sources, including the US Securities and Exchange Commission, the NCSC, and CISA. Some media reports attributed the attack to the Chinese state-sponsored actor UNC5221 (aka Emissary Panda/APT27).

name: Unknown Threat Actors Stole Firewall Configuration Backups from SonicWALL’s Cloud Service In United States In September 2025
description:
In September 2025, unknown threat actors stole firewall configuration backups from SonicWall’s cloud service, impacting all users of its MySonicWall cloud backup platform. The stolen data contains configuration data and credentials, albeit the latter are protected by strong encryption. The company notified all impacted customers, released tools to assist with threat detection and remediation, and encouraged customers to check for potential exposure. The incident was made public by SonicWALL on 17 September 2025. Mandiant aided in the investigation, which confirmed that an unauthorized party accessed firewall configuration backup files for all customers who used SonicWall's cloud backup service. In a report published on 10 October 2025, Huntress observed widespread compromise of SonicWall SSLVPN devices across multiple customer environments since 4 October. Over 100 SonicWall SSLVPN accounts across 16 customer accounts have been impacted and, in some cases, there was evidence of post-exploitation activity, with the actors conducting network scanning activity and attempting to access numerous local Windows accounts.
On 4 November, the company published a statement that the investigation conducted by Mandiant has been completed and revealed that the malicious activity was carried out by a state-sponsored threat actor and could be isolated to the unauthorized access of cloud backup files from a specific cloud environment using an API call.

name: CL0P Ransomware Gang Conducted An Extortion Campaign By Exploiting A Zero-day Vulnerability In Oracle's E-Business Suite And Demanded Ransom Of Numerous Organisations Between July And October 2025
description:
The CL0P ransomware group initiated an extortion campaign against Oracle’s E-Business Suite (EBS) on 11 July 2025. From 9 August, the threat actor employed CVE-2025-61882 as a zero-day vulnerability to target Oracle EBS customers and successfully exfiltrated significant volumes of data from some organisations, according to a joint report by Google Threat Intelligence Group and Mandiant. On 29 September, CL0P launched a high-volume extortion email campaign directed at affected entities. Oracle subsequently confirmed exploitation of vulnerabilities in its EBS platform and released an emergency patch on 4 October.
Confirmed victims include the University of Phoenix (United States), Cox Enterprises (a major US communications and automotive services conglomerate), The Washington Post (US news organisation), Allianz UK (subsidiary of German insurer Allianz), Barts Health NHS (one of England’s largest hospital trusts), Envoy Air (a US regional carrier owned by American Airlines), Harvard University (United States), Wits University (South Africa),  Logitech (Swiss multinational manufacturer of computer peripherals) and LKQ Corporation (American automotive parts manufacturer).
CL0P has named additional organisations on its leak site, but many alleged victims have not publicly confirmed whether they were affected.

name: Cybercrime Gang Storm-2657 Hijacked Salary Payments Of University Employees Using Sophisticated Social Engineering Tactics In United States Since March 2025 On 9 October 2025
description: Since March 2025, a cybercrime gang tracked by Microsoft as Storm-2657 targeted a range of US-based organizations, particularly employees in sectors like higher education, to gain access to third-party human resources (HR) software as a service (SaaS) platforms like Workday since March 2025. The attackers compromised Workday accounts and other third-party human resources SaaS platforms by using sophisticated social engineering tactics and exploiting the lack of multifactor authentication (MFA) according to a report published by Microsoft Threat Intelligence on 9 October 2025. They found that 11 accounts at three universities were successfully compromised, and then used to send phishing emails to nearly 6,000 email accounts across 25 universities. The criminals used custom-tailored themes in phishing emails, including warnings of illnesses or outbreaks on campus, suggesting that recipients might have been exposed, to trick recipients into clicking phishing links. After gaining access to compromised employee accounts, the threat actor created inbox rules to delete incoming warning notification emails from Workday, hiding the actor’s changes to the HR profiles. Storm-2657 then stealthily moved on to modify the employee’s salary payment configuration in their HR profile, thereby redirecting future salary payments to accounts under the actor’s control, causing financial harm to their victims. Microsoft identified affected customers and reached out to assist with mitigation efforts, sharing guidance for investigating these attacks and implementing phishing-resistant MFA to help block them.

name: Unattributed Hackers Leveraged Malware To Steal Banking Information From Brazilian Government And Businesses In Brazil in 2025
description: Unidentified hackers leveraged WhatsApp to target Brazilian government institutions and businesses with a new malware strain called SORVEPOTEL. The malware spread through phishing messages containing a zip file disguised as a legitimate document and was designed to deliver additional payloads collecting banking information. The infection began when users received a WhatsApp message from a compromised contact, after which the malware hijacked their WhatsApp web session and automatically sent the same file to all contacts and group chats. Trend Micro reported the incident on 03 October 2025, identifying 477 infections, 457 of which occurred in Brazil, mainly across government, public service, manufacturing, education, banking, technology and construction sectors.

name: Crimson Collective Hacking Group Stole Sensitive Customer Information From Red Hat In The United States In Mid-September 2025
description: In mid-September 2025, the Crimson Collective hacking group breached Red Hat's GitLab instance, stealing approximately 570GB of compressed data across 28,000 internal development repositories, including around 800 Customer Engagement Reports (CERs) containing sensitive customer information. The threat actors attempted to extort Red Hat into paying a ransom but received no response. Later, the Crimson Collective partnered with Scattered Lapsus Hunters and ShinyHunters to continue extortion attempts against Red Hat. They released samples of stolen CERs for companies such as Walmart, HSBC, Bank of Canada, Atos Group, American Express, Department of Defence, and Société Française du Radiotéléphone. ShinyHunters announced that they would publicly leak the data on 10 October 2025 if a ransom demand was not negotiated. BleepingComputer reported the incident, and Red Hat confirmed the breach.

name: Russian State-Sponsored Hackers Infiltrated Servers Of Central Electoral Commission In Moldova On 24 September 2025
description: On 24 September 2025, Moldova's Central Electoral Commission was breached by Russian state-sponsored hackers, according to Moldova’s Deputy Prime Minister Doina Nistor. The attack occurred just days ahead of a parliamentary election and was part of a wider hybrid campaign by Russia to destabilize Moldova's democracy. The police chief, Viorel Cernauteanu, informed local media about the attack on 25 September 2025. Doina Nistor reported that the vulnerability had been identified and fixed, securing the electoral commission. The attack occurred just days ahead of a parliamentary election and is believed to be a part of a wider hybrid campaign by Russia to destabilize Moldova's democracy.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Stole Sensitive Information From Union County In The United States On 18 May 2025
description: On 18 May 2025, a ransomware attack occurred on Union County in Ohio, impacting over 45,000 residents and employees. The attackers stole information, including Social Security numbers, financial information, driver’s license numbers, fingerprint data, medical information, and passport numbers. The county government detected the ransomware on their network and subsequently hired cybersecurity experts and notified federal law enforcement agencies. The incident was first disclosed publicly via Union County’s data breach notification on 24 September 2025.

name: Deface Peru Cyberactivist Group targeted El Peruano Newspaper In Peru On 28 September 2025
description: On 28 September 2025, a group of hacktivists known as Deface Peru targeted the website of El Peruano, the official newspaper of Peru. The group replaced content with criticism of state authorities. El Peruano regained control of the website after two hours. Deface Peru claimed responsibility for the incident through their Telegram account.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Canadian Investment Regulatory Organization (CIRO) And Stole Personal Data from Investment Advisors On 11 September 2025
description: On 11 September 2025, the Canadian Investment Regulatory Organization (CIRO) discovered that unknown treat actors had comprised internal systems. CIRO proactively shut down some of its systems and immediately started an investigation. Throughout this time, critical functions remained available. The investigation found that registration information for member firms and registered individuals had been exposed, including names, addresses, personal financial information required at the time of registration as well as notes from regulatory investigations, and civil or criminal disclosures. In September 2025, media reported that the data from the hack is circulating on the dark web.

name: Ransomware Group Qilin Targeted Asahi Group Holdings In Japan On 29 September 2025
description: On 29 September 2025, the Japanese brewing giant Asahi Group Holdings was breached by the Qilin ransomware group, which caused system failures affecting their shipping and customer service operations in Japan. The company stopped taking orders and suspended call centre operations due to disrupted access to corporate systems. The incident resulted in the temporary suspension of production at 30 factories throughout Japan. On 7 October 2025, Qilin claimed to have exfiltrated financial documents, budgets, contracts, employee personal data, and development plans. Later, the company confirmed that personal data of 1.5 million users belonging to customers, employees and former collaborators are affected.

name: Qilin Ransomware Group Breached US Healthcare Company ApolloMD Between 22 May 2025 And 23 May 2025
description:
On 12 June 2025, the ransomware group Qilin added ApolloMD to their darkweb leak site, claiming to hold 238 GB of files, which were allegedly obtained during a compromise that occurred between 22 May and 23 May 2025. ApolloMD, a business associate to hospitals and health systems, notified patients of 11 physician practices affected by the incident, including Passaic Hospitalist Services LLC, Pensacola Hospitalist Physicians LLC, Broad River Physicians Group LLC, Olive Branch Emergency Physicians LLC, Aurora Emergency Physicians LLC, Passaic River Physicians LLC, The Bortolazzo Group LLC, Methodist University Emergency Physicians PLLC, Trinity Emergency Physicians LLC, Lorain Emergency Physicians LLC, and Pennsylvania Hospitalist Group LLC. The affected data included patient names, dates of birth, addresses, diagnosis information, provider names, dates of service, treatment information, and/or health insurance information, as well as Social Security numbers for some individuals. ApolloMD posted a substitute notice on its website on 15 September 2025 and sent notification letters to affected patients on 17 September 2025.
In February 2026, ApolloMD confirmed that over 626,000 individuals were impacted.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Ministry Of Health In Panama Between 19 And 21 September 2025
description: The Ministry of Health (MINSA) was targeted between 19 and 21 September 2025 in a breach of the Monitoring and Evaluation System of the National Health Plan (SIMEPLANS), which contained public documentation such as regulations, policies and annual planning. The incident did not compromise systems containing patient personal data or health care platforms. MINSA's IT office detected unauthorized access and migrated the system to a new server environment. MINSA confirmed the incident on 25 September 2025 and announced that it would file a formal complaint with the Public Prosecutors Office to initiate investigations.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Encrypted Data And Systems Of Communal Housing Company In Germany On 08 September 2025
description: On 8 September 2025, a ransomware attack was carried out by an unknown treat actor on the municipal housing company in Senftenberg, in the German state of Brandenburg. The threat actor used malware to encrypt data and systems, demanding a ransom for their release. As a result, the IT network was deactivated, and email and telephone communication were restricted. The Landeskriminalamt (state criminal police) investigated the attack due to suspicion of extortion and computer sabotage. According to the housing company's statement, no contact was made with the attackers. Personal data is believed to have been compromised.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Threat Actor UNC5221 Deployed BRICKSTORM Malware in a Campaign Against Tech and Legal Sector in the United States since March 2025
description:
Chinese State-Sponsored Threat Group UNC5221 deployed BRICKSTORM malware in a campaign, which is being used to maintain persistent access to victim organizations in the United States. The campaign started at least in March 2025 and was discovered by Mandiant and published in a report on 24 September 2025. The hackers targeted specialized operating systems like Linux and BSD, focusing on stealing valuable intellectual property and sensitive information related to national security and international trade from legal services, technology, SaaS, and Business Process Outsourcers (BPOs) sectors. In some cases, the attackers maintained access for over a year, exceeding log retention periods, complicating the analysis of initial access vectors.
On 4 December 2025,  the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), National Security Agency (NSA) and Canadian Centre for Cyber Security published an advisory outlining the BRICKSTORM malware based off an analysis of eight samples taken from victim organizations and they are attributing the malware to People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber actors.

name: Unknown Hackers Compromised Spanish Gas Distributor Madrileña Red de Gas On 12 September 2025
description:
Madrid-based gas distributor Madrileña Red de Gas suffered an intrusion that exposed customer identification data and Universal Supply Point Codes (CUPS), the unique reference numbers for individual gas meters on 12 September 2025. 
Madrileña Red de Gas, which manages over one million supply points across the Community of Madrid, activated internal security protocols and launched a system-wide review. The incident was reported to the Spanish Data Protection Agency (AEPD) and the National Cybersecurity Institute (INCIBE). Affected customers were notified beginning 20 September. The company confirmed that financial details and access credentials were not compromised. Public disclosure followed on 22 September 2025.

name: North Korean State-Affiliated Hacking Group Famous Chollima Delivered Malware to Cryptocurrency and Retail Sector Organisations in Multiple Countries in Late May 2025
description: Threat actors with ties to the North Korean threat actor Famous Chollima (aka Contagious Interview) were observed using ClickFix-style lures to deliver BeaverTail and InvisibleFerret malware in a campaign tracked as 'Contagious Interview'. Famous Chollima is suspected to operate as a subset of the umbrella group Lazarus. The latest attack wave was observed in late May 2025. The threat actors targeted marketing and trader roles in cryptocurrency and retail sector organisations. This activity was reported by GitLab Threat Intelligence researcher Oliver Smith on 17 September 2025. On 16 October 2025, Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) published additional details about coinciding Contagious Interview activity observed since February 2025. The TTPs documented for the infection chain by GTIG shares overlaps with the GitLab Threat Intelligence report, including the use of ClickFix techniques to compromise victim machines and the subsequent deployment of the BeaverTail stealer and InvisibleFerret backdoor. In addition, UNC5342, the threat actor GTIG associates with the Contagious Interview campaign, leveraged smart contracts to deposit malware on public blockchains, such as BNB Smart Chain and Ethereum. Known as EtherHiding, this technique takes advantage of the immutability of blockchains as a repository for malware and command and control infrastructure that is resilient to law enforcement takedowns. UNC5342 activity is the first time a nation-state actor has been detected to use this method. In a report published by NVISO Labs on 13 November 2025, identified a new technique for malware delivery leveraging legitimate JSON storage services like JSON Keeper, JSONsilo, and npoint.io.

name: INC Ransom Breached Appalachian School Services Centre and Exfiltrated Internal Information in Canada on 25 August 2025
description:
On 25 August 2025, an intrusion was detected at the Appalachian School Services Centre (CSSA), which paralysed its activities. The incident forced the organization to shut down its digital communications, including IP phones, computers, and Wi-Fi network, to limit the impact after a ransomware attack. Executive Director Jean Roberge informed the public about the attack through an interview with the Courier Frontenac on 27 August 2025. An investigation was underway, led by cybersecurity specialists, in collaboration with the Ministry of Cybersecurity and Digital Affairs, the Operational Centre for Cyberdefense (COCD), Prcicom, and the School Board's Computer Network Management Company. 
On 3 September 2025, INC Ransom claimed responsibility for the attack on their dark net forum. On 10 September, personal information belonging to members of the school community was posted on the dark web.

name: Sberislam Hacker Group Disrupted Milliyet and Hurriyet Websites in Turkey on 23 September 2025
description: On 23 September 2025, the hacker group SBERISLAM carried out a DDoS attack on the websites of the major Turkish daily newspapers Milliyet and Hurriyet, resulting in short access problems for users. SBERISLAM claimed responsibility for the incident, stating that the attack was in response to the perceived persecution of Muslim people and the alleged bias of press organizations. The group posted a statement on Milliyet's website, which included photos of Sheikh Said, a leader of a Kurdish nationalist and Islamist rebellion against the early Turkish Republic, and Abu Ubeyde, the pseudonym of the spokesperson of the al-Qassam Brigades, the military wing of Hamas, who had been killed in an airstrike on Gaza City a month earlier. The incident was reported by Milliyet on its website.

name: Iranian State-Sponsored Hacking Group Nimbus Manticore Breached Defence, Telecommunications, and Aerospace Companies in Europe since Early 2025
description: In a campaign originating in early 2025, Iranian hackers known as Nimbus Manticore breached major companies across Europe, targeting businesses in the defence, telecommunications, and aerospace sectors to steal sensitive information. The intrusions started with a fake email invitation to a job application, directing victims to a fraudulent website mimicking well-known companies like Boeing, Airbus, and flydubai. Once logged in, victims were tricked into downloading a malicious file, a compressed ZIP archive containing a legitimate looking program that secretly installed and ran other malicious files, including a backdoor. This was done to take control of the system and communicate with the attackers' servers. The downloaded file contained an evolved variant of the older malware called Minibike, also known as SlugResin, and new variants such as MiniJunk and MiniBrowse, designed to steal important data while avoiding detection. Check Point Research (CPR) reported technical details of the activity on 22 September 2025.

name: Everest Ransomware Group Breached Collins Aerospace's Muse Software On 10 September 2025, Widespread Disruption at European Airports on 19 September 2025
description:
On 10 September 2025, the Everest ransomware group breached Muse software of Collins Aerospace, an American defence and aviation system manufacturer which provides software services to many airports worldwide, and claimed to have exfiltrated 1,533,900 passenger records and other sensitive data from an insecure FTP server. On the night of 19 September 2025, the systems of multiple European major airports were disrupted. The attack was described as a “cyber-related disturbance” by Raytheon Technologies/RTX, the parent company of Collins Aerospace, which confirmed that it was working to resolve the incident as soon as possible. The incident targeted the aforementioned Muse software, which is used in passenger control (i.e., check-in) and baggage systems at several major European airports, including Brussels Airport, Berlin Airport, Dublin Airport, and Heathrow Airport. As a result, automated systems were completely disabled, forcing airports to conduct all operations manually by implementing emergency plans. This caused significant disruptions, including major setbacks in flight programs and extended passenger lines. The incident was reported by various sources, including the airports themselves, Collins Aerospace, and news agencies such as AFP and BBC. According to Brussels Airport, at least 10 flights were cancelled and 17 were delayed for more than an hour. London Heathrow Airport also reported delays, with over 140 flight delays until noon. Berlin Airport reported 62 delays, while other airports, such as Frankfurt and Zurich, were not affected. As of 22 September 2025, disruptions were still ongoing at certain European airports. On the same day, the EU's cybersecurity agency, ENISA, confirmed that the incident was a ransomware attack and stated that cybersecurity experts had identified the type of ransomware, without disclosing it to the public but stating to Deutsche Welle and Reuters that law enforcement was investigating.
On 24 September 2025, the British National Crime Agency detained a 40-years-old man suspected to be involved in the ransomware attack. 
While Everest claimed responsibility for targeting Collins Aerospace through their lake site on 17 October 2025, the group further specified its actions on 22 October. In the subsequent statement, Everest admitted to have exfiltrated data on 10 September but denied deploying ransomware or engaging in activities that could have resulted in the disruptions observed from 19 September onwards. To back up this timeline, the group shared screenshots allegedly showing conversations between Everest and representatives of RTX, Collins Aerospace's parent company. Initial statements by ENISA and the UK National Cyber Security Centre had noted that malware used in the intrusion had been identified, suggesting that ransomware may have been deployed, possibly by a separate actor in a colliding intrusion. The security company Hudson Rock noted that the credentials, which Everest claimed to have used for the intrusion, could be traced back to an infostealer infection of a Collins Aerospace employee in 2022.

name: Russian State-Sponsored Hacking Groups Gamaredon and Turla Are Collaborating to Deploy Kazuar Backdoor In Ukrainian Computer Systems in an Ongoing Espionage Campaign since at Least January 2025
description: Russian state-sponsored hacking groups Gamaredon and Turla have been observed by ESET researchers to be collaborating to deploy the Kazuar backdoor against targets in Ukraine in what is described as an ongoing collaboration. According to ESET's report, published on 19 September 2025, Turla has compromised at least seven Ukrainian systems (of unnamed victims/sectors) since March 2024, while Gamaredon and Turla's collaboration in this ongoing campaign began in January 2025 with Gamaredon's compromise of four systems. While the exact method of infiltration is unknown, ESET describes in its report how several tools believed to be exclusive to Gamaredon, including PteroGraphin and PteroOdd, were used to execute Turla's Kazuar v3 backdoor on an endpoint in Ukraine in February 2025. The attack chain involved Gamaredon deploying PteroGraphin, which downloaded a PowerShell downloader dubbed PteroOdd, retrieving a payload from Telegraph to execute Kazuar. In separate instances in April and June 2025, ESET detected the deployment of Kazuar v2 through other Gamaredon malware families tracked as PteroOdd and PteroPaste. The attackers used the Kazuar backdoor to gather and exfiltrate the victim's computer name and system drives volume serial number to a Cloudflare Workers subdomain.

name: Warlock Group/Storm-2603 spreads Warlock Ransomware to Various Organisations In Europe and Americas in Ongoing Campaign since March 2025
description: Since March 2025, a hacker group known as Warlock Group (aka GOLD SALEM, Storm-2603) has been observed by threat researchers to be operating an ongoing campaign utilizing ransomware to target nearly 20 organizations across multiple countries. According to Sophos, which first reported the campaign, victims include anywhere between "small commercial or government entities to large multinational corporations spread throughout North America, Europe, and South America." The group's origins and location are subject to further analysis, with Microsoft stating with moderate confidence that it is a China-based threat actor. Sophos noted that further evidence was necessary to corroborate information about the origin of the group. From 18 July 2025 onwards, Microsoft observed Warlock deploying ransomware, taking advantage of the SharePoint exploit chain ToolShell, initial exploitation of which had been linked to China nexus actors. Warlock's targets included at least one organization in Russia which provides engineering services and equipment to the electricity generation industry, suggesting that the group operates from outside of Russia.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Cyber Espionage Group APT41 Impersonated US Lawmaker and Stole Sensitive Data from the US Government and Academic Organisations in the United States between July and August 2025
description: Between July and August 2025, China-linked APT41, also known as TA415, conducted spearphishing campaigns targeting United States government, think tank, and academic organizations focused on US-China economic relations. The group impersonated US Representative John Moolenaar, Chair of the Select Committee on Strategic Competition with China, and the US-China Business Council to deliver infection chains that established Visual Studio (VS) Code Remote Tunnels, enabling persistent remote access without conventional malware. The attackers used legitimate services for command and control, including Google Sheets and Calendar, as well as cloud sharing services like Dropbox and OpenDrive. The phishing emails contained links to password-protected archives, which, when opened, executed a batch script that ran the WhirlCoil Python loader, ultimately establishing a VS Code remote tunnel authenticated via GitHub. The attackers collected system information and user directory contents, sending this data to free request logging services.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Using "EvilAI" Malware to Target Multiple Sectors in US, India, the EU, the UK, and other Countries in Ongoing Campaign since at least 29 August 2025
description: Unknown threat actors have been observed by Trend Micro researchers to be using legitimate-looking AI tools and software to sneak malware into organizations worldwide, with Trend Micro saying that their telemetry first collected data on 29 August 2025 but that the campaign is ongoing and rapidly spreading. The campaign has been observed targeting hundreds of victims across manufacturing, government, healthcare, and other sectors in the US, India, the UK, the EU, Brazil, and beyond. The attackers used seemingly legitimate productivity and AI-enhanced apps with names like App Suite, Epi Browser, JustAskJacky, Manual Finder, and Tampered Chef to conceal malware. Trend Micro is tracking related malware, which is presented as functioning apps featuring professionally crafted user interfaces and real, working features, as EvilAI. The malware code itself appeared to have been AI-generated and contained little to attract the attention of antivirus and threat detection tools. The operators of EvilAI chose to conceal their malware in new apps with innocuous and generic-sounding titles to try and appear authentic. By combining believable functionality with stealthy payload delivery, AI was reviving classic threats like Trojans and giving them new evasion capabilities against modern antivirus defences. Eric Skinner, Trend Micro's VP of market strategy, commented that the malicious apps were likely being used to set the stage for future exploit activity.

name: Unknown Cybercriminals Targeted Municipal Election Result Servers In North Rhine-Westphalia (Germany) with DDoS On 14 September 2025
description: On the evening of 14 September 2025, cybercriminals launched an attack meant to overload the server of the state of North Rhine-Westphalia used for presenting municipal election results. The targeted attack was quickly and successfully prevented by IT.NRW (the State Statistical Office), with the presentation of election results being disrupted for only a few minutes between 6 p.m. and 6:15 p.m. The state criminal office was informed, and an investigation was initiated. According to a spokesman for IT.NRW, the communication, recording, and evaluation of results were not affected, and no one had penetrated the system. The Rheinische Post reported that there were numerous server requests per second, aiming to break down the system entirely, but this attempt was unsuccessful, as it was only a short-term disruption to the presentation.

name: KillSec Ransomware Group Compromised Several Healthcare Institutions in the Americas in September 2025
description: Killsec Ransomware Group has been observed targeting the healthcare sector in the Americas by Resecurity Threat Intelligence in a report published on 10 September 2025. In the report, Resecurity describes how, on 8 September 2025, the KillSec Ransomware group claimed responsibility for a compromise of MedicSolution, a software solutions provider for the healthcare industry in Brazil. The attackers compromised an insecure AWS S3 bucket, resulting in data exfiltration, which is believed to have occurred over several months. The stolen data, exceeding 34 GB and comprising over 94,818 files, included sensitive laboratory results reports, medical assessments, X-rays, unredacted patient pictures, and records related to minors. Resecurity reported the incident and identified several affected patients, who were unaware of the breach at the time of disclosure. The KillSec Ransomware group threatened to leak the sensitive data unless negotiations were initiated promptly, with the intention of extorting MedicSolution. Aside from the attack on MedicSolution, days prior, the group had also announced the successful compromise of several other healthcare institutions in the Americas: namely Archer Health (USA), Suiza Lab (Peru), GoTelemedicina (Colombia), and eMedicoERP (Colombia), though the exact tactics used by the group in these compromises, nor the impacts, were not elaborated by Resecurity's report.

name: Ukrainian Military Intelligence Agency (HUR) Carried Out DDoS Attack On Russian Central Election Commission And Other Government Services In Russia On 14 September 2025
description: Ukraine’s military intelligence agency (HUR) carried out a DDoS attack on Russia’s Central Election Commission and other government services in response to voting in occupied Ukrainian regions on 14 September 2025. The operation coincided with Russia’s “unified voting day,” when regional and local elections are held simultaneously across the country. HUR said its DDoS attack targeted the servers of the CEC, Russia’s electronic voting system, the state services portal Gosuslugi and core routers of state-run telecoms provider Rostelecom. Russia confirmed its election-related websites had come under sustained attack. CEC chief Ella Pamfilova told local state media that since the start of the voting on Friday, CEC website was intermittently unavailable but stressed that voting itself had not been affected. Deputy Digital Development Minister Oleg Kachanov confirmed “short-term traffic degradation” on Gosuslugi and the CEC’s digital services but said the remote voting platform functioned as designed.

According to Uvalde CISD's Chief of Communications, Anne Marie Espinoza, the affected systems are "crucial to the safety and security of our schools," hence the closure; meanwhile, incident was reported to the FBI, which initiated a "comprehensive investigation" to uncover the source of the malware and assess whether any sensitive information has been compromised; the investigation was initiated alongside the school district's insurance cybersecurity team and other relevant agencies. Following this incident, on 14 September 2025, South Korea's ICT minister, Bae Kyunghoon, urged a cybersecurity overhaul after the breach at KT Corp. exposed major gaps in handling cyber incidents; in the incident, International Mobile Subscriber Identity (IMSI) information was stolen through an illegal "miniature base station," which enabled the hackers to charge unauthorised micropayments to nearly 280 KT subscribers, worth over 170 million won (US$122,400). name: Iran State-Sponsored Hacker Group Handala Hack Team Hacked And Leaked Personal Information Of Five "Iran International" Journalists Between 8 July and August 2025
description: Starting on 8 July 2025, the Iranian state-sponsored hacker group Handala Hack Team carried out a hack-and-leak operation targeting five journalists with Iran International, including one based in Canada, through accessing their internal communication and server infrastructure. As part of the operation, the group released uncensored photos of government IDs, email address passwords, intimate photos, and videos on their website, which were then amplified via X, Facebook, Instagram, Telegram, and Iranian news websites until early August 2025, according to Canadian officials. The operation was detected by Rapid Response Mechanism Canada (RRM Canada) on 9 July 2025 and was reported in an advisory on 12 September 2025. According to Canadian officials, Handala claimed to have acquired information of thousands of individuals linked to Iran International. The leaked information was further amplified through multiple AI chatbots, including ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot, Claude, Grok, and DeepSeek, which provided detailed information about the operation, names of affected individuals, and links to released images. The leak increased the risk to the personal safety of the affected staff, and such operations are used as a form of digital transnational repression (DTNR) to coerce, harass, silence, and intimidate those who speak against foreign actors or their interests. Several threat intelligence firms, including Recorded Future and Trellix, assess Handala maintains links with groups, such as Storm-842, with documented connections to Iranian intelligence.

name: Russian State Actor APT28 Deploys NotDoor Backdoor to Target Outlook Users in NATO Countries in 2025
description:
On 4 September 2025, threat researchers at Lab52 (S2 Grupo) reported that Russian state-sponsored group Fancy Bear (also known as APT28, Forest Blizzard, Sednit and others) deployed a custom backdoor called NotDoor by exploiting Microsoft Outlook.
The attackers used a Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) macro that scanned incoming emails for specific trigger phrases such as "Daily Report" to activate hidden functionality. They abused Microsoft's signed OneDrive.exe through a DLL sideloading technique, loading a malicious SSPICLI.dll that disabled Outlook's macro security.
The malware copied itself into Outlook’s macro project folder using encoded PowerShell commands, verified successful infection via DNS queries to webhook.site and established persistence through Windows registry changes. It supported remote command execution, file exfiltration, payload delivery and encrypted data exfiltration via Outlook.

name: Interlock Ransomware Group Breached School District Five of Lexington and Richland Counties in the United States on 3 June 2025
description:
The School District Five of Lexington and Richland Counties in South Carolina suffered a cyberattack that compromised the personal data of approximately 31,475 individuals on 3 June 2025. Exposed information included names, dates of birth, Social Security numbers, financial account details and state-issued ID numbers.
The breach occurred when an external actor gained unauthorized access to the district’s network and exfiltrated files. The incident was disclosed to the Maine Attorney General on 29 August 2025. Local media outlet WISTV reported on 5 June that staff bonuses had been delayed as a result of the disruption.
Superintendent Akil E. Ross Sr. notified affected individuals and offered complimentary credit monitoring and identity theft protection. The Interlock ransomware group claimed responsibility for the attack on 24 June.
