name: Unknown Ransomware Group Attacked City Of Greenville, NC In United States On 5 August 2025
description: On 5 August 2025, an unknown ransomware group attacked the city of Greenville's servers, affecting access to police and other records. The city immediately implemented protective measures, isolated affected systems, contacted law enforcement and engaged a third-party cybersecurity firm to mitigate the event and restore services. The attack did not affect the city's emergency 911 service, but some phone lines experienced intermittent outages or busy signals. The Greenville Electric Utility System (GEUS) was impacted and online payments and access to billing information were unavailable. There was no evidence that employee or resident information was affected by the breach. The city filed a catastrophe notice with the Texas Attorney General's office and stated that some systems will not be restored as early as 26 August 2025.

name: Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters Allegedly Breached British Automobile Manufacturer Jaguar Land Rover's Server on 31 August 2025
description:
The British automobile manufacturer Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) suffered a major cyberattack that disrupted production at its Halewood and Solihull plants and affected global IT systems on 31 August 2025. Staff were sent home and retail operations were impacted for weeks during a key delivery period.
While JLR referred to an “IT security incident,” a Tata Motors filing confirmed global issues. The newly formed hacking group Scatteres Lapsus$ Hunters claimed responsibility for the attack in early September 2025. The production was impacted until October 2025 and the losses due to the weeks-long forced production stoppage are estimated at almost 2.2 billion euros. In late June 2026, the New York Times reported that anonymous officials suspect Russian hackers responsible for the incident, due to differences in methods and motivation.

description:
The administration for the French greater Poitiers metropolitan area, comprising 40 municipalities, suffered a cyberattack on 29 August 2025, resulting in a computer outage that disrupted several online public services. The incident impacted the City of Poitiers, the Community Social Action Center (CCAS) and the wider Grand Poitiers region.
In response, authorities isolated community networks from external communications and mobilized a third-party service provider to investigate the origin and scope of the attack. While the exact nature of the intrusion has not been disclosed, the city confirmed the situation was stabilized by early September.
The attack rendered planning applications and library reservations inaccessible online. However, services related to passports, identity cards and civil status were later restored.

name: Iranian-Aligned Homeland Justice Group Infiltrated the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Oman in August 2025
description: In August 2025, Iranian-aligned operators connected to the Homeland Justice persona, associated with the MOIS (Ministry of Intelligence and Security of Iran), compromised a mailbox of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MFA) of Oman. The attackers used this breach to conduct a spear-phishing campaign targeting global governments. The attackers sent emails to multiple government recipients worldwide, disguising legitimate diplomatic communication, with malicious Microsoft Word attachments containing encoded content that was decoded using embedded VBA macro code to deploy malware. The campaign, which was identified by Dream Security's threat intelligence agents, targeted embassies, consulates, and international organizations across multiple regions, including the Middle East, Africa, Europe, Asia, and the Americas. A total of 270 emails were sent, with 104 unique compromised addresses used to mask the true origin of the activity. The attackers exploited the common practice of enabling macros to access content, and the campaign's infrastructure included a compromised Oman MFA mailbox, a NordVPN exit node in Jordan, and C2 infrastructure with the domain screenai.online. The inceident was reported by ClearskySec on X and was analysed by Dream Security, which attributed the campaign to Iranian-aligned operators.

description: WhatsApp patched a zero-click security vulnerability (CVE-2025-55177) in its iOS and macOS messaging clients on 29 August 2025, which was exploited in a targeted zero-day campaign against specific users between May and August 2025. The attackers used this flaw, in combination with an OS-level vulnerability on Apple platforms (CVE-2025-43300), to trigger processing of content from an arbitrary URL on a target's device. WhatsApp advised potentially impacted individuals to perform a device factory reset and keep their devices' operating system and software up to date.

name: UNC6395 Targeted Customers of the Sales Engagement Company Salesloft through Compromised OAuth Tokens in August 2025
description:
Between 8 and 18 August in 2025, a previously unidentified threat actor tracked as UNC6395 was linked to a breach campaign that exposed Salesforce customer data through the misuse of OAuth tokens issued via Salesloft Drift integration. It was discovered that all applications with a Salesloft Drift integration are affected. Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) and Mandiant reported on the widespread data theft on 26 August 2025. Moreover, Google is investigating a few Google Workspace accounts that are also impacted. GTIG assesses the primary intent of the threat actor is to harvest credentials. After the data was exfiltrated, the actor searched through the data to look for secrets that could be potentially used to compromise victim environments. GTIG observed UNC6395 targeting sensitive credentials such as Amazon Web Services (AWS) access keys (AKIA), passwords, and Snowflake-related access tokens. UNC6395 demonstrated operational security awareness by deleting query jobs. 
On 2 September, the cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks disclosed a data breach of primarily customer business contact information linked to the Salesloft supply chain attack, but Palo Alto Networks products itself had not been affected. Similar, the cloud security company Zscaler disclosed a breach of customer information linked to the Salesloft compromise.
On the same day, Cloudflare announced to be affected by the Salesloft hack and stated that most of stolen information is customer contact information and basic support case data, but some customer support interactions may reveal information about a customer's configuration or may contain other sensitive information. On 21 September 2025, automaker Stellantis confirmed a data breach after a hack on a third-party service provider's platform resulted in the theft of some of its North American customers' contact information. On 22 September, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for the breach and said to obtained over 18 million Salesforce records, including names and contact details, from the company's Salesforce instance. Later, the company appeared on the leak site of Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters.
On 2 October, Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters – a collective of the notorious ShinyHunters, Scattered Spider, and Lapsus$ groups—publicly launched a data leak site threatening to release close to 1 billion records allegedly stolen from the Salesforce environments of dozens of major organizations, including FedEx, Air France and KLM, Marriott, Cisco, and Google Adsense.

name: TAG-144 Targeted 19 Colombian Entities in Government, Education, Transportation, and Retail between February and July 2025
description:
Between February and July 2025, Insikt Group observed a threat actor tracked as TAG-144 (aka Blind Eagle, AguilaCiega, APT-C-36, and APT-Q-98) targeting 19 Colombian entities in government, education, transportation, and the retail sectors through a spearphishing campaign, as reported by the unit on 26 August 2025.

The campaign involved the group utilizing lures and masquerading as local Colombian government authorities. Overall, Insikt Group observed five activity clusters as part of this campaign. 
Activities linked to cluster one, were directed throughC2 IP addresses of several VPN servers from TorGuard and a Colombian ISP, COLOMBIA TELECOMUNICACIONES S.A. E.S.P. The group was specifically observed compromising its government victims by installing remote access trojans such as DcRAT, AsyncRAT, and REMCOS RAT. Some government victims were infected multiple times and were also linked to further activity by TAG-144 in other activity clusters, indicating lateral movement or broader targeting. While TAG-144 was active in this campaign until July 2025, its network communications ended in June 2025, which Insikt Group states could indicate that the threat actors were either evicted, completed their objectives, or switched to new objectives within other activity clusters.

Insikt Group noted possible connections between TAG-144 and a threat group tracked by SCILabs under the heading Red Akodon based on similar targeting patterns and TTPs. While the five activity clusters tracked by Insikt Group differ in certain tools, there also exist significant overlaps between clusters, potentially indicating related and/or evolving campaigns rather than entirely isolated activity.

name: Russian-Linked Cyber Espionage Group APT29 Compromised Websites to Steal Microsoft Device Code Authentication from Academics and Critics in August 2025
description: Amazon disrupted a watering hole campaign by Russian-linked espionage group APT29 (also known as SVR, Cozy Bear, Nobelium, BlueBravo, Midnight Blizzard and The Dukes) that targeted academics and Russia critics to gather intelligence on 31 August 2025. The attackers used compromised websites to redirect visitors to attacker-controlled infrastructure and trick them into authorizing malicious devices through Microsoft’s device code authentication flow. Amazon’s threat intelligence team detected the activity via custom analytics, identifying domains such as findcloudflare.com that mimicked Cloudflare pages and malicious JavaScript injections redirecting 10% of site visitors. Working with Cloudflare and Microsoft, Amazon isolated affected EC2 instances, blocked malicious domains and tracked attempts to migrate to new infrastructure.

name: Unidentified Highly Sophisticated Threat Actors Breached IT Infrastructure of Austrian Ministry of Interior in August 2025
description:
The Austrian Ministry of the Interior (BMI) disclosed on 30 August 2025 that it had been the target of a highly professional and targeted intrusion discovered several weeks earlier. The compromise involved unauthorized access to ministry mail servers, affecting around 100 of approximately 60,000 email accounts. Officials stressed that sensitive citizen data, registers and core police systems were not impacted and that operational capacity remained intact.
Interior Minister Gerhard Karner and State Secretary Jörg Leichtfried described the attack as “highly professional” and consistent with patterns typically used by state or state-affiliated actors. In response, affected accounts were isolated, employees notified and external cybersecurity experts engaged. Externally, email communications were restricted as a precaution, temporarily disrupting services such as police press releases.

Publimetro Mexico reported the incident and verified samples of leaked data such as employee names, corporate minutes and action plans.

According to media sources, services of the health department were also temporarily disrupted, with access being regained on 20 August. The city meanwhile initiated an investigation alongside local, state, and federal law enforcement.

name: China-Nexus Threat Actor UNC6384 aka Mustang Panda Conducted Cyberespionage Operations against Diplomats in South Asia and Organisations Worldwide since March 2025
description: China-nexus threat actor UNC6384 aka Mustang Panda carried out a sophisticated espionage campaign aimed at diplomats in South Asia and other organizations around the world. The intrusion method involved using a Captive Portal Hijacking technique, which allowed the user to load up a window that said software or plugin updates were required when accessing the site. When a user clicked on an update check, it connected to a page that appeared to be a legitimate software update site, using an HTTPS connection containing a valid TLS certificate issued by Let's Encrypt. The attacker installed and executed multiple-level malware, including Adobe Plugs.StateCLUGlN, disguised as Exe, signed with a global agent's certificate issued by Cendu Nuuxin Times Technology. The malware implemented the user's custom TForms and disguised them as Microsoft Visual C 2013 redistributable package installations. The ending malware, CanonSTAGER, was launched, providing long-term espionage capabilities through SEC backdoors. Google Threat Intelligence Group made this technical attribution on 25 August 2025.

name: UNC6040 (aka ShinyHunters) Breached Google Systems and Stole User Data of 2.5 Billion Customers in the United States in June 2025
description: In June 2025, a threat actor tracked as UNC6040 (later linked to ShinyHunters) executed a sophisticated voice phishing (vishing) attack targeting Google employees. The attackers impersonated IT staff in phone calls to convince a Google employee to authorize a malicious connected application, enabling the exfiltration of business-related information—including contact details, company names, and associated notes—for approximately 2.5 billion Gmail users. Google officially acknowledged the attack on 4 June 2025, but spoke of limited impact noting that user passwords were not stolen. However, the company warned that other information may have been compromised and advised users to take steps to improve account security, such as using the Google Security Checkup and Advanced Protection Program. Following the attack, some users reported being targeted by social engineering scams, with individuals posing as Google employees attempting to defraud them, highlighting the increased risk of identity theft and data leaks. According to an FBI Flash Alert released on 12 September 2025, the threat activity ties in with a broader vshing social engineering campaign by UNC6040 aimed at breaking into victim networks to connect a modified version of Salesforce's Data Loader app to exfiltrate data.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Swedish Data Service Provider Miljödata on 23 August 2025
description: On 23 August 2025, an extortion attack was carried out by unknown hackers against Miljödata, a data service and system provider for Swedish municipalities. The attackers took over Miljödata's data environment and demanded a ransom of 1.5 bitcoins (approximately €135,000). The attack affected around 200 municipalities and regions, including Skellefte, Kalmar, Karlstad, Mnsters and the Gotland Region. The municipality of Skellefte reported that there was a risk that sensitive personal data belonging to current and former employees, as well as students, may have been exposed in the data breach. CERT-SE, a department within Swedish Civil Contingencies Agency responsible for cybersecurity issues, was informed of the incident and offered advice and support to the affected businesses. The attack was disclosed to the public on 26 August 2025. In September 2025, Volvo North America notified in a data breach notice that they are also affected by the breach, including full names and social security numbers of employees. In mid-September, data of 1.5 million Swedes obtained during the breach was leaked.

description: On 24 August 2025, the State of Nevada identified a breach, which was later confirmed by the state Governor's office. A later investigation revealed that the threat actor had infiltrated the system as early as 14 May 2025. The incident disrupted government websites, phone systems and online platforms, forcing all state offices to close on 25 August 2025. The Governor's Technology Office warned that websites, online services and phone lines could be slow or unavailable as teams worked to restore service. A final report concluded that the incident disrupted state systems for approximately 28 days and The investigation found that 26,408 files were accessed, but only one document contained the personal information of a former employee who has since been notified. According to a report, the incident costed the state $1.5M in total.

name: Android Backdoor Disguised as Official Antivirus Tool To Spy On Russian Business Executives in Campaign Starting in January 2025
description:
In a campaign tracing back to January 2025, an Android spyware app identified as Android.Backdoor.916.origin targeted Russian business executives. Designed to resemble a legitimate antivirus app, the tool poses as software provided or approved by Russian state institutions. The Russia-based IT security company Dr. Web, which reported on the activity on 12 August 2025, described this tactic as a hallmark of cybercriminals looking to trick unsuspecting users into installing malicious programmes. 
One version distributed under the name “GuardCB” sought to invoke a link to Russia’s Central Bank, including by using branding reminiscent of the bank’s official emblem. Other discovered modifications disguised the spyware under the names “SECURITY_FSB” and “ФСБ” (FSB).
The app, which has no known links to other malware families, was circulated through private messages in instant messengers. While the malicious app does not include any security functions, it purports to conduct malware scans on the device and returns threat detections according to a predetermined pattern. When launched, Android.Backdoor.916.origin requests access to geolocation, text messages, contacts, call lists, media files, the camera and microphone as well as permissions to make calls and work in the background. The malicious app further seeks access to the device accessibility service to deploy key logging capabilities to capture user interactions in instant messengers and browsers, including Telegram, Google Chrome, Gmail, Yandex Start, Yandex Browser, and WhatsApp. Able to connect to a variety of command-and-control servers, the tool allows the operators to exfiltrate information collected through this set of permissions.

description: The Qilin ransomware group claimed responsibility for a data breach at Nissan’s Tokyo-based design subsidiary Creative Box Inc. (CBI) in August 2025, exfiltrating 4TB of data. The attackers copied more than 405,000 files, including 3D design data, reports, photos, videos and internal documents linked to Nissan automobile projects. As proof, Qilin published sample files on its dark web leak site, including 3D CAD renderings, financial data, a photorealistic vehicle interior render and images of staff using VR headsets to review designs. The group threatened to release further sensitive material unless its demands were met. The breach targeted CBI’s design workflow, potentially exposing valuable intellectual property to competitors. Hackread.com reported on the incident with screenshots from the leak site.

name: Warlock Ransomware Group Breached Orange Belgium in Late July 2025, Exposing Information of 850,000 Customers
description:
Orange Belgium, the Belgian branch of the Orange Group, an international telecommunications company, was breached by unknown actors in late July 2025, according to a press release published on 20 August 2025. 

According to Orange Belgium's press release, the actors gained unauthorized access to the information of 850,000 customers through breaching one of the company's IT systems; the impacted data included surnames, first names, telephone numbers, SIM card numbers, personal unlocking key (PUK) codes, and tariff plans. Following the detection of the breach, Orange Belgium immediately shut down the affected system, thus blocking access, while the company alerted the relevant authorities and filed a breach notification. Warlock Ransomware Group claimed responsibility for the attack after publishing 4 GB of the stolen data in August 2025.

name: Unknown Hackers Breached UK-based Inflite The Jet Centre Ltd., Accessed Data of up to 3700 People on 10 August 2025
description:
A UK-based contractor for the UK Ministry of Defence, "Inflite The Jet Centre Ltd.," was breached by unknown hackers on or around 10 August 2025, resulting in unauthorized access to the information of roughly 3,700 people, particularly including people resettled from Afghanistan, according to the Ministry of Defence's Afghan resettlement team.

Inflite The Jet Centre, Ltd., which provides ground-based services for flights linked to the UK's Ministry of Defence, published a breach notification of the incident, stating that they detected "unauthorised access to a limited number of company emails," which, according to an email sent out by the Ministry of Defence, covered the time period between January and March 2024. While details regarding the scope are yet unclear outside of the particular impact on resettled people from Afghanistan, the BBC reported that the information of military personnel and former government ministers was also impacted in the breach; Inflite, however, reiterated that the information was only limited to email accounts.

name: WarLock Ransomware Breached Internal Systems of UK Telecommunications Provider Colt Technology on 12 August 2025
description:
WarLock Ransomware attacked London-based telecommunications provider Colt Technology Services on 12 August 2025, leading to the temporary shutdown of several internal systems, including the customer portal Colt Online and the Voice API platform. As a precaution, Colt took systems offline, causing disruption to support services. 
On 15 August, the WarLock ransomware group claimed responsibility, offering a cache of allegedly stolen documents for sale. The group alleged it had exfiltrated around one million files, including financial, employee, and customer data, internal emails, and software development material. Sample data was posted on a hacker forum to support the claim. Colt acknowledged on 21 August that data had been stolen, while WarLock began auctioning files stolen in the incident.
Security researcher Kevin Beaumont linked the incident to the exploitation of a remote code execution vulnerability in Microsoft SharePoint (CVE-2025-53770), which had been actively exploited as a zero-day. He reported that Colt’s SharePoint servers had likely been compromised with webshells and taken offline, with several hundred gigabytes of data potentially stolen.

description:
A ransomware attack against the Pennsylvania Attorney General’s Office, discovered before 13 August 2025, disrupted its phone and email systems, impairing communication with state residents. While prosecutors continued working on cases, the office’s public website remained inaccessible as of Wednesday morning. Attorney General Dave Sunday announced the incident via social media, noting that Information Technology staff were working around the clock to restore services. The identities of the attackers and the methods used to compromise the networks have not been disclosed. The incident was publicly reported on 13 August 2025.
In a statement published on 29 August 2025, the Attorney General's Office disclosed that the attackers demanded a ransom, but no payment was made. 
On 21 September 2025, INC Ransom claimed responsibility for the attack by adding Pennsylvania Attorney General's Office to its dark web leak site. On 17 November 2025, the office confirmed in a statement that, in August, they were attacked with ransomware and data has been stolen including Social Security numbers.

While the potential leak and its scope is yet unclear, the city sent out a notice on 15 July to citizens who were potentially affected by the breach, informing them of their options to protect their identities.

According to the city's notice, the attack resulted in an immediate isolating of the city's servers, thus impacting the functioning of certain card payment systems. Forensic and IT experts were brought in to analyse the scope and depth of the damage. While the city acknowledged the breached database mentioned above, it also mentioned that the city's systems had been successfully isolated, and the damage contained by 9 June 2025.

name: Russian State-Affiliated Threat Actors Suspected of Breaching the Administrative Office of the US Court System In Early July 2025
description:
An unidentified threat group, widely suspected, according to Politico, to have nation-state ties, breached two systems operated by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts: the Case Management/Electronic Case Files (CM/ECF) system and PACER, its publicly accessible counterpart. These platforms are used across several federal courts to manage and access case filings, including those in criminal proceedings.
The intrusion, which occurred sometime before 4 July 2025, compromised sensitive information such as the identities of informants, sealed indictments, and search warrants. In at least one federal court district, approximately a dozen dockets were altered during the attack. Politico was first to report the breach on 6 August 2025, citing sources familiar with the incident.
As reported by The New York Times on 12 August 2025, investigators believe that Russia may have been behind the attack.

name: Threat Actor UAC-0099 Used Fake Court Summonses in Cyberespionage Campaign Against Ukrainian Defence Sector in 2025
description:
In a recent cyberespionage campaign targeting Ukraine’s government, military and defence sector, threat actor UAC-0099 sent phishing emails impersonating Ukrainian courts to deliver malware-laced archive files via legitimate file-sharing platforms. The campaign was disclosed by CERT-UA and involves a new malware family dubbed Matchboil, used to collect system data and deploy secondary tools including Matchwok (a backdoor for remote command execution) and Dragstare (a stealer that extracts browser data and desktop files).
UAC-0099 has been active since at least 2022 and has previously targeted forensic institutions, forestry departments and industrial entities in Ukraine. While CERT-UA did not attribute the attacks to a specific country, the targeting and methods bear resemblance to operations previously linked to Russian state actors.

description: Russian State-sponsored Hackers are suspected to have hacked the Dutch public prosecution department's internal systems between June and July 2025. name: Hacker ‘ByteToBreach’ Allegedly Breached Seychelles Commercial Bank Systems Since 5 February 2025
description:
A hacker operating under the pseudonym ByteToBreach allegedly breached Seychelles Commercial Bank (SCB) on 5 February 2025 and subsequently exfiltrated over 2 GB of customer data from its online banking systems, beginning on 4 July. The attacker claimed to have exploited a vulnerability in Oracle WebLogic Server to gain access, and later exfiltrated the data.
According to cybersecurity firm Resecurity, the leaked dataset includes names, phone numbers, email addresses, account types and balances - some associated with government-labelled accounts. The attacker also claimed to have decrypted portions of the data using keys obtained from SCB’s internal environment, specifically within its Oracle Flexcube Private Banking (FCDB) platform.
SCB confirmed the breach on 12 July, stating that personal information of internet banking customers was exposed but no funds were accessed. Internet banking services were temporarily suspended, and the bank urged customers to use ATMs or visit branches. Law enforcement and the Central Bank of Seychelles were notified.
ByteToBreach attempted to extort the bank and, after receiving no response, began contacting customers directly and selling the stolen data on dark web forums.

Ukrainian military intelligence (GUR) conducted a major cyber operation against the servers of the Russian-installed administration in occupied Crimea, extracting approximately 100 terabytes of data in around 25 July 2025. Some were reportedly placed for adoption by Russian citizens in violation of international law. According to GUR spokesman Andrii Yusov, the obtained data is critical for documenting Russia’s war crimes and will support ongoing efforts to locate and return the abducted children to Ukraine.

name: Interlock Ransomware Group Targeted City Systems in St. Paul, Minnesota, USA on 25 July 2025
description:
Critical systems in St. Paul, Minnesota, were targeted in a cyberattack that disrupted offline payments and digital services across libraries, recreation centres, and other city operations. The breach, first detected on 25 July through suspicious activity flagged by the city’s endpoint detection and response system, persisted through the weekend and significantly impaired essential services.
In response, Governor Tim Walz issued an executive order activating the Minnesota National Guard to support mitigation efforts. Mayor Melvin Carter declared a state of local emergency on 29 July, authorizing the Emergency Management Department and Office of Technology and Communications to coordinate with state and federal partners.
To contain the breach, the city initiated a complete network shutdown, disabling public internet terminals and Wi-Fi systems across municipal facilities. Libraries and recreation centres remained open but without internet access. The FBI and federal cybersecurity experts are leading the criminal investigation. On 10 August 2025, a city spokesperson confirmed to local news outlets the city was hit with a ransomware attack but said it has not paid the ransom. One day later, Interlock ransomware group claimed responsibility for the incident.

The attack was reported by Silent Crow on 20 July 2025 on Telegram, and later disclosed to the public through articles published on 29 July 2025.

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Groups Compromised Over 400 Organisations via Microsoft SharePoint Vulnerabilities Beginning July 2025
description:
Chinese state-sponsored threat actors exploited zero-day vulnerabilities in Microsoft SharePoint Server, compromising over 400 organisations worldwide, including several US federal agencies and the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA), according to Microsoft and cybersecurity researchers. 
Microsoft confirmed that three threat groups - Linen Typhoon, Violet Typhoon (both state-sponsored) and Storm-2603 (a China-based actor) - exploited a vulnerability chain dubbed "ToolShell" targeting on-premises SharePoint servers exposed to the internet. The vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-53770 and CVE-2025-53771) allow authentication bypass and remote code execution.
Initial exploitation began as early as 7 July 2025. The Dutch cybersecurity firm Eye Security first detected unusual activity on 18 July 2025 and subsequently identified dozens of compromised systems after scanning over 8,000 SharePoint instances. Affected sectors include government, defence, energy, healthcare and critical infrastructure. Confirmed victims include the US Departments of Energy, Homeland Security, Health and Human Services and Education, as well as the California Independent System Operator. A Department of Energy spokesperson stated that only a limited number of systems were affected and there was no evidence of classified data being compromised.
Microsoft observed Storm-2603 deploying Warlock ransomware on compromised networks beginning 18 July 2025, disabling Microsoft Defender, modifying policy settings and attempting to exfiltrate cryptographic keys from SharePoint servers to maintain persistent access. CISA added the exploited CVEs to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalogue and instructed federal agencies to apply patches and conduct compromise assessments.

name: Unknown Perpetrators Compromised Hong Kong Flag Carrier Cathay Pacific Membership Accounts on 25 July 2025
description:
On 25 July 2025, Cathay Pacific Airways disclosed that approximately 1,000 membership accounts were accessed by unauthorized parties, primarily affecting members based in Hong Kong. The attackers used valid credentials previously exposed online and exploited a weakness in the secondary verification process to gain access.
The breach allowed threat actors to view personal details and travel information and steal Asia Miles, although no credit card data was compromised. Cathay Pacific rectified the verification flaw, reinforced authentication measures, and worked to restore affected accounts and reinstate stolen Asia Miles. Most impacted members were contacted directly.
The airline reported the incident to relevant authorities, including the Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data, and appointed an external expert to conduct an independent investigation.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Disrupted Phone And Internet Systems Of Fort Smith Public School District in Arkansas, USA Around 21 July 2025
description:
Unknown threat actors breached the Fort Smith Public School District in Arkansas on or before 21 July 2025, disrupting phone and internet systems across the district. The incident was publicly disclosed by district communications specialist Addie Reith via a Facebook post on 21 July and in a statement to Talk Business & Politics on 23 July. The district engaged a third-party cybersecurity firm to assist with remediation efforts.
While the attackers' identities and methods remain undisclosed, the breach was reported to all required agencies in line with Arkansas Department of Education guidelines. As of 24 July, system restoration was still in progress, though officials stated that the disruption was not expected to delay the school year, scheduled to begin on 13 August 2025.

name: ShinyHunters/Scattered Spider Breached Insurance Company Allianz Life in United States on 16 July 2025
description:
On 16 July 2025, Allianz Life Insurance Company of North America (Allianz Life) was breached by a threat actor using a social engineering technique to access a third-party cloud-based customer relationship management (CRM) system. The incident resulted in the exposure of personally identifiable data relating to the majority of Allianz Life’s 1.4 million customers, financial professionals and select employees.
Allianz Life stated that the attack did not compromise its internal network or policy administration system. The company reported the incident to the FBI and initiated customer notifications. The breach only affected Allianz Life and not its parent company, Allianz SE. Public disclosure followed on 26 July 2025, via filings with the Maine Attorney General’s Office and subsequent media reporting. While Allianz Life has not confirmed the identity of the attacker, BleepingComputer noted that the breach may be linked to the ShinyHunters extortion group, which is thought to have significant overlap with the Scattered Spider group.

description:

The attackers compromised a server containing personally identifiable information of patients and employees, including names, addresses, Social Security numbers, member identification numbers, dates of birth, and health insurance details. External counsel SpencerFane notified the New Hampshire Attorney General, and the full scope of the breach was determined by 23 June 2025.
Although there was communication with the attackers, no ransom was paid and no data has been published.

name: Hacker 'Kazu' Exploited Vulnerability In Mexican Government Portal And Stole Sensitive Data Of Over 73,000 Citizens From Attorney General's Office Of San Luis Potosi In Mexico On 25 July 2025
description:
On 25 July 2025, a hacker known as 'Kazu' breached the online portal of the Attorney General’s Office of the State of San Luis Potosí (FGESLP) in Mexico, exploiting a structural vulnerability to obtain over 73,000 criminal history records bearing authentic digital stamps. The exposed database, totalling 36.4 GB and comprising 73,156 PDF files, included sensitive personal data and legal documentation. Kazu demanded $6,000 to withhold the leak and offered the full dataset for sale at $600 via a clandestine forum.
Cybersecurity analyst Nicolas Azuara confirmed the presence of a critical flaw in the FGESLP website that enabled unauthorized access to criminal records by modifying URL parameters with valid credentials. The vulnerability, which may have been open to exploitation through leaked access data or infostealer-type malware, allowed attackers to exfiltrate confidential documents without authentication barriers.

name: Unknown Hackers Stole Personal Data of France Travail Users in France on 13 July 2025
description:
Unknown threat actors compromised the Kairos application used by France Travail, resulting in the unauthorized access of personal data belonging to approximately 340,000 jobseekers. The breach was traced to an account belonging to a training organization based in Isère, which had been infected with an infostealer designed to capture authentication credentials. The exposed data included names, dates of birth, France Travail identifiers, email and postal addresses, and phone numbers; passwords and financial information were not affected.
The incident occurred on 12 July 2025 and was publicly disclosed by France Travail on 23 July 2025. In response, the organization closed the affected service, lodged a complaint with judicial authorities, and notified the CNIL. The Paris Public Prosecutor’s Office assigned the investigation to the BL2C (Brigade de lutte contre la cybercriminalité) for breach of an automated data processing system.

name: Iranian Espionage Group MuddyWater Stole Data Using Android Malware DCHSpy in June and July 2025
description: MuddyWater, a threat actor believed to be affiliated with the Iranian Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), used a newly-discovered strain of the Android surveillance tool DCHSpy to primarily target regime adversaries, between June and July 2025. MuddyWater distributed the malware using spoofed URLs in Telegram and other messaging app channels, intended to draw targets to websites hosting malicious VPN and banking apps. The malware exfiltrated data from WhatsApp, files stored on devices, contacts, text messages, location and call logs, and was able to use device cameras and microphones to take photos and record audio. The threat actors relied on political lures, including websites containing links to malicious apps, with one lure involving Starlink, which provided Iranians with web access after the country's government imposed an internet blackout following Israel's attacks. Researchers from the cybersecurity firm Lookout detected the latest version of DCHSpy one week after Israel began its air campaign targeting Iran's nuclear program in June 2025, according to a company report published on 21 July 2025.

name: China-nexus Threat Actors Exploited Two Zero-Day Vulnerabilities in Microsoft's SharePoint in Multiple Sectors Globally in July 2025
description:
At least three China-nexus threat actors (Linen Typhoon/APT27, Violet Typhoon/APT31 and Storm-2603) exploited two zero-day vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-53770 and CVE-2025-537701) in Microsoft's collaboration software SharePoint to breach multiple organizations globally including U.S. state and federal agencies, universities, energy companies, and an Asian telecommunications company. 
The breaches affected tens of thousands of servers worldwide that host SharePoint locally, including those of organizations in the United States, Canada, Austria, Jordan, Mexico, Germany, Switzerland, Netherlands, South Africa, Australia, Spain, Brazil, and Asia. On 19 July 2025, Microsoft issued a customer guidance for the SharePoint vulnerabilities, recommending users to modify server settings or disconnect them from the internet. As of 22 July, Microsoft had released a patch for all supported versions of the software. 
Abuse of the two zero days was first detected by the Dutch cybersecurity company Eye Security on 18 July. The observed intrusions leveraged the zero-day vulnerabilities along an exploit chain demonstrated earlier in May 2025 that takes advantage of two related SharePoint vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-49706 and CVE-2025-49704).
The compromise allowed intruders to obtain cryptographic keys for breached servers. With this access, the threat actors are able to impersonate users and maintain persistence on compromised systems through backdoors even after security updates are installed to mitigate the vulnerabilities.
In at least one instance reported by the Washington Post on 20 July, an official of an eastern US state noted losing access to a repository of documents informing the public about government services. Whether the data was deleted remained unclear at the time of reporting. On 21 July, the Washington Post further cited an assessment by Google’s Mandiant Consulting that deemed at least one China-nexus actor was involved in the early exploitation of the software flaws.  On 22 July, Microsoft Threat Intelligence reported that at least three China-linked threat actors—Linen Typhoon (APT27), Violet Typhoon (APT31), and Storm-2603—had exploited a SharePoint vulnerability for data theft and espionage.
The United States, Canada, and Australia launched an investigation into the incident.

name: Suspected Iran-Sponsored Hacker Defaced Arizona Secretary Of States Office Website In United States Beginning on 23 June
description:
A hacker suspected of ties to the Iranian government compromised the Arizona Secretary of State’s Office candidate portal, between 23 and 30 June 2025. The attacker uploaded malicious scripts to the server, replacing historical candidate images (2016–2024) with a photograph of former Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ruhollah Musavi Khomeini. Some of the altered images linked to social media accounts, including a Telegram channel containing messages critical of US President Trump’s policies towards Iran. The intrusion occurred two days after a US bombing operation in Iran and was discovered by a staff member.
The Arizona Secretary of State, Adrian Fontes, publicly disclosed the attack, noting that the hacker continued to target the Secretary of State’s website for nearly a week. Other Arizona state agencies were also reportedly targeted by the same IP addresses. Authorities confirmed that the intrusion did not affect election tabulation or results. The FBI, state Department of Homeland Security, and National Guard were engaged to support the investigation and response.

name: Lazarus Suspected to Have Stolen $44.2 Million From Indian Cryptocurrency Exchange CoinDCX On 19 July 2025
description:
On 19 July 2025, hackers breached CoinDCX, India’s leading cryptocurrency exchange, stealing approximately $44 million from one of the company’s internal operational accounts. The attackers exploited a sophisticated server breach, with initial activity traced to a wallet funded via Tornado Cash, a privacy-focused crypto mixer. A portion of the stolen assets was later bridged from Solana (SOL) to Ethereum (ETH). CoinDCX cofounders Neeraj Khandelwal and CEO Sumit Gupta disclosed the incident via X posts on the same day, assuring customers that their funds were unaffected as they are held in segregated wallets. 
The company’s internal security and operations teams launched an investigation in collaboration with external cybersecurity partners to identify vulnerabilities, track the stolen funds, and bolster defences. CoinDCX temporarily suspended trading in the Web3 section as a precaution and announced plans to introduce a bug bounty programme to aid in the recovery effort. During July 2025, the IT security company Cyvers issued a report linking the attack to North Korean state-sponsored group Lazarus.

description: Between March and June 2025, at least three China-aligned hacking groups (UNK_SparkyCarp, UNK_DropPitch, UNK_FistBump) conducted targeted cyber espionage operations against Taiwan's semiconductor companies and financial analysts, with the activities believed to ongoing in July 2025. name: Unknown Threat Actors Hit South Korean Seoul Guarantee Insurance with Ransomware Attack in Mid July 2025
description:
On 13 July 2025, Seoul Guarantee Insurance (SGI), Korea’s largest comprehensive guarantee insurance provider, confirmed it was recovering from a ransomware attack attributed to an unidentified threat actor. The attack had disrupted core systems, affecting key services such as the issuance and verification of guarantee insurance policies. SGI stated that no direct communication was established with the threat actor, and no ransom demand was received.
The firm launched a response effort led by senior leadership, with support from financial security authorities, oil pipeline infrastructure teams, and other relevant stakeholders. SGI worked closely with financial administrators and security officers to restore services and assess damage. Core operations were reportedly restored by 17 July.
The Financial Services Commission and the Financial Supervisory Service have launched an investigation into the incident and are reviewing potential legal or regulatory gaps. SGI pledged to fully compensate verified losses.

name: Interlock Ransomware Gang Launched a Ransomware Attack on West Lothian Schools in Scotland and Compromised Data on 6 May 2025
description: A suspected ransomware attack occurred on West Lothian Council Schools' education network on 6 May 2025, located in Scotland. The attack was claimed by a ransomware gang called Interlock, though the exact circumstances and impact of the attack are yet unclear as an investigation continues. In the meantime, the West Lothian Council's IT staff worked to resolve the issue and mitigate the impact, with contingency plans allowing schools to continue operating with minimal disruption. The attack was contained to the education network, which was subsequently isolated from the rest of the Council's networks in order to minimize damage. Despite the uncertainty surrounding the incident, the Council stated on 15 July 2025 that a "small percentage of the total data stored on our Education Network has been compromised" and that a "small amount of information held by a number of our secondary schools, and one primary school, was compromised" as part of the incident, with a small amount of the data being of a not further specified personal and sensitive nature.

name: North Korean State-Sponsored Hackers Targeting Developers with Malicious Code in npm Repository in Ongoing "Contagious Interview" Campaign Since at Least April 2025
description:
North Korea-linked threat actors, tied to an ongoing supply chain attack dubbed "Contagious Interview," planted 67 malicious packages in a Node Package Manager (npm) online repository since at least April 2025, delivering a new malware loader called XORIndex to developer systems. npm is the standard package manager for Node.js, a platform used by developers to share and source JavaScript libraries and tools. The packages (which deployed XORIndex and another loader known as HexEval), which together counted more than 17,000 downloads, were discovered by researchers at package security platform Socket, which reported on them on 15 July 2025. 

According to the Socket report, when victims installed any of these packages, a post-install script executed to launch XORIndex Loader, which collected host data and sent it to a hardcoded command and control (C2) address hosted by the cloud application company Vercel, consistent with the use of legitimate infrastructure providers previously observed for Contagious Interview. The C2 server responded with one or more JavaScript payloads, typically the BeaverTail and InvisibleFerret backdoors, which provided access to compromised machines, allowed data exfiltration and downloaded more payloads. The North Korean hackers blended old and new tools with subtle modifications to evade detection, rotating through new npm maintainer aliases, reusing loaders like HexEval Loader, and actively deploying newly observed variants including XORIndex Loader.

name: Unknown Hackers Compromised Employee Email Accounts of District Administration Office Odenwaldkreis in Germany on 10 July 2025
description:
Unknown hackers compromised several employee email accounts related to the district administration office Odenwaldkreis in the German state of Hesse through a phishing attack on 10 July 2025. Staff were tricked into entering their credentials on a fraudulent website designed to imitate an official login page. While multiple accounts were accessed and subsequently misused, the broader IT infrastructure remained unaffected and no citizen data was compromised.
The IT department immediately responded by blocking the impacted accounts, disconnecting affected systems, and launching a diagnostic review of around 40 employee accounts. The administration is working with the Hessian Ministry of the Interior’s Cyber Competence Centre to investigate the incident, which is believed to be part of a larger phishing campaign.

name: Pro-Russian Hacking Group NoName057(16) Launched Ddos Attack On Sachsen-Anhalt State Portal In Germany On 10 July 2025
description:
On 10 July 2025, a pro-Russian hacker group named NoName057(16) launched a Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attack on the state portal of Saxony-Anhalt, Germany, making several ministry websites temporarily inaccessible for a few hours. The attack was confirmed by a spokesperson for the Digital Ministry of the state, who informed the news agency dpa. The IT service provider Dataport, which also serves the public administration in Hamburg, Bremen, and Schleswig-Holstein, reported no disruptions in these regions.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Anatsa Banking Trojan Injected into Banking Apps targeting US and Canadian Customers between 24 and 30 June 2025
description: The Anatsa Android banking trojan was used to target North American bank customers, specifically in the United States and Canada. The malware was embedded in a seemingly harmless file reader app on the Google Play Store. The app, which had amassed over 50,000 downloads, was updated with malicious code between 24 and 30 June 2025, enabling the logging keystrokes, theft of banking credentials, and initiation of fraudulent transactions. ThreatFabric researchers, who have been monitoring Anatsa's activity since 2020, reported the campaign to Google, which subsequently removed the malicious app from the store.

name: Pakistani State-Sponsored Hacking Group Transparent Tribe (APT36) Targeted Indian Defence Sector with Linux Malware on 7 June 2025
description: On 7 June 2025, an espionage operation was detected which was carried out by the Pakistan-based group Transparent Tribe (aka APT36), targeting Indian defence personnel and organizations that used BOSS Linux, an Indian Linux distribution. The threat actor employed phishing emails with compressed files, typically ZIP archives named CyberSecurityAdvisory.zip, containing a malicious desktop file. When opened, this file created a diversion by displaying a normal-looking PowerPoint file while secretly downloading and running another malicious program, BOSS.elf, saved locally as client.elf, which attempted to connect to a control server. The activities were first reported on 8 July 2025 and attributed by IT security company Cyfirma.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Hacked Into Technology Supplier C&M Software And Stole $140 Million From Financial Institutions Ion 30 June 2025
description: On 30 June 2025, hackers breached C&M Software, a tech company authorized by Brazil's central bank to connect smaller banks and fintech companies to national banking systems, in particular the instant payment system PIX operated by the central bank. The threat actors used login credentials acquired from C&M employee João Roque for about 2,700 Brazilian reais (around $500), enabling them to initiate fraudulent PIX transactions. They accessed reserve accounts and stole over 800 million reais ($140 million) from six financial institutions, including BMP, Credsystem, and Banco Paulista. Roughly 30 to 40 million reais were converted into the cryptocurrencies Bitcoin, Ether, and Tether, then laundered via Latin American exchanges and over-the-counter trading (OTC) platforms. Brazil's central bank suspended access to C&M Software’s systems to contain damage. Roque was arrested and investigations continue to recover funds and identify others involved.

description: On 22 April 2025, BlackSuit hackers breached the systems of Gloucester County in Virginia, exposing Social Security numbers and other sensitive data of government employees, including names, drivers license numbers, bank account information, health insurance numbers, and medical information. The county administrator, Carol Steele, hired cybersecurity experts to aid in recovery and notified the FBI's Cyber Crimes Division and the Cyber Fusion Center of the Virginia State Police. The county initially reported network disruptions on 22 and 23 April but did not provide further updates until sending notices to affected employees in July. The BlackSuit ransomware gang claimed responsibility for the attack on 15 May stating that the county refused to negotiate a ransom. On 24 Jul 2025, BlackSuit’s technical infrastructure was seized in a globally coordinated takedown.

description: Iran-linked Educated Manticore launched a phishing campaign targeting Israeli journalists, cybersecurity professionals, and computer science professors in June 2025, coinciding with the outbreak of the Iran-Israel war. The threat actors posed as employees of cybersecurity firms, using emails and WhatsApp messages to trick victims into revealing their email credentials and two-factor authentication codes. Israeli cyber and computer science experts were among those targeted. The campaign was disclosed by Checkpoint Research on 25 June 2025, which assesses Educated Manticore to operate under instructions of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps’ Intelligence Organization (IRGC-IO).

description:
The China-linked threat group Hive0154 was observed by IBM X-Force to be distributing Pubload malware through an ongoing phishing campaign targeting the Tibetan community in the lead-up to events centring around the Dalai Lama's 90th birthday (in July 2025) and the 9th World Parliamentarians' Convention on Tibet (WPCT), which occurred in June 2025.

To further the campaign, Hive0154 used both legitimate and malicious files within their phishing lures, copying legitimate texts/articles and embedding malware into certain other files, such as DOCX files, in an attempt to make the victim trust the legitimacy of the emails sent.

name: Hawaiian Airlines Operations Disrupted, Potentially by Scattered Spider, on or Around 26 June 2025
description:
Hawaiian Airlines was breached in a cyber incident that disrupted access to some of its systems on or around 26 June 2025, with several anonymous sources claiming that the incident was the work of Scattered Spider; one anonymous source who spoke to Reuters about Scattered Spider's involvement had worked to mitigate several other Scattered Spider incidents in the past. An FBI warning about the group targeting the airline sector aligning with this assessment. Reporting by Palo Alto Networks indicated further circumstantial evidence for this attack pattern.

The airline hired external cybersecurity experts to assess the incident's impact and restore affected systems. Hawaiian Airlines informed the public about the breach through a statement on their website and a banner noting that flights were not impacted. The Federal Aviation Administration also commented on the incident, stating that there was no impact on safety and that the airline continued to operate safely.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Disrupted Internet Services Of Netspeed In Türkiye On 27 June 2025
description:
Netspeed, a leading Internet service provider (ISP) in Türkiye, was targeted in efforts to disrupt its services which started on 24 June 2025 and lasted for three days.

The activities caused communication cut-offs, resulting in subscribers being unable to access the internet and the company's call centre being unreachable. Netspeed disclosed the incident through a statement on social media on 27 June 2025, stating that technical teams were working around the clock to resolve the issue and that necessary measures had been taken to restore services to normal. Netspeed's statement did not provide information on whether the disruptions were the result of a network compromise.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Compromised Province Of Bolzano In Italy On 23 June 2025
description: On 23 June 2025, a cyber attack was carried out on the Province of Bolzano, specifically targeting systems of the Provincial Operations Centre of the South Tyrol Health Agency, the Provincial Road Station, the Firefighters Station of the Permanent Corps of Bolzano, and the Provincial Radio Communications Service of the Civil Protection Agency. The threat actors used malware to compromise the system, with the cause of the failure known by 25 June 2025. Forensic analysis was ongoing as of 26 June 2025. The Province reported that restoration efforts began on 26 June 2025, and were expected to be completed in the next few days without interruption. Despite the incident, emergency services remained operational, with staff working manually to receive telephone calls and radio communications. The provincial radio network was largely unaffected, with over 130 out of 140 Tetra network stations remaining active. The Province stated that the damage was limited to a single system and did not spread, ensuring all Agency services continued to function, albeit with some short-term limitations.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Glasgow City Council In Scotland On 19 June 2025
description: On 29 June 2025, Glasgow City Council was hit by a cyber attack, potentially resulting in the theft of customer data. The Council's services were taken offline, including planning applications, payment of parking or bus lane fines, and ordering certificates, after malicious activity was detected on servers managed by a third-party supplier. The incident did not involve email, and email communication with the Council remained safe. The affected services included planning, penalty charge notices, pensions, registrars, revenues and benefits as well as several online forms for citizens to submit permit and certificate applications, complaints, and FOI requests. The Council, alongside police and the national and Scottish cyber security centres, investigated the incident and advised individuals who had used the affected forms to be cautious about any contact claiming to be from the Council.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Dif Guadalajara And Civil Hospitals Of Guadalajara In Mexico On 24 June 2025
description: On 24 June 2025, a cyber attack was carried out against the DIF Guadalajara system and the Civil Hospitals of Guadalajara in Mexico, compromising personal data of its users. The exact number of people affected has not been determined, but it is reported that over 33,000 files containing personal, medical, financial, and employment data of patients and workers were stolen from the hospital. A hacker operating under the pseudonym 'Kazu' claimed responsibility for the breach and offered to sell a cache of 7.6 GB of stolen date for $250. The University of Guadalajara and DIF Guadalajara reported that they had filed complaints with the relevant authorities and strengthened cybersecurity measures to protect user data. The same threat actor is suspected to also have compromised the Guadalajara DIF System, exposing data from over 63,000 people.

name: A Student Hacked The Platform Educacyl Of The Education Council Of Castile And León In Spain On 31 May 2025
description:
A student breached the information systems of the Education Council of Castile and León's in Spain on 31 May 2025, compromising the personal data of thousands of students and their families. The compromise, which was detected on 31 May and confirmed on 2 June, gave the hacker access to personal information including names, identity cards, addresses, telephones, emails, and birthdates. The Education Department implemented technical measures to block the attack, strengthened management applications, and incorporated new filters and monitoring alerts. The incident was notified to the Spanish Data Protection Agency and a formal complaint was filed with the Civil Guard. The incident was reported by various sources, including Carlos Fernández Carriedo, the Minister of Economy and Finance, who stated that the student had usurped a teacher's identity and access key. In statements posted under the handle 'MafiaTributaria', the student claimed to have obtained a database containing over a million personal records of students and their parents. 
The Socialist Parliamentary Group raised the incident, criticizing that the event had not disclosed in the plenary of the Courts of Castile and León.

name: Russian Hackers Z-Pentest Breached Lake Risevatnet Dam Systems in Norway and Opened Water Valve at Full Capacity in April 2025
description: Pro-Russian hacking group Z-Pentest breached the control systems of a Norwegian dam at Lake Risevatnet near the city of Svelgen in southwest Norway in April 2025, opening its water valve at full capacity for four hours before the intrusion was detected. The incident, reported by Norwegian energy news outlet Energiteknikk on 25 June 2025, was discovered on 7 April by the dam’s owner, Breivika Eiendom. Norwegian authorities, including the NSM (National Security Authority), NVE (Norwegian Water Resources and Energy Directorate), and Kripos (a special agency of the Norwegian Police Service), were notified on 10 April. Officials believe the attackers exploited a weak password protecting the valve’s web-accessible control panel, a vulnerability identified by Breivika technical manager Bjarte Steinhovden. The hackers’ actions did not endanger the public, as the water flow only slightly exceeded the dam’s minimum discharge requirement. In August 2025, Z-Pentest released a video showing operatives taking control of the dam thereby claiming responsibility for the attack. Norwegian officials attributed the attack to pro-Russian hackers without specifically mentioning the threat actor.

The president of Melilla, Juan Jos Imbroda, reported the incident and stated that the technical services and the CNI (Spanish National Intelligence Center) are working to resolve the issue. The computer services were out of service for several days, causing significant declines in internet and telephone services, and generating concern among citizens and local authorities. On 26 June 2026, Qilin Ransomware Group released a statement claiming to have obtained four to five terrabytes of data and demanding a ransom of 1.8 million euros.

description: In mid-February 2025, Salt Typhoon, a Chinese state-sponsored cyber threat actor, compromised three network devices registered to a Canadian telecommunications company by exploiting the CVE-2023-20198 vulnerability in Cisco IOS XE. The incident was reported and attributed by the Canadian Centre for Cyber Security and the FBI on 19 June 2025.

description: Iran-linked threat actors, associated with the Cyber Fattah movement, leaked thousands of records containing information about visitors and athletes from past Saudi Games on 22 June 2025. The stolen data originated from a database related to the Saudi Games 2024 official website, where visitors, athletes, and their teams could register and share personal information. The breach contained sensitive data, including personally identifiable information (PII), International Bank Account Numbers (IBANs), and medical examination certificates. US-based cybersecurity firm Resecurity identified the threat actors and reported the incident.

name: INC Ransom Group Breached And Encrypted National Health Information System In Tonga Before 20 June 2025
description:
Tonga’s National Health Information System (NHIS) was breached by an unnamed ransomware group that encrypted the system and demanded a ransom payment on 20 June 2025. The attack, which began earlier that week, disrupted the organization’s website and key digital tools, including access to patient medical records, prescriptions, health risk assessments, and future care plans.
Health Minister Dr. Ana Akauola confirmed the breach and stated that staff were reverting to manual data handling. She also noted that the attackers had pledged not to destroy the data. Australian cybersecurity experts were deployed to assist with recovery efforts. The breach was first reported by Talanoa O Tonga, and Deputy Prime Minister Dr. Taniela Fusimalohi addressed the issue during a parliamentary session.
Minister for Police Paula Piukala criticized previous administrations for neglecting warnings about digital vulnerabilities, while former Prime Minister Siaosi Sovaleni suggested the breach may have been triggered by a simple phishing link. Prime Minister Aisake Eke assured the public that updates would follow as Australian teams worked to restore full system functionality.
On 27 June 2025, INC Ransom claimed responsibility for the attack through a listing in its dark net leaking site.

name: Predatory Sparrow Targets Iranian Cryptocurrency Exchange Nobitex Making $90 Million in Crypto Unaccessible on 18 June 2025
description:
On 18 June 2025, the hacker group Gonjeshke Darande (Predatory Sparrow), reportedly linked to Israel, claimed responsibility for a cyberattack on Nobitex, one of Iran’s largest cryptocurrency exchanges. The group stated it had exfiltrated and destroyed over $90 million worth of cryptocurrency, deliberately sending the assets to wallets that could not be accessed, in what analysts described as a politically motivated act rather than financially driven theft.
The hackers accused Nobitex of facilitating sanction evasion and supporting the Iranian regime’s financing of terrorist organisations. On 19 June, the group move to publish the exchange’s source code and internal data. Nobitex acknowledged the breach on X, stating it had taken its website and app offline to assess the extent of the compromise.
Blockchain analysis firms, including Elliptic and Chainalysis, confirmed the destruction of the funds and noted that some wallet addresses featured anti-IRGC messages.

name: Chinese-Speaking Threat Actors Used SuperCard Malware To Steal Bank Data From Android Users In Russia In May 2025
description: Russian cybersecurity researchers detected the first domestic SuperCard malware attacks in May 2025, which involved a modified version of legitimate near field communication (NFC) software to steal bank data. The attackers, reportedly Chinese-speaking actors, used social engineering techniques to trick victims into downloading the malware, disguising it as a legitimate app. Once installed, the malware identified the payment system used by the victim and allowed criminals to exploit the data for fraudulent transactions. F6, a Moscow-based cybersecurity firm, reported on 17 June 2025 that the total losses from NFCGate variants in Russia amounted to 432 million rubles (approximately 5.5 million USD) in the first quarter of 2025, with over 175,000 Android devices infected. The malware was first spotted in Italy in April 2025 and was reported by Italian security company Cleafy.

name: Israeli Hacker Group Predatory Sparrow Destroyed Data and Disrupted Services at Sepah Bank in Iran on 17 June 2025
description: On 17 June 2025, the hacker group Predatory Sparrow, also known as Gonjeshke Darande, conducted a disruptive operation against Bank Sepah, one of Iran's leading public banks. The attack, which occurred during a military escalation between Iran and Israel, targeted the bank's infrastructure, causing disruptions to its online services and disabling ATMs across the country. The hackers claimed responsibility for the attack on social media, stating that they had destroyed all the bank's data in a coordinated attack. The group accused Bank Sepah of circumventing international sanctions and financing Iran's nuclear and missile programs. The Central Bank of Iran denied that the banking system had been affected, but widespread reports suggested otherwise. Overlapping strategic interests as well as the group's advanced capabilities and observed concern for proportionality in its disruptive operations have led to speculations that the group is associated with Israeli intelligence services.

name: Russian state-sponsored hacking group Sandworm Used PathWiper Malware To Destroy Critical Infrastructure Organization In Ukraine on 5 June 2025
description: On 5 June 2025, Cisco Talos researchers reported that a Russia-linked threat actor targeted a critical infrastructure organization in Ukraine with a new destructive malware dubbed PathWiper. The attackers utilized a legitimate endpoint administration tool to deploy the malware, indicating they had access to the administrative console. Cisco Talos researchers noted similarities between PathWiper and HermeticWiper, a destructive tool observed in earlier targeting of critical infrastructure in Ukraine that had been attributed to the Russia-nexus group Sandworm with medium to high confidence. Based on these overlaps, Talos considers it highly likely that a Russian state-backed actor was responsible for the PathWiper campaign. The PathWiper malware scanned and identified all connected storage, including network drives, then spawned threads to overwrite key disk artifacts and files with random data, targeting NTFS structures like MBR, MFT, and others. The malicious code dismounted volumes before wiping and replaced the contents of artifacts related to the file system with random data generated on the fly. The attackers executed commands from the administrative console as BAT files, which ran a malicious VBScript that dropped and executed the PathWiper payload.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Took Down Website of French Prime Minister and Mayor of Pau François Bayrou on 25 May 2025
description: On 25 May 2025, the website of François Bayrou, the Prime Minister of France and Mayor of Pau, was taken offline by unknown actors. As of 28 May, the site remained inaccessible. Bayrou denounced the incident, stating that it was aimed at preventing him from publishing information in the public interset and that he would file a complaint. Shortly before the incident, Bayrou had sought to publish documents through the website intended to exculpate him from accusations that he had interferred in investgations of the Bétharram case. The case centered on a series of reports about sexual abuse and physical violence at Notre-Dame de Bétharram, an educational institution run by a Jesuit congregation. Claims that Bayrou obstructed investigative efforts stem from his tenure as Minister of Education, when he was ultimately responsible for overseeing an initial inquiry into the allegations in 1996. Reports of abuse remained under criminal investigation as of 2025, with the Pau Public Prosecutor's Office having registered 200 complaints. On 14 May 2025, Bayrou had appeared at a hearing before the National Assembly to refute these allegations. Documents Bayrou had planned to release through the website included his opening statement and a chronology of events in the case.

name: Taiwanese Hacking Group Targeted Unnamed Chinese Technology Company on 20 May 2025
description: Around 20 May 2025, a hacker group, allegedly operating from Taiwan, infiltrated the back-end systems of an unidentified Chinese technology company based in the southeastern city of Guangzhou. The incident was disclosed by the Guangzhou Municipal Public Security Bureau's Tianhe District Branch on 20 May 2025. Following an investigation, the same office released a statement on 27 May that held a hacking group with purported links to Taiwan's Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) responsible for the intrusion. According to these findings, the hacking group used relatively unsophisticated techniques such as mass phishing emails, exploiting publicly known vulnerabilities, and brute-force password attacks. The group's self-developed Trojan programs were described as poorly coded, leaving behind traces that facilitated tracking and investigation by Chinese authorities. In a statement to Reuters, Taiwan's National Security Bureau responded to the accusations by claiming that the Communist Party of China manipulated "inaccurate information to confuse the outside world, so as to cover up the related cyber hacking acts".

name: Chinese State-Sponsored Hacking Group UNC5221 Exploited N-Day Ivanti EPMM Flaw to Breach Government Agencies and Critical Infrastructure in Europe and the US since 15 May 2025
description: Chinese hackers exploited a remote code execution flaw in Ivanti Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM) to breach high-profile organizations worldwide, including UK National Health Service institutions, National healthcare-pharma provider in North America, and US medical device manufacturer, among others, by leveraging an exploit chain to execute code remotely on Ivanti EPMM version 12.5.0.0 and earlier via specially crafted API requests. EPMM centrally manages the security of enterprise mobile devices. The campaign developed initial access through the joint exploitation of two vulnerabilities, CVE-2025-4427 and CVE-2025-4428. The first exploitation attempts were detected on 15 May 2025, the day that Ivanti disclosed the two vulnerabilities. In a report originally published on 21 May, EclecticIQ attributed the activity with high confidence to the UTA0178/UNC5221 cluster, which is considered an Ivanti specialist. EclecticIQ subsequently revised this report, replacing references to UNC5221 with a broader attribution assessment that linked the activities to a "China-nexus espionage group".

name: Chinese Threat Actor UAT-6382 Breached Multiple Local Governing Bodies in the United States by Exploiting a Zero-Day Vulnerability in January 2025
description: Chinese-speaking hackers, identified as UAT6382, breached multiple local governing bodies across the United States by exploiting a now-patched Trimble Cityworks zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2025-0994) in January 2025. The hackers used a Rust-based malware loader to deploy Cobalt Strike beacons and VSHell malware, as well as web shells and custom malicious tools written in Chinese, to gain long-term persistent access to the compromised systems. The campaign was first observed by Cisco Talos, which reported that the hackers expressed a clear interest in pivoting to systems related to utilities management. The vulnerability was patched by Trimble in early February 2025, and the US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) added it to its catalogue of actively exploited vulnerabilities, ordering federal agencies to patch their systems within three weeks.

The incident came shortly after the president criticised foreign involvement in domestic matters, following the expulsion of several Kenyan activists who had entered the country to observe a high-profile treason trial.
Although access to X was reportedly available in some parts of the country the following day, its nationwide status remained uncertain.

The company has established a $20 million reward for information on the attackers and will reimburse customers who were tricked into sending funds to the attackers. The incident is estimated to cost Coinbase between $180 million and $400 million. The breach was disclosed in an SEC filing and reported by various news outlets, including Coinbase's blog post on 15 May 2025.

name: Scattered Spider Suspected to Have Deployed DragonForce Ransomware against British Multinational Retailer Marks & Spencer in the United Kingdom following a breach in February 2025
description:
On 22 April, the British retailer Marks & Spencer (M&S) confirmed that it was managing a cyber incident in a filing with the London Stock Exchange. The company subsequently reported disruptions to its systems, including its Sparks loyalty rewards app, that led to the suspension of online orders on 25 April. In the week following the incident, stores faced supply shortages with some food shelves remaining empty.
The attackers, believed to be a criminal gang, were holding the company to ransom, and M&S was forced to resort to pen and paper to honour loyalty rewards. In a conversation with the BBC on 2 May, the ransomware-as-a-service group DragonForce claimed responsibility for targeting M&S.
Already on 28 April, unnamed sources had indicated to the technology news portal Bleeping Computer that a loose network of criminals tracked as Scattered Spider, acting as affiliates of DragonForce, may have deployed its malicious encryption software. The threat actors are suspected to have breached M&S through the use of social engineering techniques, possibly as early as February 2025. According to this report by Bleeping Computer, the threat actors obtained a credential database for Active Directory Services, allowing them to gain widespread access to M&S networks and to deploy the DragonFroce ransomware to virtual machines of M&S on 24 April.
On 13 March, M&S announced that a limited set of customer data had been compromised, though there was no indication at the time that the information had been leaked. Data included cumstomers’ names, email and home address, phone number, date of birth, online order history as well as partial payment card details.
In the three weeks following the disclosure of the incident, the company's share price dropped by as much as 15% in value. As of 20 May, online orders remained paused without a clear timeline for resuming services. Analysts at the Bank of America estimated that M&S had been sustaining 43 million GBP of lost sales per week.
In a statement from 4 May also addressing a ransomware attack against the supermarket chain Co-op and an attempted compromise of the department store Harrods, the UK National Cyber Security Centre announced its ongoing efforts to establish whether the incidents are part of a “concerted campaign by a single actor or whether there is no link between them at all.“
On 10 July 2025, the National Crime Agency announced that four people have been arrested in the UK as part of a National Crime Agency investigation into incidents targeting M&S, Co-op and Harrods.
