name: Multiple Chinese State-Sponsored Threat Actors Infiltrated Critical Infastructure in the UK, US and Saudi Arabia in April 2025
description: EclecticIQ analysts assess that, in April 2025, China-nexus nation-state APTs launched high-tempo exploitation campaigns against critical infrastructure networks by targeting SAP NetWeaver Visual Compose. EclecticIQ attributed some of the activities with high confidence to Chinese state-backed APTs CL-STA-0048 and UNC5174. Actors leveraged CVE-2025-31324, an unauthenticated file upload vulnerability that enables remote code execution (RCE). The intrusions were discovered through publicly exposed directory (opendir) found on attacker-controlled infrastructure, which contained detailed event logs capturing operations across multiple compromised systems. Analysis of the open-dir infrastructure reveals a targeted campaign against critical sectors across multiple countries including critical natural gas distribution networks, water and integrated waste management utilities in UK, advanced medical device manufacturing plants, upstream oil and gas exploration and production companies in US. It also targets government ministries responsible for investment strategy and financial regulation in Saudi Arabia.

name: Unknown Threat Actors targeted Multiple Paraguayan Government Institutions in early May 2025
description:
On 9 May 2025, Paraguay’s Ministry of Information and Communication Technologies (Mitic) confirmed a widespread cyberattack affecting at least eleven public institutions. Detected by CERT-PY, the incidents involved both website defacement and unauthorized access to sensitive data. While many of the affected entities experienced changes to the appearance of their websites, the breach at the Ministry of Health raised particular concern due to the confirmed leak of personal data from the national vaccination system.
Mitic Minister Gustavo Villate stated that the attackers exploited vulnerabilities to access systems and that personal health data — including information provided by citizens during the vaccination process — was compromised. He described the breach as one of the most serious data leaks the country has faced, with possible consequences ranging from identity theft to financial fraud.
The institutions targeted include the Ministry of Health, Ministry of Justice, Chamber of Deputies, Presidency of the Republic, and several others across key sectors. In response, Mitic activated its cyber incident response protocols, launched an investigation, and began working with affected entities to contain the impact and identify the full scope of the breach. On 13 May, another intrusion targeted a Paraguayan institution within the scope of the large-scale campaign against Paraguay - this time, the website of SENABICO (the National Secretariat for the Administration of Seized and Confiscated Assets) was defaced in an attack claimed by the group "CyberTeam," though it was unclear if the same group was behind the activities targeting other Paraguayan institutions.

name: Pro-Russian Hacking Group NoName057(16) Launched a DDoS Attack on UK Local Councils and the Association for Police and Crime Commissioners on 6 May 2025
description:
On 6 May 2025, a pro-Russian hacking group called NoName057(16) claimed to have targeted several UK websites, including local councils, the Harwich International Port, National Highways, and the Association for Police and Crime Commissioners, using a DDoS attack, temporarily making some websites inaccessible, but with limited success, as reported by various organizations, including Arun district council and National Highways. The websites were back online after a few hours.


From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: NoName057(16) Carried out a DDoS Attack against the website of the City Council of Stuttgart in Germany on 29 April 2025
description:
On 29 April 2025, the German city of Stuttgart was targeted by a DDoS attack, which caused the city council's website to be temporarily unavailable. The attack, attributed to the Russian group NoName057(16), started on 29 April, and the city administration blocked traffic to the site offline citing technical problems. The site was accessible again by the afternoon. A city spokesperson confirmed the incident, clarifying that the website had been flooded with requests and traffic from a number of bots or a botnet, causing the servers to fail.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Canadian ultility companies Nova Scotia Power and Emera on 25 April 2025
description: On 25 April 2025, Canadian electric utility Nova Scotia Power and its parent company Emera suffered an intrusion that impacted their IT systems and networks. The companies discovered unauthorized access to certain parts of their Canadian network and servers, and immediately activated their incident response and business continuity protocols. They engaged leading third-party cybersecurity experts and took actions to contain and isolate the affected servers. Law enforcement officials were notified. The incident disrupted IT systems, including customer support lines and an online customer portal. On 2 May, Nova Scotia Power disclosed that it had temporarily suspended billing. As of 28 April 2025, the companies were still working to restore services. The incident was disclosed to the public by Nova Scotia Power through a social media post on 28 April 2025, and by Emera through a press statement on 25 April 2025. Although the companies did not share technical details about the incident, experts speculate that it may have been a ransomware attack. At the time of reporting, no ransomware gang had publicly claimed responsibility for the compromise. In an update from 14 Mai 2025, Nova Scotia Power confimed that on or around March 19, 2025, certain customer information stored on the impacted servers was accessed and later taken by an unauthorized third party. he types of impacted personal information varied by individual customer and depended, in part, on the information provided by each customer. This may have included one or more of the following: name, phone number, email address, mailing and service addresses, Nova Scotia Power program participation information, date of birth, and customer account history (such as power consumption, service requests, customer payment, billing, and credit history, and customer correspondence), driver’s license number, and Social Insurance Number. For some of our customers, bank account numbers (for pre-authorized payment) may also have been impacted, if this information was provided by these customers.

name: Alleged US Cyber Operations Target Asian Winter Games and Critical Infrastructure in China in February 2025
description:
In February 2025, during the 9th Asian Winter Games held from 7 to 14 February in Harbin, Heilongjiang Province, Chinese authorities reported a coordinated cyber operation targeting both the event’s digital infrastructure and critical sectors across the province. According to the National Computer Virus Emergency Response Center (CVERC), the attacks involved a range of techniques, including SQL injection, arbitrary file read vulnerabilities and HTTP header spoofing. The malicious activity reportedly affected information systems related to event registration, scheduling, and logistics, as well as infrastructure in the energy, transportation, water, telecommunications and research sectors.
CVERC stated that the cyber operations were traced to infrastructure allegedly linked to the United States and attributed the activity to the US government, citing indicators such as behavioral patterns, time zones and language artefacts. While Chinese officials noted that the attacks did not cause serious disruption, they indicated that the transmission of encrypted commands had been detected, which appeared to be intended to activate pre-implanted backdoors.
On 15 April 2025, the Harbin Public Security Bureau publicly identified three alleged NSA operatives said to be involved in the campaign and issued warrants along with monetary rewards for information leading to their apprehension. The HPSB also claimed that the three individuals were involved in unspecified compromise attempts against Huawei. Without providing any further substantiation, Chinese state media noted findings by technical teams that purportedly pointed to the involvement of the University of California and Virginia Tech in the campaign reportedly targeting the Asian Winter Games. Although the Chinese government has presented attribution with institutional backing - including CVERC, the Foreign Ministry, and law enforcement agencies - no independent confirmation of the attack’s effectiveness or the identities of those involved has been made publicly available. The investigation remains ongoing.

name: Baltimore City Public Schools System (US) was the victim of a ransomware incident by Cloak ransomware gang on 13 February 2025
description: On 13 February 2025, the Baltimore City Public Schools system was affected by a ransomware attack launched by the Cloak ransomware gang. The attack resulted in the theft of information belonging to roughly 25,000 individuals, including current and former employees, volunteers, and contractors. Files also were related to around 1.5% of the student population (roughly 1,150 students). The stolen records likely contained Social Security numbers, drivers license numbers, or passport numbers for employees, while student data stolen included call logs, absenteeism records, and/or maternity status for students. The incident was reported by various news outlets, including The Baltimore Banner and 11 News WBALTV, and was confirmed by the school system in a data breach alert published on 22 April 2025. The school system did not pay a ransom. Law enforcement was contacted, and cybersecurity firms were hired to help the school system recover from the attack. Victims were offered two years of credit monitoring services, and a call centre was set up to answer questions about the incident.

name: Unknown threat actors are distributing backdoors masqueraded as secure networking software updates to target Russian organisations in government, finance, and industrial sectors in campaign detected in April 2025
description:
In April 2025, Kaspersky observed an unidentified threat actor targeting various large organizations in Russia, spanning the government, finance, and industrial sectors. 
The threat actor used a backdoor, masquerading as a secure networking software update for ViPNet, distributed inside LZH archives, which contained malicious files, including msinfo32.exe, which loaded the backdoor into memory. The backdoor allowed attackers to steal files from infected computers and launch additional malicious activities. Kaspersky solutions detected this threat as HEURTrojan.Win32.Loader.gen. The ViPNet developer confirmed intrusion activity against some of their users and issued security updates and recommendations for customers.

name: Threat actors with suspected links to Russia or Belarus targeted systems of Civic Platform in Poland on 2 April 2025
description:
On 2 April 2025, unidentified threat actors targeted the computer system of the Civic Platform, a Polish political party led by Prime Minister Donald Tusk.

Tusk identified an "eastern source," possibly Russia or Belarus for the malicious activity and linked the incident to "foreign election interference." Several leaders of Civic Platform, including the head of the Prime Minister's Chancellery, Jan Grabiec, also confirmed the incident, which allegedly affected the computers of both the Civic Platform office employees and the election headquarters; he also asserted that there was "concrete evidence pointing to the modus operandi of the Eastern secret services," without providing further details.

Grabiec stated that the attackers utilized the Civic Platform's website to access the systems used by the party's electoral apparatus, which are used to coordinate the presidential campaign. Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Digital Affairs, Krzysztof Gawkowski, stated that the police were working to investigate, while no data theft was detected due to rapid action by the Polish services. 

The attack occurred just before the first round of the presidential elections, scheduled for 18 May 2025, and was seen as an attempt to influence the campaign and destabilize electoral preparations. The Polish Minister of Digital Affairs, Krzysztof Gawkowski, later confirmed that the attack was carried out by groups related to the Russian Federation and Belarus.

name: Ransomware group PLAY attacked Regionale Verkehrsbetriebe Baden-Wettingen in Switzerland on 21 March 2025
description: On 21 March 2025, the ransomware group PLAY targeted the Regionale Verkehrsbetriebe Baden-Wettingen (RVBW), demanding a ransom and threatening to publish stolen data by 6 April 2025. The RVBW refused to pay the ransom and isolated their IT infrastructure, cutting off external connections and conducting a security check on all computers. The attack resulted in the encryption of some data, but no customer data was affected. The incident had no impact on the RVBW's bus operations, but some digital signs at bus stops were temporarily out of order. The RVBW is working with the Swiss federal criminal police to investigate the attack and improve their IT security measures. The incident was first reported on 3 April 2025.

name: Unknown hackers defaced the webpage of Moscow's subway system on 31 March 2025
description:
On 31 March 2025, the website of the Moscow subway system was defaced and the system itself disrupted by unknown hackers, possibly in retaliation for an attack against Ukraine's national railway operator the week prior.

The website of the Moscow metro system displayed a message made to look as if it had been authored by Ukraine's national railway operator, Ukzaliznytsia, which itself was the victim of a cyberattack the week before. The post mimicked the defacement message left on the Ukrainian railway system's website. According to transport officials, the website of Moscow's metro system was inaccessible due to "technical maintenance". Officials did not confirm outside interference. This lack of public confirmation contrats with reports by Roskomnadzor, Russia's internet regulator, of disruptions and observations from passengers on 31 March about service disruptions that prevented them from topping up their subway cards remotely or recharging cards at stations. The transport authority did not immediately provide any assessment as to whether these disruptions had a connection to the defacement.

name: Unknown threat actors stole patient data from Oracle Health in the United States sometime in January 2025
description: Sometime after 22 January 2025, unknown threat actors compromised servers of the US software company Oracle, specifically older servers belonging to Cerner, a US healthcare IT company acquired by Oracle in 2022 and later renamed to Oracle Health. The threat actor copied patient data to an outside location, in an attempt to extort multiple medical providers in the United States. The FBI launched a probe into the incident as reported by Bloomberg News on 28 March 2025, citing a person familiar with the matter. Oracle became aware of the breach around 20 February 2025 and notified customers about the breach. According to the notification, the breach affected data on former Cerner servers that had not yet been migrated to Oracle's cloud storage. At the time of reporting it remained unclear how many patient records were affected and which healthcare providers were subsequently targeted as part of the extortion scheme. In January 2026, Munson Healthcare, another US-based healthcare provider, confirmed that its data was stolen through the breach of Cerner.

name: Unknown hacker(s) stole $12.9 million worth of Ethereum coins from Abracadabra Finance in the United States on 25 March 2025
description:
On the morning of Tuesday, 25 March 2025, unknown hacker(s) stole approximately $13 million worth of digital currency from Abracadabra Finance, a decentralised cryptocurrency platform. Only after the transactions were executed did Abracadabra catch the exploit.

The company acknowledged the incident on social media, stating the issue was sourced back to a product called "cauldrons," which are isolated lending markets that allow users to borrow against various cryptocurrencies. In the wake of the theft, Abracadabra initiated an investigation alongside experts. The blockchain security company Chainalysis tracked the stolen funds, which included 6,260 ETH ($12.9 million). Abracadabra Finance offered a bug bounty to the hacker of 20% of the stolen funds.

name: An individual hacker accessed and defaced New York University in the United States on 22 March 2025
description:
On 22 March 2025, an individual hacker, claiming to be part of a larger hacking group with the acronym CNE, which contains a racial slur [and is therefore only referenced by the acronym here], accessed NYU's IT systems without authorisation, took control of the systems directing web traffic to NYU's website, and defaced the NYU homepage, exposing admissions data on approximately 1 million students. The hacker claimed to have redacted personal information but actually leaked full names, addresses, phone numbers, grade point averages, email addresses, and citizenship status. NYU spokesperson John Beckman informed the public about the incident, stating that the university's IT team worked with a cybersecurity consultant to regain control of the system and redirect traffic back to its real website in less than three hours. The incident was reported to law enforcement authorities, and the university is working to provide notice to anyone whose personal information was subject to unauthorised access. The hacker claimed the intrusion was in response to the Supreme Court decision in 2023 striking down affirmative action, aiming to prove that schools are not abiding by the ruling.

[Note on threat actor identification: The full name of the allegedly associated hacker is listed in the initiator category solely for documentation purposes. The European Repository of Cyber Incidents strongly opposes reproducing offensive terms that may form part of the name by which threat actors refer to themselves.]

name: Unknown state-sponsored threat actors launched a zero-day exploit chain attack in "Operation ForumTroll," targeting media outlets and educational institutions in Russia via phishing emails in March 2025
description:
In mid-March 2025, Kaspersky detected a wave of infections by previously unknown and "highly sophisticated" malware which was utilised in an ongoing phishing campaign allegedly by state-sponsored actors targeting Russian entities, particularly educational institutions and media outlets. Kaspersky dubbed the operation "ForumTroll" due to its targeting patterns and phishing theme.

The attackers sent personalized phishing emails related to a scientific and expert forum called "Primakov Readings;" the emails contained malicious links that, when clicked, infected the victims' devices via a Google Chrome zero-day exploit chain, specifically exploiting the CVE-2025-2783 vulnerability, which Google fixed on 25 March 2025, following disclosure by Kaspersky. The malware was designed to run in conjunction with an additional exploit for remote code execution.

On 27 October 2025, Kaspersky published further reporting on Operation ForumTroll, stating that it is like that tools and infrastructure used by the threat actor are linked to the Italian spyware company Memento Labs (formerly operating as Hacking Team).

name: Russian hacktivist group NoName057(16) launched DDoS attacks against Belgian government websites in "OpBelgium" at the end of March 2025
description:
The Russia-linked hacktivist group NoName057(16) launched DDoS-type attacks against several official Belgian websites, including the e-government portal MyGov.be and the website of the regional Walloon Parliament, on 24 March 2025. On its Telegram channel, the group declared that the operation was carried out in response to a new aid package of €1 billion adopted by Belgium in support of Ukraine. Belgian Defence Minister Theo Francken pledged the package to Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelensky during a meeting in early March. The Walloon Parliament stated that the disruption attempt only briefly interfered with access to the website. 

The following day, on 25 March 2025, NoName057(16) launched several more DDoS attacks on Belgian websites, including the websites of the Federal Public Service Economy, the European Sea Ports Organisation (ESPO), the Municipality of Sint-Genesius-Rode, the Municipality of Wemmel, the Municipality of Wezembeek-Oppem, and the City of Enghien. 

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Anti-Iranian regime hacktivists Lab Dookhtegan disrupted communications on 116 Iranian cargo ships in Iran on 21 March 2025
description: On 21 March 2025, the supposedly Iranian, anti-regime hacktivist group Lab Dookhtegan claimed to have carried out a cyber attack that disrupted communications on 116 Iranian cargo ships. The attack targeted the communication network of two Iranian companies, National Iranian Tanker Company (NITC) and Islamic Republic of Iran Shipping Lines (IRISL), which are sanctioned by the US Department of the Treasury, the UK, and the European Union. The companies are responsible for supplying munitions to Houthi rebels in Yemen. Lab Dookhtegan did not provide specific information on its uses of TTPs, but as Cydome noted, open-source reporting indicates the group likely exploited vulnerabilities in the maritime satellite communication systems that these ships rely on. The hackers interfered with communications between ships, ports, and external third parties, affecting 50 NITC vessels and 66 IRISL vessels. Cydome further state that based on the information presented by the hackers, they were able to take full control of the communications system, with elevated credentials, full access to the ships’ networks and ability to remotely execute malicious code. The owners would take weeks to restore their communications systems fully. The high degree of automation and coordination in the attack suggests advanced capabilities, including prior reconnaissance of the fleet's IT infrastructure. Iran International also reported on the purported event.

The attack was first reported by local media, including El Observador and Underline, and was later confirmed by President Orsi in statements to the press. On 30 March 2025, an 18-year-old was charged with 180 days of pretrial detention for hacking the websites of state agencies and private companies including the website of the DINACIA.

name: CrazyHunter launched ransomware operation against Changhua Christian Hospital (Taiwan) in early March 2025
description:
Following another incident at Mackay Memorial Hospital in Taiwan on 9 February 2025, CrazyHunter ransomware gang, which primarily targets Taiwanese organisations (specifically healthcare), launched another ransomware operation against Changhua Christian Hospital in Taiwan, in early March 2025.

While the specifics of data stolen or encrypted were not clear, TeamT5 Threat Intelligence linked the attack to CrazyHunter's Mackay attack, which involved the encryption and exfiltration of data, as well as the disruption of hundreds of computers; similarly, the Taiwanese Ministry of Health and Welfare indicated that systems had been infiltrated by CrazyHunter, without clarifying further specifics. In the meantime, the Ministry of Health and Welfare coordinated with the Cybersecurity Administration to repair damaged systems; as of 4 March, secondary systems remained down as a result of the attack. The Ministry of Health and Welfare also referred to the back-to-back incidents as "systematic." 

It should be noted that the exact date of the incident was not clarified, including the actual month; certain Taiwanese publications linked it to the end of February (28.02), while other publications and the TeamT5 report claimed it took place in "early March."

name: CrazyHunter steals data of 16.6 million patients from Taiwanese Mackay Memorial Hospital on 9 February 2025
description:
On 9 February 2025, the ransomware organisation CrazyHunter, which has been observed targeting primarily Taiwanese targets in healthcare, attacked Mackay Memorial Hospital in Taipei, according to a notice from the Taiwanese Ministry of Health and Welfare on 11 February. As a result, 500 computers were disrupted and allegedly over 16.6 million patient data was encrypted and stolen (32.5GB) from Mackay and its partner organisations across Taiwan. 

On 28 February, an allegedly CrazyHunter-affiliated user of a hacker forum claimed to have the data, which was subsequently published and included names, ID numbers, mobile phone numbers, home addresses, and medical records included within this patient data. However, on 13 March 2025, another group named "Babuk" published 120GB of data allegedly from the hospital as well.

TeamT5, a Taiwan-based cybersecurity company, promptly responded to the incident at Mackay and provided IoC intelligence, assisting the hospital in completing thorough scans of its environments. TeamT5 also linked the incident to CrazyHunter, though has not commented on Babuk's claims.

name: Pro-Palestinian hacktivist group Dark Storm conducted DDoS attacks against X in the United States on 10 March 2025
description:
On Monday, 10 March 2025, the Dark Storm hacktivist group, a pro-Palestinian group that launched in 2023, claimed responsibility for a massive cyberattack against X (fka Twitter), causing multiple worldwide outages. According to security analysts, Dark Storm conducted five DDoS attacks against X, in varying lengths; the group posted screenshots and links to the checkhost.net site as proof of the attack on their Telegram channel. Furthermore, external cyber experts such as those with Cisco's ThousandEyes confirmed that network conditions also pointed to the attack being DDoS. 

X owner Elon Musk confirmed that the outages were caused by a "massive cyberattack," alleging that the attack was carried out by a large, coordinated group or a country; later in the day, Musk linked the attack to Ukraine in an interview with Fox Business, saying that Ukrainian IPs were observed in the attack but offering no evidence to support his claims. However, other independent security analysts argued that the severity of the attack was likely due to many of the X origin servers not being properly secured by Cloudflare DDoS protection, and further contributing to the severity was the fact that the attack was likely from a Mirai botnet. Such a botnet, comprised of infected, old (unpatched) cameras and DVRs all acting together to launch the DDoS attacks, would also contradict Musk's claims that Ukrainian actors could have been behind the attack since Ukrainian IPs were observed; a botnet would have IPs from many places, not just Ukraine. Importantly, one anonymous security researcher from a "prominent firm" pointed out that Ukraine was not even in the top 20 IP address origins involved in the X attacks. Interestingly, some security analysts, such as those at Orange Cyberdefense, believe there may be a link to the Russian Federation, as evidenced by previous targeting patterns and coalitions built with Russian groups such as Killnet, Bluenet Russia, or the (potentially Russian) Anonymous Sudan group.

As a result of the attack, X enabled DDoS protections from Cloudflare, which now shows a captcha when suspicious IP addresses connect to the site. The outages were also tracked by Downdetector, which reported tens of thousands of user reports from the US and over 8,000 reports from the UK. Alp Toker, director of Netblocks, suggested that the outages were consistent with a denial of service attack targeting X's infrastructure at scale.

On 5 March 2025, unknown hackers exploited a vulnerability in 1inch’s older smart contract, stealing $5 million. The attack targeted a third-party market maker, TrustedVolumes, rather than individual users. After negotiations, the hacker agreed to return most of the stolen funds in exchange for a bug bounty.

name: Russian hacktivist group NoName057(16) launched a DDoS attack on several Spanish websites between 3-5 March in 2025
description:
Between 3-5 March in 2025, the Russian hacktivist group NoName057(16) launched a DDoS attack on several Spanish websites including Toledo City Council, Basque Government, the Diputación de Bizkaia, the Junta de Comunidades de Castilla y León, the Port of Barcelona, the EMT of Madrid, Chamber of Commerce of Madrid, the City of Valladolid, San Sebastian, Barakaldo, Ayuntamiento de Donostia, Ayuntamiento de Irun, Diputación de Gipuzkoa, Ayuntamiento de Hondarribia, Ayuntamiento de Zaragoza, Tranvía de Zaragoza, Provincia de Huesca, Comunidad Autónoma de Castilla-La Mancha, Diputación de Albacete, Diputación de Girona, Diputación de Ciudad Real, Diputación de Toledo. On X, the hackers claimed that the attack was a response to a statement of Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez expressing support for Ukraine.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Unknown actors are operating an ongoing campaign targeting industrial organisations and governments in the Asia-Pacific region, known as "Operation SalmonSlalom"
description:
On 24 February 2025, Kaspersky reported a new, ongoing campaign targeting unnamed industrial organisations in the Asia-Pacific region, with a particular focus on Chinese-speaking targets, as evidenced by the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) utilised, such as leveraging Chinese-language search engines and apps. 

Kaspersky was initially tipped off to the campaign through reports from victims of attacks on government agencies and industrial organisations in the APAC region, and upon investigating, realised that the attackers were utilising a multi-stage, long-term, complex procedure to install malware (FatalRAT), which was delivered via zip files in Telegram, WeChat, and by email. The zip archives were disguised as invoices or legitimate tax filing applications for Chinese-speaking individuals and contained the FatalRAT first-stage loader packed using tools make detection and analysis more difficult. According to Kaspersky, the targets were primarily from Taiwan, Malaysia, China, Japan, Thailand, South Korea, Singapore, the Philippines, Vietnam, and Hong Kong; in any case, the vast majority were from China, while the victims included targets in IT, telecoms, healthcare, construction, manufacturing, governments, logistics and transportation, and finance.

While Kaspersky and other IT companies are thus far unable to identify the specific group behind the attack, Kaspersky attributes it with medium confidence to Chinese-speaking initiators.

name: Rey, a member of the HellCat ransomware group, breached the Romanian branch of French telecommunications provider Orange Group, in February 2025
description:
In or around early February 2025, a hacker, known by the alias Rey and a member of the HellCat ransomware group, breached the systems of Orange Group, a French telecommunications operator. Rey allegedly maintained persistent access within the Orange systems for roughly a month, before finally exfiltrating data on 23 February 2025 and leaking it days later after a ransom was not paid. The affected data was mostly from the Romanian branch of Orange and includes 380,000 unique email addresses, as well as 12,000 (6.5GB) of administrative documents such as invoices and contracts, as well as customer and employee information.

The breach was carried out by exploiting compromised credentials and vulnerabilities in the company's Jira software and internal portals. BleepingComputer reported on the incident and received a statement from Orange Group, who confirmed the breach and stated that they were committed to protecting the data and interests of their employees, customers, and partners. The company also stated that there was no impact on customer operations and that they were cooperating with relevant authorities to address the matter.

name: Pro-Ukraine hacktivist group Ukrainian Cyber Alliance attacked the Russian microfinance company CarMoney in Russia on 19 February 2025
description:
On 19 February 2025, the Cyber Resistance / Ukrainian Cyber Alliance, a group of pro-Ukraine hacktivists, carried out a cyberattack on CarMoney, a Russian microfinance company. CarMoney confirmed earlier this week that it had suffered a cyber incident, forcing it to shut down all systems after attackers sent spam messages to customers claiming the company was closing its business, donating its proceeds to charity and writing off all debts. The organization has not attributed the attack to Ukrainian hackers or disclosed the full extent of the breach.  The hackers said their attack destroyed CarMoney’s infrastructure and compromised “terabytes of data.”
While CarMoney has assured clients and investors that no personal data was affected, the hackers claim they obtained information on a large number of borrowers, including members of Russian military units and intelligence officers.
The hackers shared screenshots on Telegram but the data’s  authenticity could not be independently verified. While CarMoney’s website remains operational, customers have reported ongoing payment service disruptions and difficulties accessing their accounts. 

CarMoney, one of Russia’s largest microfinance firms, issues short-term loans secured by vehicles. According to independent Russian media, Putin’s ex-wife, Lyudmila Ocheretnaya, controls the company through a network of offshore entities.

name: Unknown threat actors hacked  crypto-focused Infini Neobank on 24 February 2025 stealing USD Coins worth $49.5 million
description: On 24 February 2025, Infini Neobank was hacked, resulting in the theft of $49.5 million USDC, which was then transferred to the Ethereum network. The attacker traded the stolen USDC for Ethereum-based DAI and purchased 17.696 ETH, later transferring it to the address 0xfcc8...6e49. The attack was likely carried out by obtaining a private key, with the private key to the address 0xc49b...e3e1 possibly being leaked. The stolen assets were transferred to wallets associated with TornadoCash, an anonymization protocol. An Infini developer, who secretly retained executive authority after the project was completed, is thought to be behind the attack. An official police report has been filed, and a member of Infini's team claimed that the engineer who committed the theft had been identified and reported to the police.

name: Criminal actors stole financial and medical information from Rainbow District School Board in Ontario, Canada, on 7 February 2025
description:
The Rainbow District School Board in the Greater Sudbury region of Ontario, Canada, was breached by cyber criminals in early February 2025, resulting in the theft of sensitive personal information. The breach, detected on 7 February 2025, affected people who worked for the board as early as 2010 and students who attended a school in the Rainbow District School Board as early as 2011. Stolen information included social insurance numbers, bank account information, and medical information belonging to staff and students. The board reported the incident to the Greater Sudbury Police Service and the Ontario Provincial Police. The incident was announced by the board on 20 February 2025.
In February 2026, the media Sudbury.com reported that a freedom of information request revealed the ransomware cyber incident affecting the Rainbow District School Board cost the board more than $680,000.

name: RansomHub targeted Riverdale Country School in the United States on 20 February 2025
description: On 20 February 2025, the cybercriminal group RansomHub claimed it had breached Riverdale Country School, an elite prep school in the Bronx in New York City and was holding its data for ransom. RansomHub, which formed in February 2024, posted the school's website along with a countdown clock on its dark web site, alleging that it had stolen 42 GB of data. The group warned that the school had just over five days to comply with its demands or it would release the data. The data potentially includes personal information about students and their families, vaccination records, academic records, health data, and staff payroll. RansomHub typically employs multiple extortion tactics against its victims, including encrypting data and exfiltrating it to servers under the group's control.

name: Qilin Ransomware Group targeted Palau Ministry of Health and Human Services in Palau on 17 February 2025
description: The Qilin Ransomware Group claimed to have compromised the Palau Ministry of Health and Human Services (MHHS) on 17 February 2025, and threatened to release internal data by 27 February. The MHHS confirmed being a victim of a cyber attack on 18 February 2025, and stated that work to determine the extent of the breach was ongoing. The ministry runs Belau National Hospital, an 80-bed facility that serves the country’s nearly 20,000 residents spread across hundreds of islands. The hospital remained open, and patients were encouraged to continue seeking care as usual. The ministry was unable to confirm whether patient data had been affected at the time. The group published some of the stolen information on 28 February. In a statement, the Health Ministry confirmed that patient data was compromised as result of the cyberattack and may include billing summaries for Belau National Hospital patients between from 2018 to 2022. Personal information like names, addresses, phone numbers and data on diagnoses and procedures were likely exposed.

name: North Korean state-sponsored Lazarus group suspected to have stolen assets worth $1.46 billion from crypto exchange Bybit through a smart contract attack on 21 February 2025
description: On 21 February 2025, the Dubai-based cryptocurrency exchange Bybit became the victim of smart contract attack, resulting in the theft of crypto assets worth $1.46 billion, the largest amount obtained in a single compromise to date. The threat actors tapped Bybit's ETH cold wallet by manipulating a transaction through a sophisticated attack that masked the signing interface and altered the underlying smart contract logic. The attacker gained control of the affected ETH cold wallet and transferred its holdings. Crypto fraud investigator ZachXBT reported that the exploiter split 10,000 ETH out of the roughly 401,346 ETH stolen to 48 addresses. The analyst was also among the first to link the compromise to the North Korean state-sponsored group Lazarus. Bybit CEO Ben Zhou confirmed the incident, stating that the exchange had enough liquidity to honor withdrawals and continue operations, with all other cold wallets being secure and client funds safe. Zhou speculated that the source of the compromise could have been the wallet provider Safe, which Bybit uses for its Ethereum cold wallet. Safe wrote that they had not found evidence that the official Safe frontend was compromised, but temporarily paused certain functionalities out of caution. Bybit had reserve assets of over $16 billion before the hack and secured bridge loans to cover 80% of the stolen ETH. Ethereum temporarily lost 4% of its value when news of the incidents became public. On 26 February 2025 the FBI also attributed the ByBit hack to TraderTraitor (Lazarus).

name: Chinese cybercriminals hacked into Kuwaiti telecom and bank networks to steal bank account data and seize funds using "advanced electronic devices" in early February 2025
description:
A group of Chinese cybercriminals conducted large-scale cyberattacks targeting telecommunications towers and banks in Kuwait at least since 13 February 2025 but potentially earlier, leading to the Kuwaiti government to dismantle the group on the 13th following alerts from banks and telecommunications companies.

According to the Kuwaiti Ministry of Interior, the criminal gang, composed of Chinese nationals, used advanced electronic devices planted within mobile vehicles to penetrate telecommunications networks and broadcast fraudulent messages impersonating banks, enabling them to steal bank account data and thus to seize funds. 

Security teams within the Department of Combating Cybercrime identified the source of the suspicious signals, traced it to a vehicle in the Farwaniya area, and apprehended the driver, a Chinese national. Further investigation revealed additional devices and technical tools used to analyse the compromised data, and the accused confessed to collaborating with others in hacking communication networks. The accused were referred to the relevant authorities for legal action, as reported by Al Jarida newspaper.

name: Pro-Russian group NoName057(16) Launched DDoS Attacks on Italian Ministries, Airports, Banks and Ports on 17 February 2025
description:
On 17 February 2025, the pro-Russian group NoName057(16) carried out a cyber attack on Italian banking and transport companies. The targeted companies included Intesa San Paolo bank, airports of Linate and Malpensa, the Transport Authority, and the ports of Taranto and Trieste. Further targets included the websites of ministries (Ministry of Enterprises and Made in Italy), the Financial Police (Guardia di Finanze), and certain sub-sites of the Ministries of the Interior and Finance. The attack was in response to statements made by the President of the Republic, Sergio Mattarella, on 5 February 2025, comparing Russia's aggression against Ukraine to the Third Reich project in Europe. The group used DDoS actions to conduct the attack. NoName057(16) claimed responsibility for the attack through a message on their Telegram channel. The National Cybersecurity Agency alerted the affected entities and initiated mitigation actions in collaboration with the victims' technicians. Local media reported on the incident.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Unknown Hackers Stole Personal Data of 70,000 People, Including 1,000 Elected Officials, from Caisse des Dépôts in France by Using Fraudulently Obtained Connection Identifiers on 13 February 2025
description: On 13 February 2025, the Caisse des Dépôts, a French financial institution, was the subject of a cyberattack in which the personal data of approximately 70,000 people, including 1,000 elected officials, were stolen. The attackers used fraudulently obtained connection identifiers to access the platform and remove sensitive information, including social security numbers, names, dates and cities of birth, and mailing addresses, from the Ircantec pension fund scheme. The victims were informed of the breach via email, and the Caisse des Dépôts blocked the fraudulent connections, strengthened account creation controls, and notified its partners to set up alert systems. A complaint was filed, and a report was made to the National Commission on Informatics and Liberty (CNIL) in accordance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The attack was first reported by Franceinfo and later confirmed by the Caisse des Dépôts to Figaro on 13 February 2025. Additionally, Chronopost, a subsidiary of the La Poste group, was also targeted in a separate cyberattack at the end of January 2025, resulting in the theft of personal data of 210,000 customers, including surname, first name, postal address, signature, and sometimes telephone number.

Check Point chief of staff Gil Messing and Panorays cofounder Demi BenAri reported that the attack involved bombarding the payment server with a large number of requests, causing it to crash. Messing suggested that the attack may have been carried out by a state actor, possibly with the assistance of smaller entities, and that the goal of the attack was to create a cognitive effect rather than steal data or money. The incident was reported by several customers and was disclosed by Shva, with updates provided on the company's website.

name: Unknown threat actors exploited a smart contract flaw to steal $9.5 million worth of Ethereum from zkLend in the United States on 11 February 2025
description: On 11 February 2025, decentralised money lender zkLend was breached by an unknown actor/actors who exploited a smart contract flaw, specifically a rounding error bug in the mint() function, to steal 3,600 Ethereum, worth $9.5 million as of 13 February 2025. The attack was detected on the same day, with zkLend warning on X about the cybersecurity incident. The EthSecurity Telegram channel reported that the threat actor(s) manipulated the 'lendingaccumulator' to exploit the rounding error during ztoken mint() and withdraw() to repeatedly deposit and withdraw wstETH. Starkware confirmed that the vulnerability was not part of Starknet technology but rather an application-specific bug. The threat actor(s) attempted to launder the crypto through the RailGun privacy protocol but were blocked due to protocol policies. zkLend has offered the hacker a deal to return 90% of the stolen Ethereum, keeping 10%, in exchange for not facing liability for the attack. The hacker has until 13 February 2025, at 7:00 PM EST, to respond, after which zkLend will pursue legal action.

name: Alabama man breached into the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) account on X in a SIM swapping attack in January 2024
description: On  January 2024, Eric Council Jr., a 25-year-old man from Alabama, carried out a SIM swapping attack on the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) account on X. He and his co-conspirators posted a fake announcement that Bitcoin ETFs were approved. This caused Bitcoin's price to jump up by 1,000 USD and then plummet by 2,000 USD after SEC Chairperson Gary Gensler tweeted that the announcement was fake. The SEC confirmed the next day that the SECGov X account was compromised through a SIM-swapping attack targeting the phone number of the person in charge of the X account. Council used a fraudulent identification card to impersonate the victim and gain access to the victim's cellular phone number. He was paid 50,000 USD in Bitcoin by his co-conspirators. The incident was reported by SEC Chairperson Gary Gensler on X. The Justice Department disclosed the details of the attack. Council pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit aggravated identity theft and access device fraud and is scheduled to be sentenced on 16 May 2025, facing a maximum penalty of five years in prison.

name: Pro-Russian Hacker Group NoName057(16) Launched DDoS Attacks on Seven Ukrainian Websites in Ukraine on 5 February 2025
description:
On 5 Februar 2025, a pro-Russian hacker group, Noname057(16), attacked seven Ukrainian websites, including those of companies involved in military aircraft repairs and the production of military weapons and equipment, such as Ukroboronprom, Zaporizhzhia State Aviation Repair Plant, MiGremont, Antonov JSC, and JSC Kyiv Radar Plant, by using distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks, as claimed by the group via their X account. The attack was reported by authors Jess Daly and Seven, exposing the identities of Russia's 'Digital Army' as part of Russia's relentless cyber attacks on Ukraine and its NATO Allies since Russia's full-scale invasion.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Launched a DDoS Attack on Kaveri Portal in India on 1 February 2025
description: On 1 February 2025, the Kaveri portal, operated by the Karnataka Stamps and Registration Department, was hit by a suspected Artificial Intelligence-based DDoS attack. The attack involved flooding the portal with around 20 lakh (2 000 000) searches, causing technical issues and disrupting property registration services. The portal's search strength of around 2.5 lakh (250 000) queries per day was exploited by the attackers, who used AI to pile the portal with search requests for documents such as Encumbrance Certificate (EC). The attack was detected after the portal was unable to handle the increased traffic, resulting in a significant drop in property registrations, from 8,500-9,000 daily to 556 on 1 February 2025. The Department of Egovernance was informed, and assistance was sought to track those responsible. Dayananda K A, Inspector General of Registration and Commissioner of Stamps, reported the attack to The Indian Express. The portal was fully restored by Wednesday evening, 5 February 2025, with property registration numbers returning to normal levels, reaching 7,225 with a revenue of around Rs 62.59 crore (625,9 million Rs)/ 6.94 million EURO.

name: IntelBroker Stole Documents from US-American Information Technology Company Hewlett Packard Enterprise Using API, WePay and GitHub Repositories Breach on 16 January 2025
description: On 16 January 2025, the US-American multinational information technology company Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) was informed by IntelBroker that they had stolen documents from HPE's developer environments, including source code, certificates, and personal information. IntelBroker claimed to have had access to HPE's API, WePay, and GitHub repositories for at least two days. HPE immediately launched an investigation and activated their cyber response protocols. As of 20 January 2025, HPE had not found any evidence of a security breach. The incident was reported by BleepingComputer, who was informed by HPE spokesperson Clare Loxley. IntelBroker announced the sale of the allegedly stolen information on Breach Forums, a cybercrime and data breach forum, and also shared details with Hackread.com. The stolen data included source code, private GitHub repositories, Docker builds, certificates, product source code belonging to Zerto and iLO, and user data such as old personal information related to deliveries.

name: Pro-Russian Hacking Group NoName057(16) Launched DDoS Attacks on Local Government and Bank Websites in Switzerland on 20 January 2025
description:
On 20 January 2025, a Russian cyber attack, claimed by the Russian group of hackers NoName057(16), affected several websites of local governments and banks in Switzerland, including the governments of three localities in Lucerne, namely Adligenswil, Kriens and Ebikon, and the cantonal banks of Zurich and Vaud, using Distributed Denial DDoS attacks. The attacks were anticipated by the Federal Office for Cyber Security due to the ongoing World Economic Forum in Davos. The Federal Office for Cyber Security reported that the hackers wanted to attract attention with this virtual demonstration online. The attacks were reported by multiple sources, including Bluewin.ch, KeystoneATS, and ATS, on Tuesday, 21 January 2025. 

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: North Korean State-Sponsored Lazarus Group Compromised Web3 and Virtual Currency Developers Globally Beginning on 9 January 2025
description:
North Korean state-sponsored hacking group Lazarus Group compromised Web3 and virtual currency developers in numerous countries beginning on 9 January 2025 at the latest. Unveiled the same day by the SecurityScorecard STRIKE team and further detailed in a 15 January report, the campaign involved fake recruiters on platforms such as LinkedIn who tricked developers into cloning malicious GitLab repositories. These repositories infected systems with modular malware capable of keylogging, clipboard monitoring, file exfiltration, and browser credential theft.
Victims were located in countries including Canada, the United States, Mexico, Brazil, Argentina, Portugal, Spain, France, the United Kingdom, Netherlands, Germany, Switzerland, Finland, Estonia, Slovakia, Ukraine, Egypt, the United Arab Emirates, Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, Malaysia, Indonesia, Brunei, and South Korea with the highest number of victims in Italy.
The campaign built on previous Lazarus operations but featured enhanced obfuscation, persistence, and modularity.

name: Lynx ransomware group stole data from Italian retailer Conad in January 2025
description: On 14 January 2025, the Lynx group carried out a ransomware attack on Conad, an Italian consortium of independent retail enterprises. The attackers exfiltrated documents, including human resources and customer information, and threatened to make them public if Conad did not pay a ransom within 72 hours. The attack did not impact the operation of Conad's website and telematic services. The Lynx group published some documents as proof of the attack, including digitized versions of paper documents, specifications, and correspondence with customers and suppliers. Conad has not issued an official statement on the matter.

name: Unknown threat actors launched a ransomware attack on the Slovakian Geodesy, Cartography and Cadastre Office in Slovakia on 7 January 2025
description: On January 7, 2025, the Slovakian Geodesy, Cartography and Cadastre Office (UGKK) was targeted by a ransomware attack, that shut down the agency's systems and closed its physical offices, with the attackers demanding millions of euros in ransom. The attack was reported by local media and confirmed by Agriculture Minister Richard Takac in a press conference on Friday, 10 January 2025, who assured that the systems would be restored with backups and that there is no risk of changes or fraudulent transcriptions of ownership data. Takac stated that this was the biggest cyberattack in the country's history and that there were "strong indications" that it originated from Ukraine, without providing any evidence or further details. Slovakia and Ukraine have been in a geopolitical dispute about Kyiv`s suspension of Russian gas transit through Slovakia at the time of the incident.

name: Unknown threat actors launched a DDoS attack on the Swiss federal government websites on 10 January 2025
description: On Friday, 10 January 2025, the Swiss federal government was targeted by a distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) cyber attack, which caused numerous federal websites to be unavailable for approximately 45 minutes. The attack, which was confirmed by the Federal IT Bureau (BIT), affected various websites, phone services, and Outlook, as well as other specialized applications. The BIT and the Federal Office for Cyber Security (BACS) took countermeasures to stabilize the situation and are currently further investigating the incident. According to the BIT, no data was compromised during the attack.

name: Pro-Russian threat actor NoName057(16) carried out DDoS attacks on Italian public institutions and companies in Italy on 11 and 12 January 2025
description:
On 11 January 2025, the pro-Russian cyber criminal group NoName057(16) carried out a series of cyber attacks on Italian public institutions and companies. The attack targeted the websites of the ministries of Foreign Affairs, Infrastructure, and Transport, as well as the Carabinieri, Navy, and Air Force. Local public transport companies, including Atac of Rome, Amat of Palermo, and Amt of Genoa, were also affected. The group used DDoS attacks, causing temporary slowdowns in services. The following day, similar DDoS attacks were registered against various Italian companies, including the banks of Intesa Sanpaolo and Monte Paschi di Siena as well as ports of Taranto and Trieste.
The attacks were announced by NoName057(16) on their Telegram channel and were linked to the visit of Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to Italy, who arrived in Rome two days prior. Most of the attacks could be mitigated within hours the same day with support of the Italian Cybersecurity Agency ACN.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Unknown threat actors breached Dutch Eindhoven University of Technology on 11 January 2025
description: Eindhoven University of Technology was targeted by an unknown attacker, who breached the university's computer network on 11 January 2025 at 9 pm, prompting the university to shut down its network and suspend classes until at least 13 January 2025. The exact circumstances are not clear yet and subject to an ongoing internal investigation.

name: Threat actors DNA, Grep, Pryx, and Rey breached internal ticketing system of Spanish telecommunications operator Telefónica by using compromised employee credentials on 9 January 2025
description: Telefónica, a Spanish multinational telecommunications company, was breached by four attackers using the aliases DNA, Grep, Pryx, and Rey, who compromised an employee's credentials to access the company's internal ticketing system on 9 January 2025, and scraped approximately 2.3 GB of documents, tickets, and various data. The attackers then leaked the data on a hacking forum. The breach was reported by BleepingComputer on 10 January 2025, after being informed by Telefónica, who confirmed the incident and stated that they were investigating and had taken steps to block unauthorized access. BleepingComputer further reported that three of the four initiators are also members of recently formed ransomware outfit Hellcat.

name: Silent Crow breached Russian cadastre Rosreestr by hacking into its database and releasing sensitive information of Russian citizens on 9 January 2025
description: On January 9, 2025, a group of hackers known as Silent Crow claimed responsibility for breaching Rosreestr, a Russian government agency responsible for managing property and land records. The hackers released a portion of a database containing personal information of Russian citizens, including names, dates of birth, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses, and individual insurance account numbers. Rosreestr denied the breach, but Russian investigative journalists from Agentstvo news outlet confirmed the legitimacy of the leaked data. The motivation behind the attack is unclear, but it is possible that Silent Crow is a pseudonym for a more well-known hacker group. The hackers warned of another attack and their Telegram channel was blocked shortly after the announcement. The incident was reported by Russian investigative journalists, including Andrey Soshnikov, editor-in-chief of Sistema, who noted that the scope of the leaked data could be extensive. The incident comes just week after an attack on similar databases in Ukraine attributed to Russian attackers.

name: Hacktivist group Ukrainian Cyber Alliance breached Russian ISP Nodex, stole sensitive documents and wiped systems on 7 January 2025
description: On January 9, 2025, the Ukrainian Cyber Alliance, a pro-Ukraine hacktivist group, breached Russian ISP Nodex, stealing sensitive documents and wiping systems, thereby destroying the company's connectivity. The attack was confirmed by Nodex, who speculated the involvement of Russia-linked threat actors, and by internet monitoring service NetBlocks, which reported a disruption in Nodex's connectivity. The Ukrainian Cyber Alliance published screenshots as proof of the breach, including images of the Veeam backup and Hewlett Packard Enterprise server consoles. Nodex informed the public about the attack through a message on VKontakte, stating that the network had been destroyed and was being restored from backup copies. The company later announced that it had restored its network core and was working to restore connections, but its website remained unreachable as of 9 January 2025. The attack affected fixed-line and mobile services.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Disrupted The Upper Canada District School Board Network In Canada On 5 January 2025
description: On 5 January 2025, the Upper Canada District School Board was targeted by a cyber attack, disrupting its network and preventing all schools under its jurisdiction from accessing the internet. The board contacted police and worked with trusted experts to assess the impact and restore services. The board informed the public about the attack on 6 January 2025. On 22 January, the UCDSB issued a statement confirming that personal information, including Date of birth, gender information, home address information and medical diagnosis and information about related needs for some students, had been exposed and began notifying those affected. On 20 February, UCDSB announced that the main services impacting students and families have been fully restored.

name: Unknown Threat Actors Breached Kingston Police Service In Canada On 5 January 2025
description: On 5 January 2025, the Kingston Police Service in Ontario, Canada, was affected by a cyber incident that impacted some of its IT systems, particularly non-emergency functions. The incident was first identified on Friday, 5 January 2025, and reported by Kingston police on 6 January 2025. The Ontario Provincial Police (OPP) Cybercrime Investigations Team is investigating the incident, with the assistance of third-party cybersecurity experts. Kingston police immediately implemented mitigation strategies to protect the public, staff, data, and organization. The Information and Privacy Commissioner was notified of the incident. The OPP and Kingston police stated that the ability to respond to emergency situations was not impacted.

name: The threat group called Illegal Platform hacked Manisa News Agency and Manisa Pocket TV in Turkey on January 5, 2025
description: On January 5, 2025, Manisa News Agency and Manisa Pocket TV were attacked by the 'Illegal Platform' group. The attackers used a photo of Soma Mayor Sercan Okur and posted anticapitalism statements. Mayor Okur denied any involvement, stating his name and photo were used without his consent, and announced he would follow the legal process. Manisa News Agency officials filed a criminal complaint. The 'Illegal Platform' group claimed the incident, stating they were not affiliated with other organizations like Anonymous.

name: Pro-Russian hacktivist group NoName057(16) launched DDoS attacks on multiple French city websites on December 31, 2024
description:
On December 31, 2024, coordinated cyberattacks disrupted municipal websites in several major French cities. A pro-Russian hacking group known as NoName claimed responsibility, framing the attacks as a protest against France's support for Ukraine.
The attacks initially brought down websites for Marseille, Tarbes, and the Haute-Garonne department. NoName later claimed on X  to have targeted 23 sites, including those of Nantes, Bordeaux, Nice, Montpellier, French Polynesia, and New Caledonia. While many websites were restored by the end of the day, the disruptions prompted an investigation by the Paris prosecutor's office.

From 14 to 17 July 2025, an international law enforcement operation code-named Eastwood was launched against NoName057(16). Coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, the operation involved simultaneous actions by authorities in Czechia, France, Finland, Germany, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the Netherlands, and the United States, targeting individuals and digital infrastructure linked to the pro-Russian network. Operation Eastwood allowed for the deactivation of several hundred servers supporting the group’s DDoS activities. Overall, national authorities issued seven arrest warrants, including against six Russian nationals, over the suspected involvement in NoName057(16). The investigation further facilitated the arrest of two individuals in France and Spain. Law enforcement personally notified over 1,000 supporters of the group, including 15 suspected administrators, of their legal liability related to the participation in NoName057(16)‘s criminal activities.

name: Unknown threat actors launched a DDoS attack on NTT Docomo in Japan on 2 January 2025
description: On 2 January 2025, Japan's largest mobile carrier, NTT Docomo, was targeted by a distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack. The attack temporarily disrupted some of its services, including its news website, video streaming platform, mobile payment, webmail services, and a golf enthusiasts website. These services were unavailable from early Thursday morning until late afternoon. NTT Docomo reported the incident and worked to restore full operations, with access to most services restored, although some content updates might still face delays.
